topics.title: Measures of Location
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT861 - BIOSTATISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Measures of Location

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Measures of Location?

In biostatistics, **Measures of Location** (also called **Measures of Central Tendency**) are single numerical values that are used to describe a dataset by identifying the **central position** or **typical value** around which all observations tend to cluster. They give us a "one-number summary" that represents the entire distribution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> A *measure of location* is a statistical parameter that yields a single value, situated within the range of the data, which is intended to represent the entire distribution of observations. The principal measures covered in this module are the **Mean, Median, Mode, Geometric Mean, Harmonic Mean, Quartiles, and Percentiles**.

The three most common measures of location are:

- **Mean (Arithmetic Average)** — the "balance point" of the data
- **Median** — the "middle value" when data is sorted
- **Mode** — the "most frequently occurring" value

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Class Height" Story

Imagine you walk into a classroom of **100 B.Tech students** and want to tell a friend outside the classroom: *"How tall are the students in this room?"*

- If you say the **average height is 168 cm**, you are giving the **Mean**.
- If you say **"half the class is shorter than 170 cm and half are taller"**, you are giving the **Median**.
- If you say **"the most common height is 167 cm"**, you are giving the **Mode**.

All three numbers describe the *location* of the "center" of the data, but each captures a different aspect of centrality. The **Mean** uses *every* value mathematically; the **Median** uses only the *position*; the **Mode** uses only the *frequency of repetition*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why are they called "Measures of Location"?**
> Because on a number line, the data is spread across many positions. These measures point to *one specific location* that best represents the whole spread. Just as a *pin on a map* marks a location, these measures mark the "location" of the center of data.

### 1.3 Why Do Biostatisticians Need Them?

In real-world biomedical research, a researcher cannot publish the heights of 10,000 patients in a paper. Instead, she publishes: *"The mean systolic blood pressure of the sample was 122 mmHg."* This single number is a **measure of location**. It allows:

1. **Comparison** between groups (e.g., Treatment A vs. Treatment B).
2. **Summarization** of huge datasets into one interpretable figure.
3. **Decision-making** in clinical thresholds (e.g., "BP above 140 is hypertension").

### 1.4 Types of Measures of Location — At a Glance

| Family | Members | Best For |
|---|---|---|
| **Mathematical Averages** | Arithmetic Mean, Geometric Mean, Harmonic Mean | Symmetric, continuous data |
| **Positional Averages** | Median, Quartiles, Percentiles | Skewed or ordinal data |
| **Frequency-based** | Mode | Categorical / nominal data |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:** The choice of measure of location depends on the **scale of measurement** (nominal, ordinal, interval, ratio) and the **shape of the distribution** (symmetric vs. skewed). This is a frequently tested concept in KTU university exams.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Arithmetic Mean (AM) — The Mathematical Average

The arithmetic mean of a set of $n$ observations $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$ is the sum of all values divided by the number of observations.

### Properties of Arithmetic Mean (KTU High-Yield)

1. **Algebric Property:** The sum of deviations of individual values from the mean is *always zero*. That is,
$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \bar{x}) = 0$$
2. **The mean minimizes the sum of squared deviations.** No other number gives a smaller value of $\sum (x_i - a)^2$.
3. **Effect of Change of Origin and Scale:** If $y_i = a + bx_i$, then $\bar{y} = a + b\bar{x}$.
4. **Effect of a Constant:** If a constant $c$ is added to (or subtracted from) every observation, the mean also changes by that constant.
5. **Use in Biostatistics:** Best suited for *interval* and *ratio* scale data with no extreme outliers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Limitation:** The mean is **highly sensitive to outliers**. In medical data, a single extremely high cholesterol reading can pull the mean upward, misrepresenting the typical patient. This is the main reason we also need the **median** and **trimmed mean**.

## 2.2 Geometric Mean (GM) — For Multiplicative Data

The geometric mean is the $n^{th}$ root of the product of $n$ observations. It is used when data are:
- **Ratios, rates, or percentages**
- **Log-normally distributed** (common in biology: bacterial counts, antibody titers, viral loads)

### Properties of Geometric Mean
- $GM \leq AM$ always (with equality only when all values are equal).
- $GM$ is **never greater than** the arithmetic mean.
- Useful when successive observations are dependent multiplicatively (e.g., growth rates over years).

## 2.3 Harmonic Mean (HM) — For Rate Data

The harmonic mean is the reciprocal of the arithmetic mean of the reciprocals. It is appropriate for:
- **Averaging rates** (e.g., average speed when equal distances are covered at different speeds)
- **Resistance in parallel circuits** (physics analogy)

### Key Relationship (Memorize for KTU!)

$$HM \leq GM \leq AM$$

with equality only when all observations are equal.

## 2.4 Median — The Middle Value

The **median** is the middle value of a sorted dataset.
- If $n$ is **odd**, the median is the $\left(\frac{n+1}{2}\right)^{th}$ observation.
- If $n$ is **even**, the median is the average of the $\left(\frac{n}{2}\right)^{th}$ and $\left(\frac{n}{2}+1\right)^{th}$ observations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why median is robust:** Only the *position* of values matters, not their magnitudes. So a single outlier (e.g., a patient with BP = 250) does not move the median much. This makes the median the **preferred measure of location for skewed medical data** like income, survival times, or antibody titers.

## 2.5 Mode — The Most Frequent Value

The **mode** is the value that occurs most frequently in the dataset. A dataset can be:
- **Unimodal** — one mode
- **Bimodal** — two modes
- **Multimodal** — many modes
- **Amodal** — no mode (all values occur with equal frequency)

The mode is the **only measure of location suitable for nominal (categorical) data** such as blood group (A, B, AB, O).

## 2.6 Quartiles, Deciles, and Percentiles

These are **positional measures** that divide an ordered dataset into equal parts.

| Measure | Symbol | Divides Data Into |
|---|---|---|
| **Quartile** | $Q_1, Q_2, Q_3$ | 4 equal parts (25% each) |
| **Decile** | $D_1, D_2, \ldots, D_9$ | 10 equal parts (10% each) |
| **Percentile** | $P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_{99}$ | 100 equal parts (1% each) |

Note: $Q_2$ = Median = $D_5$ = $P_{50}$.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Measure | Formula (Ungrouped) | Formula (Grouped) | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Arithmetic Mean** | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i}{n}$ | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} f_i x_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{k} f_i}$ | Symmetric, no outliers |
| 2 | **Geometric Mean** | $GM = \left( \prod_{i=1}^{n} x_i \right)^{1/n}$ | $GM = \left( \prod_{i=1}^{k} x_i^{f_i} \right)^{1/N}$ | Ratios, log-normal data |
| 3 | **Harmonic Mean** | $HM = \dfrac{n}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (1/x_i)}$ | $HM = \dfrac{\sum f_i}{\sum (f_i / x_i)}$ | Rates, speeds |
| 4 | **Median (Ungrouped)** | Middle value after sorting | — | Skewed, ordinal data |
| 5 | **Median (Grouped)** | — | $\tilde{x} = L + \left( \dfrac{N/2 - F}{f} \right) \cdot h$ | Grouped continuous data |
| 6 | **Mode (Ungrouped)** | Most frequent value | — | Categorical data |
| 7 | **Mode (Grouped)** | — | $\hat{x} = L + \left( \dfrac{d_1}{d_1 + d_2} \right) \cdot h$ | Grouped continuous data |
| 8 | **Quartile $Q_k$** | Position $= \dfrac{k(n+1)}{4}$ | $Q_k = L + \left( \dfrac{kN/4 - F}{f} \right) \cdot h$ | Spread analysis |
| 9 | **Percentile $P_k$** | Position $= \dfrac{k(n+1)}{100}$ | $P_k = L + \left( \dfrac{kN/100 - F}{f} \right) \cdot h$ | Reference ranges |
| 10 | **Empirical Relation** | $Mode \approx 3 \cdot Median - 2 \cdot Mean$ | Same | Quick check for skewness |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Symbols used in grouped formulas:**
> - $L$ = lower class boundary of the median/mode class
> - $N$ = total frequency
> - $F$ = cumulative frequency *before* the median class
> - $f$ = frequency of the median class
> - $h$ = class width
> - $d_1$ = difference between modal class frequency and *preceding* class frequency
> - $d_2$ = difference between modal class frequency and *succeeding* class frequency

## 2.8 Engineering / Medical Utility

- **Clinical Trials:** Mean and median of blood pressure, cholesterol, glucose levels across treatment arms.
- **Pharma Manufacturing:** Geometric mean of drug concentration dilutions.
- **Public Health:** Median household income for skewed economic data.
- **Quality Control:** Mean and mode of defect sizes in medical device production.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why the Arithmetic Mean Minimizes Squared Deviations

Let the arithmetic mean of $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$ be denoted as $\bar{x}$. We want to prove that the value $a = \bar{x}$ minimizes the function

$$S(a) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - a)^2$$

### Step 1: Differentiate $S(a)$ with respect to $a$

$$\frac{dS}{da} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} 2(x_i - a)(-1) = -2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - a)$$

### Step 2: Set the derivative to zero (first-order condition for minimum)

$$-2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - a) = 0$$

### Step 3: Solve for $a$

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - a) = 0 \implies \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i - na = 0 \implies a = \frac{\sum x_i}{n} = \bar{x}$$

### Step 4: Verify it is a minimum using the second derivative

$$\frac{d^2S}{da^2} = -2(-n) = 2n > 0 \quad \text{(always positive, hence minimum)}$$

**Conclusion:** The arithmetic mean $\bar{x}$ is the *unique* value that minimizes the sum of squared deviations. This is why the mean is the **best estimator** under the *least-squares criterion*.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: The Grouped Median Formula

We are given grouped data with class intervals. We assume observations are **uniformly distributed** within the median class.

Let the median class be the class containing the $\left(\frac{N}{2}\right)^{th}$ observation. Let:
- $L$ = lower class boundary of the median class
- $h$ = class width
- $F$ = cumulative frequency *just before* the median class
- $f$ = frequency of the median class

### Step 1: Number of observations needed in the median class

$$m = \frac{N}{2} - F$$

### Step 2: Since observations are uniformly spread, the fraction of the median class needed is

$$\text{fraction} = \frac{m}{f} = \frac{N/2 - F}{f}$$

### Step 3: Multiply fraction by class width to get the distance from $L$

$$\text{distance} = \left( \frac{N/2 - F}{f} \right) \cdot h$$

### Step 4: Add this distance to the lower boundary

$$\text{Median} = L + \left( \frac{N/2 - F}{f} \right) \cdot h$$

This completes the derivation.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: The Grouped Mode Formula (King's Formula)

The mode is assumed to lie *within* the modal class (the class with highest frequency). Let:
- $L$ = lower boundary of the modal class
- $h$ = width of the modal class
- $d_1 = f - f_1$ (excess of modal class frequency over the *preceding* class)
- $d_2 = f - f_2$ (excess of modal class frequency over the *succeeding* class)

### Step 1: Proportion of the way through the modal class

We assume the mode lies in the modal class at a point where the influence of the preceding and succeeding classes is balanced. This proportion is:

$$p = \frac{d_1}{d_1 + d_2}$$

### Step 2: Distance into the modal class from the lower boundary

$$\text{distance} = p \cdot h = \left( \frac{d_1}{d_1 + d_2} \right) \cdot h$$

### Step 3: Final mode formula

$$\text{Mode} = L + \left( \frac{d_1}{d_1 + d_2} \right) \cdot h$$

This is the standard **King's formula** used universally in KTU grouped data problems.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example — End-to-End

**Problem:** The following data represent the serum cholesterol levels (mg/dL) of 60 patients in a clinical trial. Compute the mean, median, and mode.

| Cholesterol (mg/dL) | Frequency ($f_i$) | Midpoint ($x_i$) | $f_i x_i$ | Cumulative Freq |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 120 – 140 | 5 | 130 | 650 | 5 |
| 140 – 160 | 12 | 150 | 1800 | 17 |
| 160 – 180 | 18 | 170 | 3060 | 35 |
| 180 – 200 | 15 | 190 | 2850 | 50 |
| 200 – 220 | 7 | 210 | 1470 | 57 |
| 220 – 240 | 3 | 230 | 690 | 60 |
| **Total** | **60** | — | **10520** | — |

### Step A: Compute the Arithmetic Mean

$$\bar{x} = \frac{\sum f_i x_i}{\sum f_i} = \frac{10520}{60} = 175.33 \text{ mg/dL}$$

### Step B: Compute the Median

- $N/2 = 30$
- The class with cumulative frequency just crossing 30 is **160 – 180**.
- $L = 160$, $h = 20$, $F = 17$ (cumulative freq of previous class), $f = 18$ (freq of median class).

$$\text{Median} = 160 + \left( \frac{30 - 17}{18} \right) \cdot 20 = 160 + \left( \frac{13}{18} \right) \cdot 20 = 160 + 14.44 = 174.44 \text{ mg/dL}$$

### Step C: Compute the Mode

- The modal class is **160 – 180** (highest frequency = 18).
- $L = 160$, $h = 20$, $d_1 = 18 - 12 = 6$, $d_2 = 18 - 15 = 3$.

$$\text{Mode} = 160 + \left( \frac{6}{6 + 3} \right) \cdot 20 = 160 + \left( \frac{6}{9} \right) \cdot 20 = 160 + 13.33 = 173.33 \text{ mg/dL}$$

### Step D: Verify Empirical Relationship

$$3 \cdot \text{Median} - 2 \cdot \text{Mean} = 3(174.44) - 2(175.33) = 523.32 - 350.66 = 172.66 \approx 173.33 \text{ (Mode)} \checkmark$$

The empirical formula holds approximately — this confirms the distribution is **nearly symmetric with slight skewness**.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example — Quartiles for Grouped Data

Continuing the same data, find $Q_1$ and $Q_3$.

### $Q_1$ (First Quartile):

- $N/4 = 15$
- The class with cumulative frequency just crossing 15 is **140 – 160**.
- $L = 140$, $h = 20$, $F = 5$, $f = 12$.

$$Q_1 = 140 + \left( \frac{15 - 5}{12} \right) \cdot 20 = 140 + \left( \frac{10}{12} \right) \cdot 20 = 140 + 16.67 = 156.67 \text{ mg/dL}$$

### $Q_3$ (Third Quartile):

- $3N/4 = 45$
- The class with cumulative frequency just crossing 45 is **180 – 200**.
- $L = 180$, $h = 20$, $F = 35$, $f = 15$.

$$Q_3 = 180 + \left( \frac{45 - 35}{15} \right) \cdot 20 = 180 + \left( \frac{10}{15} \right) \cdot 20 = 180 + 13.33 = 193.33 \text{ mg/dL}$$

**Interpretation:** 25% of patients have cholesterol below 156.67 mg/dL, and 75% have cholesterol below 193.33 mg/dL.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Taxonomy of Measures of Location

The following block diagram classifies all measures of location covered in Module 1, showing how each is mathematically and contextually related.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["MEASURES OF LOCATION"]:::root
    A --> B["MATHEMATICAL AVERAGES"]:::grp1
    A --> C["POSITIONAL AVERAGES"]:::grp2
    A --> D["FREQUENCY-BASED AVERAGES"]:::grp3

    B --> E["Arithmetic Mean AM"]:::leaf
    B --> F["Geometric Mean GM"]:::leaf
    B --> G["Harmonic Mean HM"]:::leaf

    C --> H["Median Q2"]:::leaf
    C --> I["Quartiles Q1 Q2 Q3"]:::leaf
    C --> J["Deciles D1 to D9"]:::leaf
    C --> K["Percentiles P1 to P99"]:::leaf

    D --> L["Mode"]:::leaf

    E --> M["Formula Sum over n"]:::formula
    F --> N["Formula nth root of product"]:::formula
    G --> O["Formula n over sum of reciprocals"]:::formula
    H --> P["Formula Middle value"]:::formula
    L --> Q["Formula Most frequent value"]:::formula

    classDef root fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#0b1f33,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px
    classDef grp1 fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#ffffff
    classDef grp2 fill:#ef6c00,stroke:#bf360c,color:#ffffff
    classDef grp3 fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#38006b,color:#ffffff
    classDef leaf fill:#fdd835,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000000
    classDef formula fill:#eeeeee,stroke:#9e9e9e,color:#000000,stroke-dasharray: 4 2
```

**How to read this diagram:**

- The **blue root** is the umbrella concept: *Measures of Location*.
- The three **colored branches** (green, orange, purple) are the three families: *Mathematical*, *Positional*, and *Frequency-Based* averages.
- The **yellow boxes** are the individual measures.
- The **dashed grey boxes** show the *mathematical intuition* for each measure (their defining formula concept).

## 4.2 Block Architecture — Workflow for Choosing the Right Measure of Location

When a biostatistician is given a dataset, the choice of which measure of location to use depends on the data's measurement scale and shape. The following architecture flow captures this decision process.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["STEP 1 Identify DATA TYPE"]:::step --> B{"Nominal Categorical"}:::decision
    A --> C{"Ordinal or Skewed Numerical"}:::decision
    A --> D{"Interval or Ratio Symmetric"}:::decision
    A --> E{"Ratio with Multiplicative Growth"}:::decision
    A --> F{"Rates or Speeds Equal Denominators"}:::decision

    B --> B1["Use MODE"]:::ans
    C --> C1["Use MEDIAN or Quartiles"]:::ans
    D --> D1["Use ARITHMETIC MEAN"]:::ans
    E --> E1["Use GEOMETRIC MEAN"]:::ans
    F --> F1["Use HARMONIC MEAN"]:::ans

    B1 --> Z["Report Single Value as Measure of Location"]:::final
    C1 --> Z
    D1 --> Z
    E1 --> Z
    F1 --> Z

    classDef step fill:#1565c0,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#ffffff
    classDef decision fill:#f9a825,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000000
    classDef ans fill:#43a047,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#ffffff
    classDef final fill:#37474f,stroke:#263238,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px
```

**How to read this architecture flow:**

- **Step 1** (blue) — The researcher first classifies the data.
- **Yellow diamonds** — Decision points based on data scale.
- **Green boxes** — The recommended measure of location.
- **Dark grey final node** — The output: a single value summarizing the dataset.

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Inequality Chain Between Means

A foundational KTU result is the chain of inequalities relating the three mathematical means.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["ARITHMETIC MEAN AM"]:::am -->|GM is less than or equal to AM| B["GEOMETRIC MEAN GM"]:::gm
    B -->|HM is less than or equal to GM| C["HARMONIC MEAN HM"]:::hm

    A -.->|Equality only when all x_i are equal| C

    classDef am fill:#c62828,stroke:#7f0000,color:#ffffff
    classDef gm fill:#ef6c00,stroke:#bf360c,color:#ffffff
    classDef hm fill:#283593,stroke:#1a237e,color:#ffffff
```

**Interpretation:** The arrows are unidirectional — values always move *downward* from AM to GM to HM. The dashed line indicates that equality holds in all three only when the data is constant.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Define the term "measure of central tendency." List any four measures of location and state one situation where each is preferred.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A *measure of central tendency* (or measure of location) is a single numerical value that represents the center or typical value of a dataset, summarizing the entire distribution in one figure.

Four measures of location:

1. **Arithmetic Mean** — preferred for symmetric, interval/ratio scale data without outliers (e.g., average BP of a healthy group).
2. **Median** — preferred for skewed data or ordinal data (e.g., median income, median survival time).
3. **Mode** — preferred for nominal/categorical data (e.g., most common blood group).
4. **Geometric Mean** — preferred for ratios, percentages, or log-normally distributed data (e.g., average bacterial count growth, average rate of return).

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for the list with situations — awarded ½ mark per correctly matched measure.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**State and prove the empirical relationship between mean, median, and mode for a moderately skewed distribution.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Statement:** For a moderately skewed (unimodal) distribution, the following empirical relationship holds:

$$\text{Mode} = 3 \cdot \text{Median} - 2 \cdot \text{Mean}$$

**Justification (sketch):** In a moderately skewed distribution, the median lies approximately one-third of the distance from the mean to the mode. That is,

$$\text{Median} - \text{Mean} = \frac{1}{3}(\text{Mode} - \text{Mean})$$

Multiplying both sides by 3:

$$3 \cdot \text{Median} - 3 \cdot \text{Mean} = \text{Mode} - \text{Mean}$$

Rearranging:

$$\text{Mode} = 3 \cdot \text{Median} - 2 \cdot \text{Mean} \quad \blacksquare$$

*(1 mark statement + 2 marks proof.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice Pattern

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**The following table shows the distribution of hemoglobin levels (g/dL) of 100 patients in a rural health survey. Compute the arithmetic mean, median, and mode. Also, compute $Q_1$ and $Q_3$, and verify the empirical relationship.**

| Hemoglobin (g/dL) | Number of Patients |
|---|---|
| 8 – 10 | 6 |
| 10 – 12 | 14 |
| 12 – 14 | 22 |
| 14 – 16 | 28 |
| 16 – 18 | 18 |
| 18 – 20 | 12 |
| **Total** | **100** |

#### (a) Compute the arithmetic mean, median, and mode. (7 marks)

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Set up the calculation table.

| Class | Frequency $f_i$ | Midpoint $x_i$ | $f_i x_i$ | Cumulative Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 8 – 10 | 6 | 9 | 54 | 6 |
| 10 – 12 | 14 | 11 | 154 | 20 |
| 12 – 14 | 22 | 13 | 286 | 42 |
| 14 – 16 | 28 | 15 | 420 | 70 |
| 16 – 18 | 18 | 17 | 306 | 88 |
| 18 – 20 | 12 | 19 | 228 | 100 |
| **Total** | **100** | — | **1448** | — |

**Arithmetic Mean:**

$$\bar{x} = \frac{\sum f_i x_i}{\sum f_i} = \frac{1448}{100} = 14.48 \text{ g/dL}$$

**Median:**

- $N/2 = 50$
- The median class is the one with cumulative frequency first exceeding 50, i.e., **14 – 16**.
- $L = 14$, $h = 2$, $F = 42$, $f = 28$.

$$\text{Median} = 14 + \left( \frac{50 - 42}{28} \right) \cdot 2 = 14 + \left( \frac{8}{28} \right) \cdot 2 = 14 + 0.571 = 14.57 \text{ g/dL}$$

**Mode:**

- Modal class: **14 – 16** (highest frequency = 28).
- $L = 14$, $h = 2$, $d_1 = 28 - 22 = 6$, $d_2 = 28 - 18 = 10$.

$$\text{Mode} = 14 + \left( \frac{6}{6 + 10} \right) \cdot 2 = 14 + \left( \frac{6}{16} \right) \cdot 2 = 14 + 0.75 = 14.75 \text{ g/dL}$$

#### (b) Compute $Q_1$ and $Q_3$ and verify the empirical relationship. (7 marks)

**Solution:**

**First Quartile $Q_1$:**

- $N/4 = 25$
- The class with cumulative frequency first exceeding 25 is **12 – 14**.
- $L = 12$, $h = 2$, $F = 20$, $f = 22$.

$$Q_1 = 12 + \left( \frac{25 - 20}{22} \right) \cdot 2 = 12 + \left( \frac{5}{22} \right) \cdot 2 = 12 + 0.4545 = 12.45 \text{ g/dL}$$

**Third Quartile $Q_3$:**

- $3N/4 = 75$
- The class with cumulative frequency first exceeding 75 is **16 – 18**.
- $L = 16$, $h = 2$, $F = 70$, $f = 18$.

$$Q_3 = 16 + \left( \frac{75 - 70}{18} \right) \cdot 2 = 16 + \left( \frac{5}{18} \right) \cdot 2 = 16 + 0.5556 = 16.56 \text{ g/dL}$$

**Verification of Empirical Relationship:**

$$\text{Mean} = 14.48, \quad \text{Median} = 14.57, \quad \text{Mode} = 14.75$$

$$3 \cdot \text{Median} - 2 \cdot \text{Mean} = 3(14.57) - 2(14.48) = 43.71 - 28.96 = 14.75$$

$$\text{Empirical Mode} = 14.75 \approx \text{Actual Mode} = 14.75 \quad \checkmark$$

The empirical relationship holds exactly in this case, indicating an approximately symmetric distribution.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 — Alternative Choice]

**The bacterial count (in thousands per mL) in 8 water samples from a river is given below. Compute the arithmetic mean, geometric mean, harmonic mean, and median. Comment on the relationship between them.**

$$\text{Data: } 12,\ 18,\ 24,\ 15,\ 30,\ 21,\ 27,\ 9 \quad (\text{in thousands/mL})$$

#### (a) Compute the arithmetic mean, geometric mean, and harmonic mean. Comment on the relationship. (7 marks)

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Arithmetic Mean**

$$\bar{x} = \frac{12 + 18 + 24 + 15 + 30 + 21 + 27 + 9}{8} = \frac{156}{8} = 19.5$$

**Step 2: Geometric Mean**

$$GM = (12 \times 18 \times 24 \times 15 \times 30 \times 21 \times 27 \times 9)^{1/8}$$

Compute the product step by step:
- $12 \times 18 = 216$
- $24 \times 15 = 360$
- $30 \times 21 = 630$
- $27 \times 9 = 243$

$$216 \times 360 = 77760, \quad 630 \times 243 = 153090, \quad 77760 \times 153090 = 1.19 \times 10^{10}$$

$$GM = (1.19 \times 10^{10})^{1/8} \approx 18.55$$

Alternative using logs:
$$\log GM = \frac{1}{8}(\log 12 + \log 18 + \log 24 + \log 15 + \log 30 + \log 21 + \log 27 + \log 9)$$
$$= \frac{1}{8}(1.079 + 1.255 + 1.380 + 1.176 + 1.477 + 1.322 + 1.431 + 0.954) = \frac{10.074}{8} = 1.2593$$
$$GM = 10^{1.2593} \approx 18.18$$

**Step 3: Harmonic Mean**

$$HM = \frac{8}{\frac{1}{12} + \frac{1}{18} + \frac{1}{24} + \frac{1}{15} + \frac{1}{30} + \frac{1}{21} + \frac{1}{27} + \frac{1}{9}}$$

Compute reciprocals:
$$\frac{1}{12} = 0.0833,\ \frac{1}{18} = 0.0556,\ \frac{1}{24} = 0.0417,\ \frac{1}{15} = 0.0667$$
$$\frac{1}{30} = 0.0333,\ \frac{1}{21} = 0.0476,\ \frac{1}{27} = 0.0370,\ \frac{1}{9} = 0.1111$$

$$\text{Sum} = 0.4763$$

$$HM = \frac{8}{0.4763} \approx 16.80$$

**Step 4: Comment on Relationship**

The three means satisfy the expected inequality:
$$HM \ (16.80) \ < \ GM \ (18.18) \ < \ AM \ (19.5) \quad \checkmark$$

This confirms the **chain inequality** $HM \leq GM \leq AM$, with strict inequality because the data values are not all equal. The data is **positively (right) skewed**, as the mean is greater than the median (computed in part b).

#### (b) Compute the median and comment on skewness. (7 marks)

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Sort the data in ascending order.**

$$9,\ 12,\ 15,\ 18,\ 21,\ 24,\ 27,\ 30$$

**Step 2: Since $n = 8$ (even), the median is the average of the 4th and 5th values.**

$$\text{Median} = \frac{18 + 21}{2} = 19.5$$

**Step 3: Compare measures.**

- $\text{Mean} = 19.5$
- $\text{Median} = 19.5$
- $Q_1 = \frac{15+18}{2} = 16.5$ (average of 2nd and 3rd values)
- $Q_3 = \frac{24+27}{2} = 25.5$ (average of 6th and 7th values)

**Step 4: Skewness Interpretation.**

Since $\text{Mean} = \text{Median} = 19.5$, the data is **approximately symmetric**. The slight difference between GM (18.18) and AM (19.5) suggests mild positive skewness in the underlying distribution, but at the level of mean and median, the distribution is symmetric.

This is consistent with the **Pearson's coefficient of skewness:**

$$\text{Skewness} = \frac{3(\text{Mean} - \text{Median})}{\text{SD}} = \frac{3(19.5 - 19.5)}{\text{SD}} = 0$$

The data shows a symmetric central tendency, with no strong skewness detected by the primary measures.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Wrong median class:** Many students pick the class with the *middle* cumulative frequency, not the class where cumulative frequency *first exceeds* $N/2$. This costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to use $F$ (cumulative frequency *before* the median class):** Some students use the cumulative frequency *of* the median class. The KTU key explicitly marks this wrong.
> 3. **Midpoint vs. boundary confusion:** For class 8–10, the midpoint is 9, but the lower boundary for grouped median is 8. Always read carefully.
> 4. **Geometric mean with zeros or negatives:** GM is *undefined* if any $x_i \leq 0$. KTU examiners frequently test this in trick questions.
> 5. **Empirical relation sign error:** Students often write $3 \text{Mean} - 2 \text{Median}$ instead of $3 \text{Median} - 2 \text{Mean}$. Memorize correctly.
> 6. **Units forgotten:** Always state the final value *with units* (e.g., mg/dL, thousands/mL). KTU deducts ½ mark if units are missing.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Measures of Location**

- [ ] **Arithmetic Mean** = Sum of values ÷ Number of values. Use for symmetric, no-outlier interval/ratio data.
- [ ] **Geometric Mean** = $n^{th}$ root of product. Use for ratios, percentages, log-normal data. **Never use for zero or negative values.**
- [ ] **Harmonic Mean** = $n$ ÷ Sum of reciprocals. Use for rates, speeds, parallel resistances.
- [ ] **Chain Inequality:** $HM \leq GM \leq AM$ — *always* true, equality only when all values are equal.
- [ ] **Median** = Middle value of sorted data. Robust to outliers. Preferred for skewed or ordinal data.
- [ ] **Mode** = Most frequent value. Only measure valid for nominal (categorical) data.
- [ ] **Quartiles** divide data into 4 equal parts; **Deciles** into 10; **Percentiles** into 100.
- [ ] $Q_2 = \text{Median} = D_5 = P_{50}$.
- [ ] **Grouped Median Formula:** $L + \left( \frac{N/2 - F}{f} \right) \cdot h$ — identify $L$, $F$, $f$, $h$ correctly.
- [ ] **Grouped Mode (King's) Formula:** $L + \left( \frac{d_1}{d_1 + d_2} \right) \cdot h$ — modal class has highest frequency.
- [ ] **Empirical Relation:** $\text{Mode} \approx 3 \cdot \text{Median} - 2 \cdot \text{Mean}$. Use for quick skewness check.
- [ ] **Mean is sensitive to outliers; Median is not.** This is a *very* common KTU conceptual question.
- [ ] For data with multiplicative growth (e.g., bacterial counts, viral titers, antibody levels), use **Geometric Mean**.
- [ ] Always state units with the final answer. Always show the working table for grouped data problems.
- [ ] **Choice Rule:** Nominal → Mode; Ordinal/Skewed → Median; Interval/Ratio Symmetric → Mean; Ratios → GM; Rates → HM.
- [ ] For KTU 14-mark problems, **always show three things:** (i) the working table, (ii) identification of the relevant class, (iii) substitution into the formula with units.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Arithmetic Mean
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT861 - BIOSTATISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Arithmetic Mean

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)**
> The **Arithmetic Mean (AM)** is defined as the sum of all observations in a statistical series divided by the total number of observations. It is the most widely used measure of **central tendency** in biostatistics, and represents the value around which all observations in a dataset tend to cluster. Mathematically, it is the **first moment about the origin** divided by the frequency, which classifies it as a moment-based measure of central tendency.

### Symbolic Representation

For a dataset containing $n$ observations $x_1, x_2, x_3, \ldots, x_n$, the Arithmetic Mean is universally denoted as $\bar{x}$ (sample mean) or $\mu$ (population mean), and is computed using:

$$\bar{x} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i}{n}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In the KTU 2024 scheme for PEBRT861, the Arithmetic Mean is introduced as the **foundation of descriptive biostatistics**. Mastery of its properties, derivations, and series-wise computation (ungrouped, discrete grouped, and continuous grouped) is mandatory before progressing to other measures of central tendency like Median and Mode.

### Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are a **biostatistician working in a clinical trial** measuring the systolic blood pressure (SBP) of **5 patients** enrolled in a hypertension study. The recorded values are: $120, 130, 140, 150, 160$ mmHg.

Now, suppose you wanted to **summarize this entire dataset with one single representative value** that captures the "typical" blood pressure of the group. What value would you pick?

The most logical answer is the **Arithmetic Mean**:

$$\bar{x} = \frac{120 + 130 + 140 + 150 + 160}{5} = \frac{700}{5} = 140 \text{ mmHg}$$

This single value of **140 mmHg** acts as the **"balancing point"** of the dataset. If you were to place all 5 readings on a number line, the mean is the exact point where the data would balance perfectly, just like a **seesaw pivots at its center of gravity**.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering/Biomedical Analogy:** Think of the Arithmetic Mean as the **centroid (center of mass)** of a uniform rod. Just as the centroid represents the single point where the entire weight of the rod can be considered to be concentrated, the AM represents a single point where the entire "statistical mass" of the data can be considered to be concentrated.

### Why Arithmetic Mean Matters in Biostatistics

- It is the **only measure of central tendency** that uses **every observation** in the dataset.
- It is the foundation for many advanced statistical procedures including **ANOVA, regression analysis, t-tests, and confidence interval estimation**.
- It is mathematically tractable and possesses **well-defined algebraic properties** (e.g., the sum of deviations from the mean is zero).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Types of Arithmetic Mean Based on Data Structure

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly classifies Arithmetic Mean computation into **three cases** based on data organization:

**Case 1: Individual / Ungrouped Series**
- Data is presented as a raw list of individual observations.
- Each value occurs only once (or repetitions are listed explicitly).
- Used for **small clinical samples** (e.g., BP of 10 patients).

**Case 2: Discrete Frequency Distribution**
- Data is grouped by discrete values with their corresponding frequencies.
- Each value $x_i$ has an associated frequency $f_i$.
- Used for **categorical-count data** (e.g., number of children per family in 50 households).

**Case 3: Continuous Frequency Distribution**
- Data is grouped into **class intervals** (e.g., 10–20, 20–30).
- Computation requires identifying the **midpoint (class mark)** of each class.
- Used for **large-scale epidemiological data** (e.g., hemoglobin levels of 500 patients).

### 2.2 Mathematical Properties of Arithmetic Mean

The following properties are **high-yield for KTU board examinations** and are frequently asked as 3-mark conceptual questions:

1. **Property of Zero Sum of Deviations:**
   The algebraic sum of deviations of all observations from the arithmetic mean is always **zero**.

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \bar{x}) = 0$$

2. **Property of Minimum Sum of Squares:**
   The sum of squared deviations is **minimum** when taken about the mean (compared to any other value $a$).

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \bar{x})^2 < \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - a)^2, \quad \text{where } a \neq \bar{x}$$

3. **Property of Linear Transformation:**
   If a constant $c$ is added to (or subtracted from) every observation, the mean is also increased (or decreased) by that constant.

$$\text{If } y_i = x_i + c, \text{ then } \bar{y} = \bar{x} + c$$

4. **Property of Scaling (Multiplication):**
   If every observation is multiplied by a constant $k$, the mean is also multiplied by $k$.

$$\text{If } y_i = k \cdot x_i, \text{ then } \bar{y} = k \cdot \bar{x}$$

5. **Property of Combined Mean:**
   If two groups with means $\bar{x}_1$ and $\bar{x}_2$ and sizes $n_1$ and $n_2$ are combined, the combined mean is:

$$\bar{x}_{combined} = \frac{n_1 \bar{x}_1 + n_2 \bar{x}_2}{n_1 + n_2}$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the definitive KTU 2024 reference for Arithmetic Mean formulas. Memorize these before attempting any board problem.**

| **Series Type** | **Formula** | **Variables Explained** |
|---|---|---|
| Individual / Ungrouped | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum x_i}{n}$ | $x_i$ = observation, $n$ = number of observations |
| Discrete Frequency | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum f_i x_i}{\sum f_i}$ | $f_i$ = frequency, $x_i$ = value, $\sum f_i = N$ |
| Continuous Frequency (Direct) | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum f_i m_i}{\sum f_i}$ | $m_i$ = class midpoint = (lower limit + upper limit) / 2 |
| Continuous Frequency (Step Deviation) | $\bar{x} = A + \left(\dfrac{\sum f_i d_i}{\sum f_i}\right) \cdot h$ | $A$ = assumed mean, $d_i = \dfrac{m_i - A}{h}$, $h$ = class width |
| Weighted Mean | $\bar{x}_w = \dfrac{\sum w_i x_i}{\sum w_i}$ | $w_i$ = weight assigned to $x_i$ |
| Combined Mean | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{n_1 \bar{x}_1 + n_2 \bar{x}_2}{n_1 + n_2}$ | $n_1, n_2$ = sizes of two groups |

> [!TIP]
> **Why Use the Step-Deviation Method?**
> The step-deviation method is computationally efficient for continuous distributions with large midpoint values. By choosing an appropriate assumed mean $A$ and dividing by class width $h$, the resulting $d_i$ values become small integers, drastically reducing arithmetic effort. This method is a **favorite of KTU board examiners** for testing computational skill.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Biostatistics

- **Clinical Trials:** AM is used to report the average reduction in tumor size after a drug intervention.
- **Epidemiology:** Mean incubation period of a disease across a population.
- **Public Health:** Average BMI, mean hemoglobin concentration, mean cholesterol level in cohort studies.
- **Quality Control in Pharma:** Mean drug content uniformity in manufactured tablets.
- **Nutritional Studies:** Average caloric intake of a defined population group.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Step-Deviation Method

The step-deviation method is the **most derivation-intensive topic** in the Arithmetic Mean module and is regularly tested in KTU 14-mark problems. We derive it from first principles below.

**Starting Point:** The standard formula for the arithmetic mean of a continuous frequency distribution is:

$$\bar{x} = \frac{\sum f_i m_i}{\sum f_i}$$

**Step 1: Define the assumed mean $A$.** 
Let $A$ be an arbitrary assumed mean, typically chosen as the midpoint of the central (modal) class or any class with high frequency.

**Step 2: Express each midpoint in terms of deviation from $A$.**
Define the deviation $d_i$ (before scaling) as:

$$d_i^{raw} = m_i - A$$

**Step 3: Introduce the class width $h$ as a scaling factor.**
The common difference between consecutive class midpoints in a continuous series with equal class width is exactly $h$ (the class width). Therefore, all raw deviations are integer multiples of $h$. Define:

$$d_i = \frac{m_i - A}{h}$$

This transformation yields **small integer values** for $d_i$, simplifying arithmetic.

**Step 4: Solve for $m_i$.**
From the definition of $d_i$:

$$d_i = \frac{m_i - A}{h} \implies m_i - A = d_i \cdot h \implies m_i = A + d_i \cdot h$$

**Step 5: Substitute into the mean formula.**

$$\bar{x} = \frac{\sum f_i m_i}{\sum f_i} = \frac{\sum f_i (A + d_i \cdot h)}{\sum f_i}$$

**Step 6: Expand the numerator using linearity of summation.**

$$\bar{x} = \frac{\sum f_i A + \sum f_i d_i h}{\sum f_i} = \frac{A \sum f_i + h \sum f_i d_i}{\sum f_i}$$

**Step 7: Apply the constant multiplication rule for $A$.**

$$\bar{x} = \frac{A \cdot \sum f_i}{\sum f_i} + \frac{h \cdot \sum f_i d_i}{\sum f_i}$$

**Step 8: Simplify the first term (since $A$ is a constant).**

$$\bar{x} = A + \frac{h \cdot \sum f_i d_i}{\sum f_i}$$

This is the **final step-deviation formula**, and it is mathematically equivalent to the direct method but vastly simpler to compute.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (Modeled on KTU University Exam Pattern):**
> The following data represents the hemoglobin levels (in g/dL) of 60 patients enrolled in an anemia study. Calculate the arithmetic mean using the **step-deviation method**.

| **Hemoglobin (g/dL)** | **Number of Patients ($f_i$)** |
|---|---|
| 10 – 12 | 5 |
| 12 – 14 | 10 |
| 14 – 16 | 15 |
| 16 – 18 | 20 |
| 18 – 20 | 8 |
| 20 – 22 | 2 |

**Step 1: Determine the class width $h$.**
The difference between consecutive lower limits is $12 - 10 = 2$. Therefore:

$$h = 2$$

**Step 2: Select the assumed mean $A$.**
The class with the highest frequency (20–18 contains the modal class observation 18) is the most efficient choice. We take the midpoint of the class with the highest frequency:

$$A = \frac{16 + 18}{2} = 17$$

**Step 3: Compute the midpoints $m_i$ for each class.**

| **Class** | **Midpoint $m_i$** |
|---|---|
| 10 – 12 | 11 |
| 12 – 14 | 13 |
| 14 – 16 | 15 |
| 16 – 18 | 17 |
| 18 – 20 | 19 |
| 20 – 22 | 21 |

**Step 4: Compute the step-deviations $d_i = \dfrac{m_i - A}{h}$.**

| **Class** | **Midpoint $m_i$** | **Calculation** | **$d_i$** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 10 – 12 | 11 | $(11 - 17)/2$ | $-3$ |
| 12 – 14 | 13 | $(13 - 17)/2$ | $-2$ |
| 14 – 16 | 15 | $(15 - 17)/2$ | $-1$ |
| 16 – 18 | 17 | $(17 - 17)/2$ | $0$ |
| 18 – 20 | 19 | $(19 - 17)/2$ | $1$ |
| 20 – 22 | 21 | $(21 - 17)/2$ | $2$ |

**Step 5: Compute $f_i \cdot d_i$ and $\sum f_i d_i$.**

| **Class** | **$f_i$** | **$d_i$** | **$f_i d_i$** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 10 – 12 | 5 | $-3$ | $-15$ |
| 12 – 14 | 10 | $-2$ | $-20$ |
| 14 – 16 | 15 | $-1$ | $-15$ |
| 16 – 18 | 20 | $0$ | $0$ |
| 18 – 20 | 8 | $1$ | $8$ |
| 20 – 22 | 2 | $2$ | $4$ |
| **Total** | $\sum f_i = 60$ | — | $\sum f_i d_i = -38$ |

**Step 6: Apply the step-deviation formula.**

$$\bar{x} = A + \left( \frac{\sum f_i d_i}{\sum f_i} \right) \cdot h$$

**Step 7: Substitute the numerical values.**

$$\bar{x} = 17 + \left( \frac{-38}{60} \right) \cdot 2$$

**Step 8: Compute the fractional part.**

$$\frac{-38}{60} = -\frac{19}{30} \approx -0.6333$$

**Step 9: Multiply by $h$.**

$$-0.6333 \times 2 = -1.2667$$

**Step 10: Finalize the mean.**

$$\bar{x} = 17 + (-1.2667) = 15.7333 \text{ g/dL}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{\bar{x} \approx 15.73 \text{ g/dL}}$$

### 3.3 Exhaustive Worked Example: Combined Mean

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** In a clinical study, Group A (size $n_1 = 40$) has a mean systolic BP of $\bar{x}_1 = 135$ mmHg, and Group B (size $n_2 = 60$) has a mean systolic BP of $\bar{x}_2 = 142$ mmHg. Find the combined mean.

**Step 1: Identify the inputs.**
- $n_1 = 40$, $\bar{x}_1 = 135$
- $n_2 = 60$, $\bar{x}_2 = 142$

**Step 2: Compute $\sum x$ for each group.**

$$\sum x_1 = n_1 \cdot \bar{x}_1 = 40 \times 135 = 5400$$

$$\sum x_2 = n_2 \cdot \bar{x}_2 = 60 \times 142 = 8520$$

**Step 3: Apply the combined mean formula.**

$$\bar{x}_{combined} = \frac{n_1 \bar{x}_1 + n_2 \bar{x}_2}{n_1 + n_2} = \frac{5400 + 8520}{40 + 60}$$

**Step 4: Simplify.**

$$\bar{x}_{combined} = \frac{13920}{100} = 139.2 \text{ mmHg}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{\bar{x}_{combined} = 139.2 \text{ mmHg}}$$

### 3.4 Exhaustive Worked Example: Weighted Mean

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A pharmacy student scored the following grades. Calculate the weighted arithmetic mean. Weights represent the credit hours of each course.

| **Course** | **Grade Point ($x_i$)** | **Credit ($w_i$)** |
|---|---|---|
| Biostatistics | 9 | 4 |
| Biochemistry | 8 | 3 |
| Anatomy | 7 | 5 |
| Physiology | 9 | 2 |

**Step 1: Compute $w_i x_i$.**

| **Course** | **$x_i$** | **$w_i$** | **$w_i x_i$** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Biostatistics | 9 | 4 | 36 |
| Biochemistry | 8 | 3 | 24 |
| Anatomy | 7 | 5 | 35 |
| Physiology | 9 | 2 | 18 |
| **Total** | — | $\sum w_i = 14$ | $\sum w_i x_i = 113$ |

**Step 2: Apply the formula.**

$$\bar{x}_w = \frac{\sum w_i x_i}{\sum w_i} = \frac{113}{14} \approx 8.07$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{\bar{x}_w \approx 8.07 \text{ grade points}}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Flowchart: Classification of Arithmetic Mean

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the conceptual taxonomy of Arithmetic Mean as per the KTU 2024 syllabus, showing how the choice of method depends on the data structure.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["Arithmetic Mean Computation"]
    nodeB["Identify Data Structure"]
    nodeC["Individual / Ungrouped Series"]
    nodeD["Discrete Frequency Distribution"]
    nodeE["Continuous Frequency Distribution"]
    nodeF["Formula: xbar = sum_x divided by n"]
    nodeG["Formula: xbar = sum_fx divided by sum_f"]
    nodeH["Step-Deviation Method"]
    nodeI["Direct Method using Midpoints"]
    nodeJ["Formula: xbar = A plus h times sum_fd divided by sum_f"]
    nodeK["Formula: xbar = sum_fm divided by sum_f"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeB --> nodeD
    nodeB --> nodeE
    nodeC --> nodeF
    nodeD --> nodeG
    nodeE --> nodeH
    nodeE --> nodeI
    nodeH --> nodeJ
    nodeI --> nodeK
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Step-Deviation Computational Pipeline

This diagram illustrates the sequential data-processing pipeline for the step-deviation method, mirroring the algorithmic flow used in exam problems.

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeIn["Input: Continuous Frequency Table"]
    nodeA1["Step 1: Identify class width h"]
    nodeA2["Step 2: Choose assumed mean A"]
    nodeA3["Step 3: Compute midpoints m_i"]
    nodeA4["Step 4: Compute d_i = m_i minus A divided by h"]
    nodeA5["Step 5: Multiply f_i times d_i"]
    nodeA6["Step 6: Sum f_i and sum f_i d_i"]
    nodeA7["Step 7: Apply formula xbar = A plus h times sum_fd divided by sum_f"]
    nodeOut["Output: Arithmetic Mean xbar"]

    nodeIn --> nodeA1
    nodeA1 --> nodeA2
    nodeA2 --> nodeA3
    nodeA3 --> nodeA4
    nodeA4 --> nodeA5
    nodeA5 --> nodeA6
    nodeA6 --> nodeA7
    nodeA7 --> nodeOut
```

### 4.3 Property Map: Algebraic Properties of Arithmetic Mean

This diagram maps the **five key properties** of the arithmetic mean in a hierarchical taxonomy, useful for viva and short-answer questions.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeProp["Five Key Properties of AM"]
    nodeP1["Property 1: Sum of Deviations is Zero"]
    nodeP2["Property 2: Minimum Sum of Squared Deviations"]
    nodeP3["Property 3: Effect of Adding Constant c"]
    nodeP4["Property 4: Effect of Multiplying by Constant k"]
    nodeP5["Property 5: Combined Mean Formula"]
    nodeF1["Equation: sum x_i minus xbar equals 0"]
    nodeF2["Equation: sum x_i minus xbar squared is minimum"]
    nodeF3["Equation: xbar of x plus c equals xbar plus c"]
    nodeF4["Equation: xbar of kx equals k times xbar"]
    nodeF5["Equation: xbar_combined equals n1xbar1 plus n2xbar2 over n1 plus n2"]

    nodeProp --> nodeP1
    nodeProp --> nodeP2
    nodeProp --> nodeP3
    nodeProp --> nodeP4
    nodeProp --> nodeP5
    nodeP1 --> nodeF1
    nodeP2 --> nodeF2
    nodeP3 --> nodeF3
    nodeP4 --> nodeF4
    nodeP5 --> nodeF5
```

### 4.4 Diagram: Centroid Analogy (Intuition Map)

This block diagram maps the real-world centroid analogy to formal statistical terms, reinforcing intuition for students new to the topic.

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodePhys["Physics Analogy: Centroid of a Rod"]
    nodeStat["Statistical Concept: Arithmetic Mean"]
    nodeMap1["Position of balance point on a uniform rod"]
    nodeMap2["Position of statistical balance in the dataset"]
    nodeMap3["Single point representing total mass"]
    nodeMap4["Single value representing total data"]
    nodeMap5["Computed using moment of mass about a reference axis"]
    nodeMap6["Computed using first moment of observations about origin"]

    nodePhys --> nodeMap1
    nodeStat --> nodeMap2
    nodeMap1 --> nodeMap3
    nodeMap2 --> nodeMap4
    nodeMap3 --> nodeMap5
    nodeMap4 --> nodeMap6
    nodeMap5 --> nodeMap6
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
> Define the **Arithmetic Mean**. State any **two properties** of the arithmetic mean with suitable mathematical expressions.

**Model Answer:**

The **Arithmetic Mean (AM)** of a set of $n$ observations $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$ is defined as the sum of all observations divided by the number of observations:

$$\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$$

It is the most fundamental measure of central tendency and represents the value around which the observations are distributed.

**Property 1: Sum of Deviations from Mean is Zero.**
The algebraic sum of deviations of all observations from the arithmetic mean is always equal to zero.

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \bar{x}) = 0$$

**Property 2: Effect of Adding a Constant.**
If a constant $c$ is added to every observation, the new mean becomes $\bar{x} + c$.

$$\text{If } y_i = x_i + c, \text{ then } \bar{y} = \bar{x} + c$$

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
> Distinguish between **Arithmetic Mean** and **Weighted Arithmetic Mean**. In which situation is the weighted mean preferred?

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | **Arithmetic Mean** | **Weighted Arithmetic Mean** |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Equal importance to all observations | Each observation is assigned a relative weight $w_i$ |
| Formula | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum x_i}{n}$ | $\bar{x}_w = \dfrac{\sum w_i x_i}{\sum w_i}$ |
| Application | Homogeneous data (all values equally important) | Heterogeneous data (some values more significant) |
| Examples | Mean BP of 10 patients | Mean GPA where courses have different credits |

The **weighted mean is preferred** when the observations in the dataset are **not of equal importance**. For example, in a B.Tech grade point average (GPA) calculation, a 4-credit course carries more weight than a 2-credit course, so the weighted mean gives a more accurate representation of overall performance.

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks – With Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]**

**(a)** Derive the **step-deviation method** for computing the arithmetic mean of a continuous frequency distribution. State the formula and clearly define each term. **[(7 Marks)]**

**(b)** The data below shows the cholesterol levels (in mg/dL) of 100 patients. Calculate the arithmetic mean using the step-deviation method. **[(7 Marks)]**

| **Cholesterol (mg/dL)** | **Number of Patients** |
|---|---|
| 100 – 120 | 8 |
| 120 – 140 | 15 |
| 140 – 160 | 25 |
| 160 – 180 | 30 |
| 180 – 200 | 14 |
| 200 – 220 | 8 |

**Model Solution to (a):**

The derivation proceeds as follows (already exhaustively shown in Section 3.1). The final formula is:

$$\bar{x} = A + \left( \frac{\sum f_i d_i}{\sum f_i} \right) \cdot h$$

Where:
- $A$ = assumed mean
- $h$ = class width
- $d_i = \dfrac{m_i - A}{h}$ = step deviation for class $i$
- $m_i$ = midpoint of class $i$
- $f_i$ = frequency of class $i$

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1: Compute $h$.**
$$h = 120 - 100 = 20$$

**Step 2: Choose assumed mean $A$.** 
The class 160 – 180 has the highest frequency (30), so we choose its midpoint:
$$A = \frac{160 + 180}{2} = 170$$

**Step 3: Compute $m_i$, $d_i$, and $f_i d_i$.**

| **Class** | **$f_i$** | **$m_i$** | **$d_i = (m_i - 170)/20$** | **$f_i d_i$** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 100 – 120 | 8 | 110 | $-3$ | $-24$ |
| 120 – 140 | 15 | 130 | $-2$ | $-30$ |
| 140 – 160 | 25 | 150 | $-1$ | $-25$ |
| 160 – 180 | 30 | 170 | $0$ | $0$ |
| 180 – 200 | 14 | 190 | $1$ | $14$ |
| 200 – 220 | 8 | 210 | $2$ | $16$ |
| **Total** | $\sum f_i = 100$ | — | — | $\sum f_i d_i = -49$ |

**Step 4: Apply the formula.**

$$\bar{x} = 170 + \left( \frac{-49}{100} \right) \cdot 20 = 170 + (-0.49)(20) = 170 - 9.8$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{\bar{x} = 160.2 \text{ mg/dL}}$$

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the **concept of combined mean** with a suitable formula. In a biostatistics study, two groups of patients have the following data: Group I: $n_1 = 50$, $\bar{x}_1 = 72$ bpm (resting heart rate). Group II: $n_2 = 70$, $\bar{x}_2 = 78$ bpm. Find the combined mean. **[(7 Marks)]**

**(b)** Define the **weighted arithmetic mean**. A pharmacology student obtained the following marks in 4 subjects. Calculate the weighted mean, given the weights in parentheses. **[(7 Marks)]**

| **Subject** | **Marks ($x_i$)** | **Weight ($w_i$)** |
|---|---|---|
| Biostatistics | 85 | 4 |
| Biochemistry | 78 | 3 |
| Anatomy | 92 | 5 |
| Physiology | 74 | 2 |

**Model Solution to (a):**

The **combined mean** is the weighted average of the means of two or more groups, weighted by their respective sample sizes. The formula is:

$$\bar{x}_{combined} = \frac{n_1 \bar{x}_1 + n_2 \bar{x}_2}{n_1 + n_2}$$

**Step 1: Identify the values.**
- $n_1 = 50$, $\bar{x}_1 = 72$
- $n_2 = 70$, $\bar{x}_2 = 78$

**Step 2: Compute $n_1 \bar{x}_1$ and $n_2 \bar{x}_2$.**
$$n_1 \bar{x}_1 = 50 \times 72 = 3600$$
$$n_2 \bar{x}_2 = 70 \times 78 = 5460$$

**Step 3: Apply the formula.**

$$\bar{x}_{combined} = \frac{3600 + 5460}{50 + 70} = \frac{9060}{120} = 75.5 \text{ bpm}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{\bar{x}_{combined} = 75.5 \text{ bpm}}$$

**Model Solution to (b):**

The **weighted arithmetic mean** is defined as:

$$\bar{x}_w = \frac{\sum w_i x_i}{\sum w_i}$$

**Step 1: Compute $w_i x_i$.**

| **Subject** | **$x_i$** | **$w_i$** | **$w_i x_i$** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Biostatistics | 85 | 4 | 340 |
| Biochemistry | 78 | 3 | 234 |
| Anatomy | 92 | 5 | 460 |
| Physiology | 74 | 2 | 148 |
| **Total** | — | $\sum w_i = 14$ | $\sum w_i x_i = 1182$ |

**Step 2: Apply the formula.**

$$\bar{x}_w = \frac{1182}{14} \approx 84.43$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{\bar{x}_w \approx 84.43 \text{ marks}}$$

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Arithmetic Mean Problems:**
> 1. **Wrong Midpoint Calculation:** For continuous classes, students often use the lower limit or upper limit as the "value" instead of the midpoint. Always compute $m_i = (\text{lower limit} + \text{upper limit})/2$. Loss: **2 marks**.
> 2. **Incorrect Assumed Mean Choice:** Choosing an assumed mean that is not the midpoint of the highest-frequency class leads to large $d_i$ values, increasing the chance of arithmetic error. Loss: **1–2 marks**.
> 3. **Forgetting to Multiply by $h$:** A very common mistake is computing $\bar{x} = A + \dfrac{\sum f_i d_i}{\sum f_i}$ without the $\cdot h$ term. This produces an answer that is dimensionally wrong. Loss: **3 marks** (entire computation invalidated).
> 4. **Sign Errors in Deviations:** When $m_i < A$, the deviation $d_i$ is **negative**. Students frequently drop the sign, leading to incorrect $\sum f_i d_i$. Loss: **2–3 marks**.
> 5. **In the Combined Mean Formula:** A common mistake is to use $\dfrac{\bar{x}_1 + \bar{x}_2}{2}$ (simple average) instead of $\dfrac{n_1 \bar{x}_1 + n_2 \bar{x}_2}{n_1 + n_2}$ (weighted average). This is only valid when $n_1 = n_2$. Loss: **3 marks**.
> 6. **Missing Units:** The final mean must be reported with **correct units** (e.g., mg/dL, mmHg, bpm). Forgetting units leads to **0.5 to 1 mark** deduction in strict valuation.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Arithmetic Mean (Module 1)**

- **Definition:** AM is the sum of observations divided by the number of observations. It is the **first moment about the origin** divided by frequency.
- **Three Series Types:** (1) Individual — use $\bar{x} = \sum x / n$; (2) Discrete — use $\bar{x} = \sum fx / \sum f$; (3) Continuous — use $\bar{x} = \sum fm / \sum f$.
- **Step-Deviation Master Formula:** $\bar{x} = A + \left(\dfrac{\sum f_i d_i}{\sum f_i}\right) \cdot h$ where $d_i = (m_i - A)/h$.
- **Assumed Mean Selection:** Always pick the midpoint of the class with **highest frequency** (the modal class).
- **Class Width $h$:** The numerical difference between consecutive lower (or upper) class limits.
- **Combined Mean Formula:** $\bar{x} = \dfrac{n_1 \bar{x}_1 + n_2 \bar{x}_2}{n_1 + n_2}$ — **never** use the simple arithmetic mean of two group means.
- **Weighted Mean:** Use when observations have **unequal importance**; formula is $\bar{x}_w = \sum w_i x_i / \sum w_i$.
- **Property 1 (Zero Sum):** $\sum (x_i - \bar{x}) = 0$ — this is a **definitive check** for the correctness of any computed mean.
- **Property 2 (Minimum Variance):** The mean minimizes the sum of squared deviations — this is why AM is the best estimate in least-squares regression.
- **Property 3 (Translation Invariance):** Adding $c$ to all values adds $c$ to the mean.
- **Property 4 (Scaling):** Multiplying all values by $k$ multiplies the mean by $k$.
- **When NOT to use AM:** The mean is **misleading for skewed distributions** or when there are extreme outliers. In such cases, the median is preferred (covered in later modules).
- **Units Are Mandatory:** Always attach proper SI/clinical units to the final answer.
- **KTU 14-Mark Strategy:** For 7-mark sub-questions, present the formula, the computation table, and the final numerical answer with units in a structured 3-step layout.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Median
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT861 - BIOSTATISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Median

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Biostatistics** and descriptive statistics, the **Median** is formally defined as the **central positional value** of a finite, one-dimensional dataset that has been arranged in either ascending or descending order of magnitude. It is the value that partitions the entire dataset into **two equal halves**, such that exactly **50%** of the observations lie below the median and the remaining **50%** of the observations lie above the median.

Mathematically, for a sorted dataset of size $N$, the median $M$ satisfies:

$$P(X \le M) = P(X \ge M) = 0.5$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The median is classified under Module 1 of PEBRT861 (Biostatistics) as a **measure of central tendency**. Unlike the arithmetic mean, the median is a **positional average (or partition value)** — its value is determined entirely by the *position* an observation occupies in the ordered sequence, not by the *magnitude* of the observation itself.

The median belongs to the broader family of **quantiles** (also called **percentiles** or **fractiles** in biostatistical literature). Specifically, the median is the **2nd quartile ($Q_2$)**, the **50th percentile ($P_{50}$)**, and the **5th decile ($D_5$)** of a distribution.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Median Strip of a Highway"

Imagine a long highway passing through a city. The **median strip** is the central divider that splits the road into two perfectly equal halves — the left lane and the right lane. No matter how many vehicles (data points) are travelling on each side, the strip itself sits *exactly in the middle*.

The **statistical median** behaves in the exact same way:

- A dataset is like the vehicles travelling along the highway.
- The **median value** is the "median strip" — the *single central value* that splits the observations into a "lower half" and an "upper half."
- Just as a median strip remains unchanged whether the left side has 10 trucks or 10,000 trucks, the **statistical median is resistant to extreme values** (outliers). A few unusually high or low values cannot shift its position, because the median only cares about the *count* of points, not their *size*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Summary:** The median is the *middle value* of a sorted dataset. If you line up all the values from smallest to largest, the median is the one standing exactly in the middle. It is a **robust measure of central tendency** widely preferred in medical and biostatistical research because biological data (e.g., patient recovery times, blood pressure readings) often contains skewed distributions and extreme outliers.

## 1.3 Why Median Matters in Biostatistics

In the KTU 2024 scheme, students of Biostatistics are expected to recognise that:

1. **Survival analysis** uses the **median survival time** as a key endpoint.
2. **Skewed biological distributions** (e.g., incubation periods of diseases, antibody titres) are better summarised by the median than the mean.
3. **Non-parametric tests** such as the **Wilcoxon rank-sum test** and the **Mann–Whitney U-test** are constructed around the median.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The median is the **second most frequently used measure of central tendency** after the arithmetic mean, and is officially listed as a high-priority learning outcome (CO1 / CO2) in the PEBRT861 2024 syllabus.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Computational Logic — The Four Cases

The median calculation procedure differs based on the **data type**. KTU examiners frequently test all four cases. Master the logical flow below.

### Case A — Ungrouped (Raw) Data with Odd $N$

1. Sort the $N$ observations in ascending order.
2. Compute the position index $p = \dfrac{N+1}{2}$.
3. The median $M$ is the value occupying the $p^{\text{th}}$ position.

**Why this works:** Because the middle value of an odd-count ordered sequence is unique — there are exactly $\dfrac{N-1}{2}$ values below it and $\dfrac{N-1}{2}$ values above it.

### Case B — Ungrouped (Raw) Data with Even $N$

1. Sort the $N$ observations in ascending order.
2. There is no single middle observation; instead, compute the **arithmetic mean of the two central observations** located at positions $\dfrac{N}{2}$ and $\dfrac{N}{2}+1$.

$$M = \frac{1}{2}\left(x_{N/2} + x_{N/2+1}\right)$$

**Why this works:** Averaging the two middle values produces a single value that *minimises* the sum of absolute deviations, which is the defining property of the median.

### Case C — Discrete Frequency Distribution

1. Compute the **cumulative frequencies** $C_f$ for each class.
2. Compute $N = \sum f_i$ (total frequency).
3. Compute the median position $p = \dfrac{N}{2}$.
4. The median is the **value of the variable** $x$ whose cumulative frequency is **first equal to or just greater than** $p$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Distinction:** In discrete frequency distributions, the median is always one of the **observed values** (an $x_i$), NOT an interpolated value. This is the single most common point of confusion between discrete and continuous cases.

### Case D — Continuous (Grouped) Frequency Distribution

This is the most heavily tested case in KTU 2024 ESE papers. The median lies *within a class* (not at a class boundary) and must be found by **linear interpolation**.

**Step-by-step operational logic:**

1. Compute the cumulative frequencies $C_f$.
2. Find $N/2$, where $N = \sum f_i$.
3. Identify the **median class**: the first class whose cumulative frequency is $\ge N/2$.
4. Apply the interpolation formula:

$$M = L + \left(\frac{\dfrac{N}{2} - F}{f_m}\right) \times h$$

where each symbol is defined precisely in the formula sheet below.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering intuition for linear interpolation:** We assume that observations within the median class are *uniformly distributed*. Therefore, we locate the cumulative count $N/2$ within the median class by taking a *proportionate* distance from the lower boundary. This is identical in spirit to **trilinear interpolation in 2D graphics** and **piecewise-linear curve fitting** in numerical methods.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Data Type** | **Condition** | **Median Formula** | **Key Symbols** |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| Ungrouped | $N$ odd | $M = \text{value of } \dfrac{N+1}{2}^{\text{th}} \text{ item}$ | $N$ = number of observations |
| Ungrouped | $N$ even | $M = \dfrac{1}{2}\left(x_{N/2} + x_{N/2+1}\right)$ | $x$ = sorted observations |
| Discrete Frequency | Any $N$ | $M = x_k$ where $C_{f,k} \ge N/2$ first true | $C_f$ = cumulative frequency, $x_k$ = value at position $k$ |
| Continuous Frequency | Any $N$ | $M = L + \left(\dfrac{N/2 - F}{f_m}\right) h$ | $L$ = lower class boundary of median class, $F$ = $C_f$ of class preceding median class, $f_m$ = frequency of median class, $h$ = class width |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units check:** Since $L$ and $h$ carry the same unit (e.g., mg/dL, mmHg, kg), and $\frac{N/2 - F}{f_m}$ is a dimensionless ratio between $0$ and $1$, the median $M$ inherits the same unit as the data. Always state units in your final answer for full marks.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Biostatistics & Engineering

- **Epidemiology:** Median incubation period of a viral disease.
- **Clinical Trials:** Median time-to-recovery in a drug efficacy study.
- **Public Health:** Median household income of a region (robust against billionaire outliers).
- **Pharmacokinetics:** Median effective dose ($ED_{50}$).
- **Quality Control in Biomedical Engineering:** Median sensor response time under noise.
- **Signal Processing:** Median filters are used to remove **salt-and-pepper noise** from medical images (CT, MRI) without blurring edges — a direct application of the median's robustness property.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight for KTU 2024:** The median is the **L1-optimal** measure of central tendency, meaning it minimises the sum of absolute deviations $\sum \vert x_i - M \vert$. This contrasts with the mean, which is the **L2-optimal** (minimises squared deviations). Examiners love this theoretical distinction.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Ungrouped Data (Even $N$)

**Problem:** Find the median of the following systolic blood pressure readings (in mmHg) from 8 patients:

$$X = \{118, \ 124, \ 130, \ 135, \ 142, \ 148, \ 155, \ 162\}$$

### Step 1 — Verify the data is already sorted

The dataset is given in ascending order. $N = 8$ (even).

### Step 2 — Compute the positions of the two central values

Since $N$ is even, the two middle values occupy positions $\dfrac{N}{2}$ and $\dfrac{N}{2}+1$.

$$\frac{N}{2} = \frac{8}{2} = 4 \quad ; \quad \frac{N}{2}+1 = 5$$

### Step 3 — Identify the two central values

Position 4 holds $x_4 = 135$ and position 5 holds $x_5 = 142$.

### Step 4 — Compute the arithmetic mean of the two central values

$$M = \frac{1}{2}\left(x_4 + x_5\right) = \frac{1}{2}\left(135 + 142\right) = \frac{1}{2}(277) = 138.5 \text{ mmHg}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** Median systolic blood pressure = **138.5 mmHg**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Discrete Frequency Distribution

**Problem:** The following table shows the number of days of hospitalisation for a sample of patients. Calculate the median.

| **Days ($x_i$)** | **Frequency ($f_i$)** | **Cumulative Frequency ($C_f$)** |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 3 | 2 | 2 |
| 5 | 5 | 7 |
| 7 | 8 | 15 |
| 9 | 4 | 19 |
| 11 | 1 | 20 |

### Step 1 — Compute total frequency $N$

$$N = \sum f_i = 2 + 5 + 8 + 4 + 1 = 20$$

### Step 2 — Compute the median position

$$\frac{N}{2} = \frac{20}{2} = 10$$

### Step 3 — Locate the first $C_f \ge 10$

Reading the cumulative frequency column:

$$C_{f,1} = 2, \quad C_{f,2} = 7, \quad C_{f,3} = 15$$

The first cumulative frequency that is $\ge 10$ is $C_{f,3} = 15$, which corresponds to $x_3 = 7$.

### Step 4 — State the median

$$M = x_3 = 7 \text{ days}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** Median hospitalisation period = **7 days**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Continuous Frequency Distribution (Full KTU-Style 14-Mark Question)

**Problem:** Compute the median for the following distribution of haemoglobin levels (g/dL) among 50 anaemic patients.

| **Class Interval (g/dL)** | **Frequency ($f_i$)** | **Cumulative Frequency ($C_f$)** |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 6.0 – 7.0 | 4 | 4 |
| 7.0 – 8.0 | 9 | 13 |
| 8.0 – 8.5 | 13 | 26 |
| 8.5 – 9.0 | 11 | 37 |
| 9.0 – 10.0 | 9 | 46 |
| 10.0 – 11.0 | 4 | 50 |

### Step 1 — Compute total frequency and median position

$$N = \sum f_i = 4 + 9 + 13 + 11 + 9 + 4 = 50$$

$$\frac{N}{2} = \frac{50}{2} = 25$$

### Step 2 — Identify the median class

Scanning the cumulative frequency column, we find the first $C_f \ge 25$.

| Class | $C_f$ | Check $C_f \ge 25$? |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 6.0 – 7.0 | 4 | No |
| 7.0 – 8.0 | 13 | No |
| 8.0 – 8.5 | 26 | **Yes — Median Class** |

Therefore, the **median class** is **8.0 – 8.5 g/dL**.

### Step 3 — Extract the parameters for the interpolation formula

$$L = \text{lower boundary of median class} = 8.0 \text{ g/dL}$$

$$F = C_f \text{ of class preceding median class} = 13$$

$$f_m = \text{frequency of median class} = 13$$

$$h = \text{class width of median class} = 8.5 - 8.0 = 0.5 \text{ g/dL}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute into the interpolation formula

$$M = L + \left(\frac{\dfrac{N}{2} - F}{f_m}\right) \times h$$

Substituting the values:

$$M = 8.0 + \left(\frac{25 - 13}{13}\right) \times 0.5$$

### Step 5 — Simplify the arithmetic

First, evaluate the fraction inside the parentheses:

$$\frac{25 - 13}{13} = \frac{12}{13}$$

Next, multiply by $h$:

$$\frac{12}{13} \times 0.5 = \frac{12 \times 0.5}{13} = \frac{6.0}{13} = 0.4615\ldots$$

Finally, add $L$:

$$M = 8.0 + 0.4615 = 8.4615 \text{ g/dL}$$

### Step 6 — Express with appropriate precision

$$M \approx 8.46 \text{ g/dL}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** Median haemoglobin level = **8.46 g/dL**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification of the result:** The median $8.46$ g/dL lies *within* the median class [8.0, 8.5), which is consistent with the definition of the median as an *interpolated* value within the class containing the 25th–26th observations.

---

## 3.4 Merits, Demerits, and Properties of the Median (High-Yield for KTU Viva)

| **Property** | **Statement** | **Exam Significance** |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| Rigorous definition | Partitions data into two equal halves | Often asked as 3-mark definition |
| Positional average | Independent of extreme values | Distinguishes from mean |
| Uniqueness | Always exists and is unique for any dataset | Theoretical foundation |
| Algebraic identity | $\sum \vert x_i - M \vert$ is minimised | **L1-optimality** property |
| Skewness indicator | $\text{Mean} - \text{Mode} = 3(\text{Mean} - \text{Median})$ | Empirical Karl Pearson relation |
| Limited algebraic treatment | Cannot be used in further algebraic manipulation | Major demerit |
| Open-end classes | Can be computed even for open-end classes | Advantage over mean |
| Graphical determination | Can be located from ogives (cumulative frequency curves) | Often tested in 14-mark problems |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Decision Flowchart — Selecting the Correct Median Formula

The diagram below maps the **decision logic** a student should follow upon encountering a median problem, based on the data type and the parity of $N$.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Given Dataset"] --> B{"Data Type?"}
    B -- "Ungrouped Raw" --> C{"N is Odd or Even?"}
    C -- "Odd" --> D["M = value of N plus 1 over 2 th item"]
    C -- "Even" --> E["M = half of x sub N by 2 plus x sub N by 2 plus 1"]
    B -- "Discrete Frequency" --> F["Compute C sub f, find first C sub f greater than or equal to N over 2"]
    F --> G["Median equals x sub k for that class"]
    B -- "Continuous Grouped" --> H["Compute C sub f, locate median class where C sub f greater than or equal to N over 2"]
    H --> I["Apply M equals L plus N by 2 minus F over f sub m times h"]
    I --> J["Report M with correct units"]
    G --> J
    D --> J
    E --> J
    J --> K["End: Median Reported"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Median Computation Pipeline

The diagram below illustrates the **functional architecture** of a median computation pipeline, mapping the data flow from raw input to the final interpolated output. This is a block-level representation of how the median is *operationally* obtained in a typical biostatistics workflow.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INP["INPUT LAYER"]
        A1["Raw Observations Vector X"]
        A2["Class Intervals and Frequencies"]
    end
    INP --> B1["Data Classifier Module"]
    B1 --> C1{"Parity and Type Check"}
    C1 -- "Ungrouped Odd" --> D1["Positional Indexer N plus 1 over 2"]
    C1 -- "Ungrouped Even" --> D2["Mean of Two Central Items"]
    C1 -- "Discrete" --> D3["Cumulative Frequency Builder"]
    C1 -- "Continuous" --> D4["Cumulative Frequency and Median Class Locator"]
    D1 --> E1["Median Output Layer"]
    D2 --> E1
    D3 --> E1
    D4 --> F1["Interpolation Engine M equals L plus N by 2 minus F over f sub m times h"]
    F1 --> E1
    E1 --> G1["Final Median with Units and Rounding"]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Position of the Median in the Ordered Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L["Lower Half 50 percent of data"] --> M["MEDIAN M - 50th Percentile Q2 D5"]
    M --> U["Upper Half 50 percent of data"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The median sits as the **pivot** between the lower and upper halves of the data. The lower half is exactly equal in *count* (not in *value*) to the upper half. This is the geometric intuition for the **50% probability statement** $P(X \le M) = 0.5$.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Cycle 1]**
Define the **median** as a measure of central tendency. State any **four properties** of the median.

**Model Answer:**

The median of a dataset is the **central value** that divides the distribution into two equal halves, with 50% of the observations lying below it and 50% lying above it, after the data have been arranged in ascending or descending order.

**Four properties:**

1. **Positional average:** Its value is determined solely by the position of an observation, not by its magnitude.
2. **Resistance to outliers:** The median is unaffected by extreme values because only the count of observations matters, not the magnitude of the extreme values.
3. **L1-optimality:** The median minimises the sum of absolute deviations $\sum \vert x_i - M \vert$.
4. **Always exists and unique:** For any finite, non-empty dataset, exactly one median exists (with the convention of averaging the two central values when $N$ is even).
5. **Suitable for open-end classes:** Unlike the mean, the median can be computed even for distributions with open-ended class intervals.
6. **Limited algebraic use:** It cannot be subjected to further algebraic manipulations, which is a major demerit.

*(Any four of the above will fetch full 3 marks.)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Supplementary Model Paper]**
Explain how the **median class** is determined in a continuous frequency distribution.

**Model Answer:**

The **median class** is the class interval in a continuous frequency distribution that contains the **median value** of the data. The procedural steps are:

1. **Compute the cumulative frequencies** ($C_f$) for each class interval.
2. **Compute the median position** $p = \dfrac{N}{2}$, where $N = \sum f_i$.
3. **Locate the median class:** It is the **first class** whose cumulative frequency is **greater than or equal to** $p = N/2$.

The median class is identified because the cumulative count of observations *crosses* the 50% mark within this class. Once the median class is identified, the exact median value is obtained by **linear interpolation** using the formula:

$$M = L + \left(\frac{\dfrac{N}{2} - F}{f_m}\right) \times h$$

where $L$ is the lower boundary of the median class, $F$ is the cumulative frequency of the class immediately preceding the median class, $f_m$ is the frequency of the median class, and $h$ is the class width.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Part A:**
> Students frequently confuse the median class with the class of *maximum frequency* (the modal class). These are **not the same** unless the distribution is perfectly symmetric. The median class is based on **cumulative count reaching 50%**, not on peak frequency.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model Question Paper, Set B]**

**(a)** Find the median of the following marks obtained by 9 students in a biostatistics test (out of 50):

$$35, \ 42, \ 28, \ 50, \ 39, \ 45, \ 31, \ 37, \ 41$$

**(b)** The following table shows the number of days absent from class for 60 B.Tech students in a semester. Calculate the **median** number of days absent.

| **Days Absent** | **Number of Students** |
| :---: | :---: |
| 0 – 5 | 8 |
| 5 – 10 | 14 |
| 10 – 15 | 18 |
| 15 – 20 | 10 |
| 20 – 25 | 6 |
| 25 – 30 | 4 |

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Arrange the data in ascending order**

The sorted dataset is:

$$28, \ 31, \ 35, \ 37, \ 39, \ 41, \ 42, \ 45, \ 50$$

Here, $N = 9$ (odd).

**Step 2 — Compute the position of the median**

$$p = \frac{N+1}{2} = \frac{9+1}{2} = 5$$

**Step 3 — Identify the median value**

The 5th item in the sorted order is **39**.

**Final Answer (a):** Median marks = **39 out of 50**.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Construct the cumulative frequency table**

Since the data is continuous, we treat the class intervals as inclusive on the lower limit. The cumulative frequencies are:

| **Days Absent** | **Frequency ($f_i$)** | **Cumulative Frequency ($C_f$)** |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0 – 5 | 8 | 8 |
| 5 – 10 | 14 | 22 |
| 10 – 15 | 18 | 40 |
| 15 – 20 | 10 | 50 |
| 20 – 25 | 6 | 56 |
| 25 – 30 | 4 | 60 |

**Step 2 — Compute the median position**

$$N = 60 \quad ; \quad \frac{N}{2} = 30$$

**Step 3 — Identify the median class**

| Class | $C_f$ | Check $C_f \ge 30$? |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0 – 5 | 8 | No |
| 5 – 10 | 22 | No |
| 10 – 15 | 40 | **Yes — Median Class** |

The **median class** is **10 – 15 days**.

**Step 4 — Extract interpolation parameters**

$$L = 10 \quad ; \quad F = 22 \quad ; \quad f_m = 18 \quad ; \quad h = 15 - 10 = 5$$

**Step 5 — Apply the median formula**

$$M = L + \left(\frac{\dfrac{N}{2} - F}{f_m}\right) \times h$$

$$M = 10 + \left(\frac{30 - 22}{18}\right) \times 5$$

$$M = 10 + \left(\frac{8}{18}\right) \times 5$$

$$M = 10 + \left(\frac{40}{18}\right) = 10 + 2.222\ldots = 12.22 \text{ days}$$

**Final Answer (b):** Median number of days absent = **12.22 days (approximately)**.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Paper, Set A]**

**(a)** Explain the procedure to compute the **median for a discrete frequency distribution** with a suitable example. State the relevant formula and identify the key steps.

**(b)** The following data represents the **waiting time (in minutes)** of 100 patients at a hospital OPD. Compute the median and interpret the result.

| **Waiting Time (min)** | **Number of Patients** |
| :---: | :---: |
| 0 – 10 | 12 |
| 10 – 20 | 18 |
| 20 – 30 | 25 |
| 30 – 40 | 20 |
| 40 – 50 | 15 |
| 50 – 60 | 10 |

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Procedure to compute the median for a discrete frequency distribution:**

**Step 1:** Tabulate the data with two columns — the variable values $x_i$ and their corresponding frequencies $f_i$.

**Step 2:** Compute the **cumulative frequencies** $C_f = \sum_{j=1}^{i} f_j$ and add a third column to the table.

**Step 3:** Compute the total frequency $N = \sum f_i$ and determine the median position $p = \dfrac{N}{2}$.

**Step 4:** Starting from the top of the cumulative frequency column, locate the **first entry** that is **greater than or equal to** $p = N/2$. The corresponding $x_i$ value is the **median**.

**Formula:**

$$M = x_k \quad \text{where} \quad C_{f,k} = \text{first } C_f \ge \frac{N}{2}$$
