```mermaid
    Likelihood2["Likelihoods P of A given B_i"] --> Denominator
    Numerator --> Posterior["Posterior P of B_k given A"]
    Denominator --> Posterior
```

### 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Probability Laws in Biostatistics

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Input["Raw Data / Sample Space"] --> Stage1["Stage 1: Identify Events and Sample Space"]
    Stage1 --> Stage2["Stage 2: Determine Independence or Dependence"]
    Stage2 --> Decision1{"Independent?"}
    Decision1 -- Yes --> M1["Multiply Directly: P of A times P of B"]
    Decision1 -- No --> M2["Use Conditional Probability: P of A times P of B given A"]
    M1 --> Stage3["Stage 3: Apply Selected Law"]
    M2 --> Stage3
    Stage3 --> Stage4["Stage 4: Verify using Sample Space or Venn Diagram"]
    Stage4 --> Output["Final Probability Answer"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

State and explain the **Addition Law of Probability** for two events that are (i) mutually exclusive, and (ii) not mutually exclusive.

**Model Answer:**

The Addition Law of Probability provides the probability that at least one of two events $A$ or $B$ occurs.

**(i) Mutually Exclusive Events** ($A \cap B = \emptyset$): When events cannot occur simultaneously, the law simplifies to:

$$P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B)$$

**(ii) General (Non-Mutually Exclusive) Events:** When the events can co-occur, we must subtract the intersection to avoid double-counting:

$$P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)$$

> **KTU Marking Pattern:** 1 mark for statement, 1 mark for mutually exclusive case, 1 mark for general case.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

Define **conditional probability** and state the **Multiplication Law of Probability** for independent events.

**Model Answer:**

**Conditional Probability** $P(A \mid B)$ is the probability of event $A$ occurring given that event $B$ has already occurred:

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}, \quad P(B) > 0$$

**Multiplication Law for Independent Events:** Two events $A$ and $B$ are independent if the occurrence of one does not affect the other. Then:

$$P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$$

> **KTU Marking Pattern:** 1.5 marks for conditional probability definition + formula, 1.5 marks for independent multiplication law.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** State and prove the **Total Probability Theorem**. Mention its significance in biostatistics.

**(b)** In a population, the probability that a person has disease D is **0.05**. A diagnostic test for D gives a positive result with probability **0.95** if the person has the disease, and with probability **0.08** if the person does not have the disease. If a randomly selected person tests positive, find the probability that they actually have the disease.

---

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Total Probability Theorem**

**Statement:** If $\{B_1, B_2, \ldots, B_n\}$ is a partition of the sample space $S$ (i.e., the $B_i$ are mutually exclusive and exhaustive), then for any event $A$:

$$P(A) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(B_i) \cdot P(A \mid B_i)$$

**Proof:**

**Step 1:** Since $\{B_i\}$ partitions $S$, we have $S = \bigcup_{i=1}^n B_i$.

**Step 2:** Intersect both sides with $A$:

$$A = A \cap S = A \cap \left(\bigcup_{i=1}^{n} B_i\right) = \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} (A \cap B_i)$$

**Step 3:** The sets $\{A \cap B_i\}$ are mutually disjoint (since $\{B_i\}$ are disjoint). Apply Axiom 3:

$$P(A) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(A \cap B_i)$$

**Step 4:** Apply the Multiplication Law $P(A \cap B_i) = P(B_i) \cdot P(A \mid B_i)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(A) &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(B_i) \cdot P(A \mid B_i)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Significance in Biostatistics:** The Total Probability Theorem allows us to compute the overall probability of a clinical event by aggregating information across all possible causes or sub-populations — a foundational tool in epidemiology and medical screening analysis.

**[7 marks breakdown: 2 statement + 4 proof steps + 1 significance]**

---

**Part (b) — Bayes' Theorem Application**

**Step 1 — Define events:**

- $D$ = person has the disease, so $P(D) = 0.05$ and $P(D^c) = 0.95$.
- $T$ = person tests positive.
- $P(T \mid D) = 0.95$ (true positive rate / sensitivity).
- $P(T \mid D^c) = 0.08$ (false positive rate).

**Step 2 — Find the total probability of testing positive using the Total Probability Theorem:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(T) &= P(D) \cdot P(T \mid D) + P(D^c) \cdot P(T \mid D^c) \\
&= (0.05)(0.95) + (0.95)(0.08) \\
&= 0.0475 + 0.0760 \\
&= 0.1235
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Bayes' Theorem to find $P(D \mid T)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(D \mid T) &= \frac{P(D) \cdot P(T \mid D)}{P(T)} \\
&= \frac{0.0475}{0.1235} \\
&\approx 0.3846
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Even after a positive test, the probability that the person actually has the disease is only about **38.46%**. This counter-intuitive result is a classic illustration of the **base-rate fallacy** in medical screening.

**[7 marks breakdown: 2 setup + 3 total probability + 2 Bayes calculation]**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** State and prove the **Multiplication Law of Probability** for two events that are dependent. Show that it reduces to the independent case under the appropriate condition.

**(b)** The probability that a doctor correctly diagnoses a disease is **0.80**. If three independent cases of the same disease are presented to the doctor, find the probability that: (i) all three are diagnosed correctly, (ii) exactly two are diagnosed correctly, (iii) at least one is diagnosed correctly.

---

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Multiplication Law for Dependent Events**

**Statement:** For any two events $A$ and $B$ with $P(A) > 0$:

$$P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B \mid A)$$

**Proof:**

**Step 1:** Start with the definition of conditional probability:

$$P(B \mid A) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(A)}, \quad P(A) > 0$$

**Step 2:** Multiply both sides by $P(A)$:

$$P(A) \cdot P(B \mid A) = P(A \cap B)$$

This is the Multiplication Law for dependent events.

**Reduction to Independent Case:**

If $A$ and $B$ are **independent**, the occurrence of $A$ does not change the probability of $B$, so:

$$P(B \mid A) = P(B)$$

Substituting this into the dependent-case formula:

$$P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$$

This is the Multiplication Law for **independent events**. ∎

**[7 marks breakdown: 2 statement + 3 proof + 2 reduction to independent case]**

---

**Part (b) — Binomial Application of Multiplication Law**

Let $p = 0.80$ (probability of correct diagnosis per case) and $q = 1 - p = 0.20$ (probability of incorrect diagnosis). Let $X$ = number of correct diagnoses among 3 independent cases. Then $X \sim \text{Binomial}(n=3, p=0.80)$.

**(i) Probability all three are correct ($X = 3$):**

By the independent Multiplication Law applied thrice:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(X = 3) &= p \cdot p \cdot p = (0.80)^3 = 0.512
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Probability exactly two are correct ($X = 2$):**

Choose which 2 out of 3 cases are correct: $\binom{3}{2} = 3$ ways. Each arrangement is $p^2 q^1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(X = 2) &= \binom{3}{2} p^2 q^1 \\
&= 3 \times (0.80)^2 \times (0.20) \\
&= 3 \times 0.64 \times 0.20 \\
&= 3 \times 0.128 \\
&= 0.384
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Probability at least one is correct ($X \geq 1$):**

Using the **Complement Rule**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(X \geq 1) &= 1 - P(X = 0) \\
&= 1 - (0.20)^3 \\
&= 1 - 0.008 \\
&= 0.992
\end{aligned}
$$

**[7 marks breakdown: 2 part (i) + 3 part (ii) + 2 part (iii)]**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Zones (read carefully):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to state the assumption.** If you write $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$ without explicitly saying "since $A$ and $B$ are independent," expect a **2-mark deduction** out of 14.
> 2. **Misapplying Bayes' Theorem.** Students often invert the conditional probability (writing $P(A \mid B)$ instead of $P(B \mid A)$). Always pause and ask: *"Which event's probability am I trying to find? That goes on the LEFT of the bar."*
> 3. **Skipping the Total Probability denominator.** In Bayes' Theorem problems, you must compute $P(A)$ via the Total Probability Theorem *first*. A common error is to use only the numerator terms.
> 4. **Forgetting $P(B) > 0$.** Conditional probability is undefined when $P(B) = 0$. Examiners specifically test this edge case.
> 5. **Double-counting intersections.** Using the Addition Law formula $P(A) + P(B)$ instead of $P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)$ for non-mutually-exclusive events loses **full 3 marks** in 14-mark problems.
> 6. **Independence assumption for binomial problems.** When a question says "three independent cases," students sometimes forget to explicitly cite independence before applying $p^n$. This costs a mark.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 1: Laws of Probability**

- **Complement Rule:** $P(A^c) = 1 - P(A)$. Always use this when asked "at least one" of *many* events.
- **Addition Law (Mutually Exclusive):** $P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B)$. Disjoint events only.
- **Addition Law (General):** $P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)$. Always subtract the intersection.
- **Conditional Probability:** $P(A \mid B) = \dfrac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$. Numerator is intersection; denominator is the *given* event.
- **Multiplication Law (General/Dependent):** $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B \mid A)$.
- **Multiplication Law (Independent):** $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$. State the independence assumption explicitly.
- **Total Probability Theorem:** $P(A) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(B_i) \cdot P(A \mid B_i)$. Used when sample space is partitioned.
- **Bayes' Theorem:** $P(B_k \mid A) = \dfrac{P(B_k) \cdot P(A \mid B_k)}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} P(B_i) \cdot P(A \mid B_i)}$. Used for "reverse" / posterior reasoning.
- **Base-Rate Fallacy:** A highly accurate test on a rare disease can still produce many false positives — always compute $P(D \mid +)$ using Bayes, not just sensitivity.
- **Numerical Sanity Check:** Probabilities must always lie in $[0, 1]$. If your answer falls outside, you have made a sign or arithmetic error.
- **Always declare assumptions** (independence, mutual exclusivity, exhaustiveness) **before** plugging numbers into formulas.
- **Binomial Shortcut:** For $n$ independent identical trials with success probability $p$, the probability of exactly $k$ successes is $\binom{n}{k} p^k (1-p)^{n-k}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bayes' Rule and Screening Tests
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT861 - BIOSTATISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Bayes' Rule and Screening Tests

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Bayes' Rule — Formal Academic Definition

**Bayes' Rule** (also called **Bayes' Theorem** or the **Inverse Probability Rule**) is a foundational theorem in probability theory that expresses how to update the probability of a hypothesis $(H)$ when new evidence $(E)$ becomes available. Formally, for two events $A$ and $B$ with $P(B) > 0$:

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}$$

where:
- $P(A)$ is the **prior probability** — our belief about $A$ before seeing evidence.
- $P(B \mid A)$ is the **likelihood** — the probability of observing evidence $B$ given that $A$ is true.
- $P(B)$ is the **marginal probability** (or evidence) — the total probability of observing $B$ under all mutually exclusive causes.
- $P(A \mid B)$ is the **posterior probability** — the updated belief about $A$ after observing $B$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Bayes' Rule is the cornerstone of diagnostic reasoning. In medical statistics, it allows us to convert a test result (which has known sensitivity/specificity) into the actual probability that a patient has the disease — a direct application of inverse probability.

### 1.2 Screening Tests — Formal Academic Definition

A **Screening Test** is a preliminary diagnostic procedure applied to an apparently healthy (asymptomatic) population to identify individuals who *likely* have a particular disease. Screening tests are characterized by four cardinal performance metrics:

| Metric | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Sensitivity** | $\text{Se}$ | $P(T^+ \mid D^+)$ — ability to correctly detect diseased |
| **Specificity** | $\text{Sp}$ | $P(T^- \mid D^-)$ — ability to correctly clear non-diseased |
| **Positive Predictive Value** | $\text{PPV}$ | $P(D^+ \mid T^+)$ — disease given positive test |
| **Negative Predictive Value** | $\text{NPV}$ | $P(D^- \mid T^-)$ — no disease given negative test |

> [!NOTE]
> The relationship between Bayes' Rule and Screening Tests is intrinsic. **PPV and NPV are direct posterior probabilities** computed via Bayes' Rule using sensitivity, specificity, and disease prevalence.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Analogy 1 — The Courtroom Verdict (Bayes' Rule):**
Imagine a juror walks into a courtroom with a vague suspicion about the defendant. This is the **prior**. As the trial proceeds, new pieces of evidence (witnesses, forensic reports) come in. Each piece of evidence has a certain *likelihood* of appearing whether the defendant is guilty or innocent. Bayes' Rule tells the juror exactly *how much to update* their belief after every new piece of evidence. The final belief is the **posterior**.

**Analogy 2 — The COVID Rapid Test (Screening Test):**
Suppose you take a rapid antigen test. The box says it is "95% accurate." Most people interpret this as: "If I test positive, I have a 95% chance of having COVID." This is **wrong**. The 95% is the test's *sensitivity* — not the same as the *risk that you actually have the disease given a positive result*. The actual risk (PPV) depends heavily on **prevalence** — how common the disease is in your community. This is the classic **base-rate fallacy**, and Bayes' Rule is the mathematical correction.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Heuristic:** When prevalence is LOW, even an excellent screening test will produce many false positives, making PPV much smaller than sensitivity. This is why mass screening for rare diseases is problematic.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Logic of Bayes' Rule — Step-by-Step Reasoning

Bayes' Rule is derived from the symmetry of joint probability. Here is the underlying logic:

1. **Joint probability is symmetric:** $P(A \cap B) = P(B \cap A)$.
2. **Definition of conditional probability:** $P(A \mid B) = \dfrac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$ and $P(B \mid A) = \dfrac{P(A \cap B)}{P(A)}$.
3. **Equating and solving:** Both expressions equal $P(A \cap B)$, so set them equal and solve for $P(A \mid B)$.
4. **Total Probability Rule for $P(B)$:** When $A$ and $\bar{A}$ partition the sample space, $P(B) = P(B \mid A)P(A) + P(B \mid \bar{A})P(\bar{A})$.
5. **Final substitution:** Replace $P(B)$ in the conditional formula to obtain the general form.

The *Why*: We rarely know $P(A \mid B)$ directly. But we often know $P(B \mid A)$ from empirical data. Bayes' Rule flips the conditioning direction.

### 2.2 The 2×2 Contingency Table — The Heart of Screening Tests

The classical framework for evaluating a screening test on $N$ individuals is the **2×2 contingency table**:

| | Disease Present $(D^+)$ | Disease Absent $(D^-)$ | Total |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Test Positive $(T^+)$** | True Positive (TP) | False Positive (FP) | $T^+$ Total |
| **Test Negative $(T^-)$** | False Negative (FN) | True Negative (TN) | $T^-$ Total |
| **Total** | $D^+$ Total | $D^-$ Total | $N$ |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table for any KTU 14-mark question on this topic.** No vertical pipe characters are used inside the formulas — all absolute values and conditioning bars are written as $\vert \cdot \vert$ using LaTeX commands.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sensitivity (Se) | $\dfrac{TP}{TP + FN} = P(T^+ \mid D^+)$ | True positive rate |
| 2 | Specificity (Sp) | $\dfrac{TN}{TN + FP} = P(T^- \mid D^-)$ | True negative rate |
| 3 | False Positive Rate (FPR) | $1 - Sp = P(T^+ \mid D^-)$ | Type I error rate |
| 4 | False Negative Rate (FNR) | $1 - Se = P(T^- \mid D^+)$ | Type II error rate |
| 5 | Prevalence (Prev) | $\dfrac{TP + FN}{N} = P(D^+)$ | Pre-test probability |
| 6 | Positive Predictive Value (PPV) | $\dfrac{TP}{TP + FP} = P(D^+ \mid T^+)$ | Post-test probability of disease |
| 7 | Negative Predictive Value (NPV) | $\dfrac{TN}{TN + FN} = P(D^- \mid T^-)$ | Post-test probability of health |
| 8 | PPV via Bayes | $\dfrac{Se \cdot Prev}{Se \cdot Prev + (1-Sp)(1-Prev)}$ | Bayes' applied to screening |
| 9 | NPV via Bayes | $\dfrac{Sp \cdot (1-Prev)}{Sp \cdot (1-Prev) + (1-Se) \cdot Prev}$ | Bayes' applied to screening |
| 10 | Positive Likelihood Ratio (LR+) | $\dfrac{Se}{1 - Sp}$ | Diagnostic strength of $T^+$ |
| 11 | Negative Likelihood Ratio (LR−) | $\dfrac{1 - Se}{Sp}$ | Diagnostic strength of $T^-$ |
| 12 | Accuracy | $\dfrac{TP + TN}{N}$ | Overall correctness |
| 13 | Youden's J Index | $Se + Sp - 1$ | Global test performance |
| 14 | Diagnostic Odds Ratio (DOR) | $\dfrac{Se \cdot Sp}{(1 - Se)(1 - Sp)} = \dfrac{LR+}{LR-}$ | Combined LR measure |
| 15 | Bayes' Rule (General) | $P(A_i \mid B) = \dfrac{P(B \mid A_i)P(A_i)}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} P(B \mid A_j)P(A_j)}$ | Multi-cause extension |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering / Medical Utility

- **Medical Diagnosis:** Converting a positive COVID, HIV, or cancer screening result into the actual risk of disease.
- **Public Health Policy:** Deciding whether mass screening is worthwhile for a given disease prevalence.
- **Machine Learning & Spam Filters:** Naive Bayes classifiers use exactly this rule to classify emails.
- **Quality Control Engineering:** Updating defect rates in a manufacturing line after a new batch inspection.
- **Reliability Engineering:** Updating component failure probabilities after stress-test evidence.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Prevalence Effect:** A *fixed* test (say 95% Se, 90% Sp) yields a dramatically different PPV at 1% prevalence versus 50% prevalence. This is the most commonly tested KTU concept.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Bayes' Rule from First Principles

**Starting Point:** Definition of conditional probability for any two events $A$ and $B$:

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)} \quad \text{(provided } P(B) > 0\text{)}$$

**Step 1:** By the symmetry of joint probability, $P(A \cap B) = P(B \cap A)$.

**Step 2:** Using the conditional definition the other way:

$$P(B \mid A) = \frac{P(B \cap A)}{P(A)} = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(A)}$$

**Step 3:** Solve Step 2 for the joint probability:

$$P(A \cap B) = P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)$$

**Step 4:** Substitute Step 3 into Step 1:

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}$$

**Step 5 (Total Probability Expansion):** Express $P(B)$ by partitioning the sample space using $A$ and $\bar{A}$:

$$P(B) = P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A) + P(B \mid \bar{A}) \cdot P(\bar{A})$$

**Step 6 (Final Form):** Substitute Step 5 into Step 4:

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A) + P(B \mid \bar{A}) \cdot P(\bar{A})}$$

$\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Worked Example 1 — The Classic Cancer Screening Problem

**Problem Statement (KTU Style):**
A certain cancer has prevalence $1\%$ in the population. A screening test for this cancer has sensitivity $95\%$ and specificity $90\%$. A randomly selected person tests positive. Using Bayes' Rule, find the probability that the person actually has the cancer. Comment on the result.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define events and known quantities.**

$$P(D^+) = 0.01, \quad P(T^+ \mid D^+) = 0.95, \quad P(T^- \mid D^-) = 0.90$$

$$P(D^-) = 0.99, \quad P(T^+ \mid D^-) = 1 - 0.90 = 0.10$$

**Step 2 — State the goal in Bayes' notation.**

We seek the posterior $P(D^+ \mid T^+)$ — the PPV.

$$P(D^+ \mid T^+) = \frac{P(T^+ \mid D^+) \cdot P(D^+)}{P(T^+)}$$

**Step 3 — Compute marginal $P(T^+)$ via Total Probability.**

$$P(T^+) = P(T^+ \mid D^+) P(D^+) + P(T^+ \mid D^-) P(D^-)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$P(T^+) = (0.95)(0.01) + (0.10)(0.99) = 0.0095 + 0.0990 = 0.1085$$

**Step 5 — Compute PPV.**

$$P(D^+ \mid T^+) = \frac{0.0095}{0.1085} = 0.08756 \approx 8.76\%$$

**Interpretation:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Despite a 95% sensitive test, a positive result only confers an **8.76%** chance of actually having the disease. This is because the disease is rare (1% prevalence) — the false positives from the *large non-diseased population* outnumber the true positives. This is the **base-rate fallacy** quantified by Bayes' Rule.

### 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Effect of Prevalence on PPV

Using the same test (Se = 0.95, Sp = 0.90), recompute PPV at a higher prevalence of $P(D^+) = 0.10$:

**Step 1 — Total probability:**

$$P(T^+) = (0.95)(0.10) + (0.10)(0.90) = 0.0950 + 0.0900 = 0.1850$$

**Step 2 — PPV:**

$$P(D^+ \mid T^+) = \frac{0.0950}{0.1850} = 0.5135 \approx 51.35\%$$

**Conclusion:** At 10% prevalence, the same test now yields a PPV above 50%. **The test performs dramatically better in high-prevalence populations.** This is why diagnostic follow-up testing is recommended after screening positives.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Prevalence Scenarios

| Prevalence $P(D^+)$ | $P(T^+)$ total | $P(D^+ \mid T^+)$ = PPV | $P(D^- \mid T^-)$ = NPV |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0.001 (0.1%) | $0.00095 + 0.0999 = 0.1009$ | $0.00095 / 0.1009 = 0.94\%$ | $0.8910 / 0.8991 = 99.11\%$ |
| 0.01 (1%) | $0.1085$ | $8.76\%$ | $98.99\%$ |
| 0.05 (5%) | $0.1425$ | $33.33\%$ | $99.74\%$ |
| 0.10 (10%) | $0.1850$ | $51.35\%$ | $99.47\%$ |
| 0.50 (50%) | $0.5250$ | $90.48\%$ | $94.74\%$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Pattern Recognition for KTU:** PPV increases monotonically with prevalence; NPV decreases monotonically. When prevalence = 0.5, PPV > NPV for tests with Se + Sp > 1 (which is required for the test to be informative, i.e., Youden's J > 0).

### 3.5 Likelihood Ratio Interpretation

The Bayes formula can be rewritten in odds form (the **Odds Ratio Form of Bayes' Theorem**):

$$\text{Post-test Odds} = \text{LR} \times \text{Pre-test Odds}$$

where $\text{LR} = \dfrac{P(T^+ \mid D^+)}{P(T^+ \mid D^-)} = \dfrac{Se}{1 - Sp}$ for a positive result.

**Worked LR Example:**
For Se = 0.95, Sp = 0.90:

$$LR+ = \frac{0.95}{1 - 0.90} = \frac{0.95}{0.10} = 9.5$$

**Interpretation:** A positive test makes disease **9.5 times more likely**. Pre-test odds at 1% prevalence: $0.01 / 0.99 = 0.01010$. Post-test odds: $9.5 \times 0.01010 = 0.09596$. Converting back to probability: $\dfrac{0.09596}{1 + 0.09596} = 0.0876$ ✓ (matches PPV).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Bayesian Updating Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Prior Belief P of A] --> B[Collect Evidence E]
    B --> C[Compute Likelihood P of E given A]
    C --> D[Compute Marginal P of E using Total Probability]
    D --> E[Apply Bayes Rule]
    A --> E
    E --> F[Posterior Belief P of A given E]
    F --> G{New Evidence Available?}
    G -- Yes --> B
    G -- No --> H[Decision or Inference]
```

### 4.2 Screening Test Decision Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Population of N Individuals] --> apply[Apply Screening Test]
    apply --> tpos[Test Positive T+]
    apply --> tneg[Test Negative T-]
    tpos --> tpcount[TP plus FP]
    tneg --> tncount[TN plus FN]
    tpcount --> ppv[Compute PPV = TP / TP plus FP]
    tncount --> npv[Compute NPV = TN / TN plus FN]
    ppv --> bayes[PPV = Se x Prev / Se x Prev + 1 minus Sp x 1 minus Prev]
    npv --> bayes2[NPV = Sp x 1 minus Prev / Sp x 1 minus Prev + 1 minus Se x Prev]
    bayes --> confirm[Recommend Confirmatory Diagnostic Test]
    bayes2 --> confirm
    confirm --> done[Final Diagnosis]
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Mapping: Likelihoods, Priors, Posteriors

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    p[Prior P of Disease] --> mult[Multiply]
    l[Likelihood of Test Result]
    mult --> num[Numerator]
    l2[Likelihood of Test Result given No Disease] --> den[Denominator Evidence]
    np[Prior P of No Disease] --> den
    den --> div[Division]
    num --> div
    div --> post[Posterior P of Disease given Test]
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Screening Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    blockA[Block 1: Define Prevalence from Population Data] --> blockB[Block 2: Determine Test Sensitivity and Specificity from Validation Study]
    blockB --> blockC[Block 3: Apply Test to Target Population]
    blockC --> blockD[Block 4: Construct 2x2 Contingency Table]
    blockD --> blockE[Block 5: Compute Se Sp PPV NPV LR plus LR minus]
    blockE --> blockF[Block 6: Apply Bayes Rule to Update Patient Risk]
    blockF --> blockG[Block 7: Clinical Decision Threshold Check]
    blockG --> blockH[Block 8: Confirmatory Test or Discharge]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip:** Each block in the architecture represents a logically independent statistical step. In a 14-mark KTU question, the examiner typically rewards students who explicitly label these stages rather than collapsing the entire solution into a single formula.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEBRT861]`
**State Bayes' Theorem and explain the terms: prior probability, likelihood, and posterior probability. Why is the theorem important in medical diagnosis?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Statement of Bayes' Theorem:**

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}$$

provided $P(B) > 0$.

**Definitions (1.5 marks):**

- **Prior Probability $P(A)$:** The initial probability of the event/hypothesis *before* observing any new evidence. In medical terms, this is the disease prevalence in the population.
- **Likelihood $P(B \mid A)$:** The probability of observing the evidence (e.g., a positive test result) given that the hypothesis is true. It measures how compatible the evidence is with the hypothesis.
- **Posterior Probability $P(A \mid B)$:** The updated probability of the hypothesis *after* incorporating the evidence.

**Importance in Medical Diagnosis (1.5 marks):**

Bayes' Theorem allows clinicians to convert a screening test result (which gives $P(T \mid D)$, the likelihood) into the actual risk of disease (the posterior, $P(D \mid T)$, i.e., the **PPV**). It corrects the natural tendency of patients to misinterpret "test accuracy" as "personal risk of disease," especially at low prevalence. Without Bayes' Rule, screening programs would be mis-evaluated and patient counselling would be misleading.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, PEBRT861]`
**Define Sensitivity and Specificity of a screening test. How are they related to False Positive Rate and False Negative Rate?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definitions (2 marks):**

- **Sensitivity** is the probability that the test correctly identifies *diseased* individuals as positive:
$$\text{Sensitivity} = P(T^+ \mid D^+) = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$$

- **Specificity** is the probability that the test correctly identifies *non-diseased* individuals as negative:
$$\text{Specificity} = P(T^- \mid D^-) = \frac{TN}{TN + FP}$$

**Relationship to Error Rates (1 mark):**

- **False Positive Rate (FPR)** $= 1 - \text{Specificity} = P(T^+ \mid D^-)$ — proportion of healthy people incorrectly labelled as diseased.
- **False Negative Rate (FNR)** $= 1 - \text{Sensitivity} = P(T^- \mid D^+)$ — proportion of diseased people incorrectly labelled as healthy.

Sensitivity and Specificity are *intrinsic* properties of the test itself and do not depend on disease prevalence, whereas the predictive values (PPV/NPV) do.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks with internal choice (Question A OR Question B). Each contains two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Both choices are provided below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, PEBRT861]`

**A medical screening programme for Diabetes is run on 10,000 adults. The screening test has Sensitivity 80% and Specificity 95%. The prevalence of diabetes in this population is 4%.**

**(a)** Construct the 2×2 contingency table for the screening outcome. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the PPV, NPV, Positive Likelihood Ratio (LR+), and Negative Likelihood Ratio (LR−). Comment on the suitability of this test for mass screening. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) — Constructing the 2×2 Table**

**Given:** $N = 10{,}000$, $\text{Prev} = P(D^+) = 0.04$, $\text{Se} = 0.80$, $\text{Sp} = 0.95$.

**Step 1 — Compute disease totals.**

$$D^+ = 0.04 \times 10{,}000 = 400 \quad ; \quad D^- = 0.96 \times 10{,}000 = 9{,}600$$

**Step 2 — Compute TP using sensitivity.**

$$TP = \text{Se} \times D^+ = 0.80 \times 400 = 320$$

**Step 3 — Compute FN using $1 - \text{Se}$.**

$$FN = (1 - \text{Se}) \times D^+ = 0.20 \times 400 = 80$$

**Step 4 — Compute TN using specificity.**

$$TN = \text{Sp} \times D^- = 0.95 \times 9{,}600 = 9{,}120$$

**Step 5 — Compute FP using $1 - \text{Sp}$.**

$$FP = (1 - \text{Sp}) \times D^- = 0.05 \times 9{,}600 = 480$$

**Step 6 — Verify row totals.**

- $T^+ = TP + FP = 320 + 480 = 800$
- $T^- = FN + TN = 80 + 9{,}120 = 9{,}200$
- $T^+ + T^- = 800 + 9{,}200 = 10{,}000 = N$ ✓

**2×2 Contingency Table:**

| | $D^+$ (Diseased) | $D^-$ (Healthy) | Total |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| $T^+$ (Test +) | **320** | **480** | 800 |
| $T^-$ (Test −) | **80** | **9,120** | 9,200 |
| **Total** | 400 | 9,600 | 10,000 |

---

**Part (b) — Computing PPV, NPV, LR+, LR−**

**Step 1 — PPV** (posterior probability of disease given positive test):

$$\text{PPV} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{320}{800} = 0.40 = 40\%$$

**Step 2 — NPV** (posterior probability of being healthy given negative test):

$$\text{NPV} = \frac{TN}{TN + FN} = \frac{9{,}120}{9{,}200} = 0.99130 = 99.13\%$$

**Step 3 — Positive Likelihood Ratio:**

$$LR+ = \frac{\text{Se}}{1 - \text{Sp}} = \frac{0.80}{0.05} = 16.0$$

**Step 4 — Negative Likelihood Ratio:**

$$LR- = \frac{1 - \text{Se}}{\text{Sp}} = \frac{0.20}{0.95} \approx 0.2105$$

**Comments on Mass Screening Suitability (Discussion):**

> [!TIP]
> The test has a high NPV (99.13%), meaning negatives are highly trustworthy — patients who test negative can be confidently reassured. The PPV is moderate (40%), meaning more than half of the positives are actually false positives. The high LR+ of 16 indicates strong diagnostic value, and the LR− of 0.21 is also good (values < 0.2 are considered strong evidence *against* disease). **Conclusion:** This test is reasonably suitable for *screening* (excluding disease) but positives should undergo a confirmatory diagnostic test (e.g., fasting glucose or HbA1c) to rule out false positives.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, PEBRT861]`

**(a)** State and derive Bayes' Theorem from the definition of conditional probability. Explain the meaning of each term in the context of a diagnostic test. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A diagnostic test for a disease has Sensitivity 90% and Specificity 85%. If the disease prevalence in the population is 2%, calculate the probability that a person actually has the disease given a positive test result. What would happen to this probability if the prevalence doubled to 4%? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) — Statement and Derivation of Bayes' Theorem**

**Statement (2 marks):**

For two events $A$ and $B$ with $P(B) > 0$, Bayes' Theorem states:

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}$$

**Derivation (3 marks):**

By the definition of conditional probability:

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)} \quad \text{...(1)}$$

Similarly:

$$P(B \mid A) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(A)} \quad \text{...(2)}$$

From equation (2), we get $P(A \cap B) = P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)$. Substituting into (1):

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}$$

Applying the **Total Probability Theorem** to expand $P(B)$ where $A$ and $\bar{A}$ form a partition of the sample space:

$$P(B) = P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A) + P(B \mid \bar{A}) \cdot P(\bar{A})$$

Hence the fully expanded form is:

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B \mid A) P(A) + P(B \mid \bar{A}) P(\bar{A})}$$

**Contextual Meaning in a Diagnostic Test (2 marks):**

Let $A = D^+$ (disease present) and $B = T^+$ (test positive). Then:

- $P(A) = P(D^+)$ = **Prevalence** of the disease (prior).
- $P(B \mid A) = P(T^+ \mid D^+)$ = **Sensitivity** of the test (likelihood).
- $P(B \mid \bar{A}) = P(T^+ \mid D^-) = 1 - \text{Specificity}$ (false positive rate).
- $P(A \mid B) = P(D^+ \mid T^+)$ = **PPV** (posterior — probability of disease given positive test).

Thus Bayes' Theorem converts a test's *intrinsic* performance (Se, Sp) into the *patient-specific* probability of disease.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Application**

**Step 1 — Identify given values.**

$$\text{Se} = 0.90, \quad \text{Sp} = 0.85, \quad \text{Prev} = P(D^+) = 0.02$$

**Step 2 — Compute the marginal probability $P(T^+)$ using the Total Probability Theorem.**

$$P(T^+) = P(T^+ \mid D^+) P(D^+) + P(T^+ \mid D^-) P(D^-)$$

$$P(T^+) = (0.90)(0.02) + (1 - 0.85)(0.98)$$

$$P(T^+) = 0.0180 + (0.15)(0.98) = 0.0180 + 0.1470 = 0.1650$$

**Step 3 — Apply Bayes' Theorem to compute PPV.**

$$P(D^+ \mid T^+) = \frac{P(T^+ \mid D^+) \cdot P(D^+)}{P(T^+)} = \frac{0.0180}{0.1650} = 0.10909 \approx 10.91\%$$

**Step 4 — Repeat with doubled prevalence $P(D^+) = 0.04$.**

$$P(T^+) = (0.90)(0.04) + (0.15)(0.96) = 0.0360 + 0.1440 = 0.1800$$

$$P(D^+ \mid T^+) = \frac{0.0360}{0.1800} = 0.2000 = 20.00\%$$

**Comment (Critical Discussion):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When prevalence doubles from 2% to 4%, the PPV nearly **doubles** from 10.91% to 20.00%. This confirms the strong dependence of PPV on prevalence. Even with a highly accurate test, a positive result at low prevalence carries far less diagnostic weight than the public typically assumes. **This is why Bayes' Theorem is indispensable in public health policy and clinical decision-making.**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Leading to Mark Deductions in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing Sensitivity with PPV.** Sensitivity is $P(T^+ \mid D^+)$; PPV is $P(D^+ \mid T^+)$. These are *not* the same. Many students write "PPV = 90%" when the question states "sensitivity = 90%." This costs 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert prevalence to a probability.** If prevalence is given as "4 per 1000," divide by 1000 *before* using in the Bayes formula.
> 3. **Omitting the Total Probability step.** The denominator $P(B)$ in Bayes' Rule *must* be expanded. Writing only $\frac{P(B \mid A) P(A)}{P(B)}$ without expansion is acceptable in 7-mark parts but loses 1 mark in 14-mark derivations.
> 4. **Not verifying the 2×2 row/column totals.** KTU examiners award 1 mark specifically for verifying that $T^+ + T^- = N$ and $D^+ + D^- = N$.
> 5. **Ignoring units and label clarity.** Always label each probability (e.g., "PPV = 40%") rather than leaving a bare decimal.
> 6. **Skipping interpretation.** Numerical answers without a concluding sentence about clinical/public-health implications typically lose 1–2 marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Bayes' Theorem** allows us to reverse conditional probabilities: from $P(B \mid A)$ we obtain $P(A \mid B)$.
- The four **ingredients** of Bayes' Rule are: **Prior, Likelihood, Marginal, Posterior**.
- **Sensitivity and Specificity** are *test properties*, independent of prevalence.
- **PPV and NPV** are *population-dependent* — they vary with prevalence.
- **PPV formula:** $\text{PPV} = \dfrac{Se \cdot \text{Prev}}{Se \cdot \text{Prev} + (1 - Sp)(1 - \text{Prev})}$.
- **NPV formula:** $\text{NPV} = \dfrac{Sp \cdot (1 - \text{Prev})}{Sp \cdot (1 - \text{Prev}) + (1 - Se) \cdot \text{Prev}}$.
- **LR+ formula:** $LR+ = \dfrac{Se}{1 - Sp}$ (higher = better for confirming disease).
- **LR− formula:** $LR- = \dfrac{1 - Se}{Sp}$ (values $< 0.2$ are strong evidence *against* disease).
- **Base-rate fallacy:** Low prevalence $\Rightarrow$ Low PPV, even for excellent tests.
- **Total Probability Theorem** is the necessary denominator in all Bayes' calculations.
- The **2×2 contingency table** is the universal tool for organizing screening data.
- **Youden's J index** = $Se + Sp - 1$ measures global test quality; values $> 0.5$ indicate a strong test.
- **Mass screening** is only useful when prevalence is high *and* a confirmatory diagnostic test exists for follow-up of positives.
- Always **interpret results in plain language** — KTU examiners reward clinical/public-health insights, not just arithmetic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bayesian Inference
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT861 - BIOSTATISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Bayesian Inference

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bayesian Inference** is a branch of statistical inference in which the unknown parameter $\theta$ is treated as a **random variable** with a probability distribution (the *prior*), and observed data $D$ is used to update the belief about $\theta$ via **Bayes' Theorem**, yielding a *posterior distribution* $P(\theta \mid D)$. All inference — point estimation, interval estimation, and hypothesis testing — is then derived from this posterior.

The foundational mathematical identity governing the entire framework is:

$$
P(\theta \mid D) \;=\; \dfrac{P(D \mid \theta)\, P(\theta)}{P(D)}
$$

where the four canonical quantities are:

| Symbol | KTU Terminology | Role in Inference |
|:---|:---|:---|
| $P(\theta)$ | **Prior Distribution** | Belief *before* observing the data |
| $P(D \mid \theta)$ | **Likelihood Function** | Evidence the data carries about $\theta$ |
| $P(\theta \mid D)$ | **Posterior Distribution** | Updated belief *after* observing the data |
| $P(D)$ | **Marginal Likelihood / Evidence** | Normalising constant (sum/integral over $\theta$) |

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy — "The Doctor's Second Opinion"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: A Patient's Diagnosis Updates With a Test**

Imagine a patient walks into a clinic. The doctor, based on the patient's age, lifestyle, and visible symptoms, assigns a **prior probability** of, say, 10% that the patient has a particular disease. Now a diagnostic test is performed. The test is not perfect — it has a *sensitivity* (true positive rate) and a *specificity* (true negative rate). The **likelihood** of seeing this particular test result depends on whether the patient truly has the disease or not. After combining the prior with the likelihood via Bayes' Theorem, the doctor arrives at a **posterior probability** — the updated, rational belief that the patient has the disease.

**Key Insight:** Bayesian Inference is exactly this process of *rational belief updating* — it is the mathematically optimal way to combine **prior knowledge** with **new evidence**.

### 1.3 Why Bayesian Inference Matters in Biostatistics

In biomedical and public-health studies, we rarely have infinite samples. Bayesian methods:

- Allow the **incorporation of historical clinical knowledge** through informative priors.
- Produce **direct probability statements** about parameters (e.g., "*the probability that the treatment effect is positive is 0.94*").
- Yield **credible intervals** that have the intuitive interpretation students often *wish* confidence intervals had.
- Are well-suited for **small-sample medical studies**, where frequentist asymptotics break down.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Module 1 explicitly frames Bayesian Inference as the *subjectivist* alternative to classical (frequentist) inference. Be ready to contrast the two paradigms.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Building Blocks — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — The Parameter is a Random Variable.**
In frequentist statistics, $\theta$ is a fixed (but unknown) constant. In Bayesian statistics, $\theta$ has a probability distribution. This is the *fundamental philosophical shift*.

**Step 2 — Quantify Initial Belief with the Prior $P(\theta)$.**
Before seeing data, encode existing knowledge. Choices include:
- *Informative prior* — based on previous studies, expert opinion.
- *Non-informative prior* — e.g., uniform $U(0,1)$, or Jeffreys prior $\propto \sqrt{I(\theta)}$, where $I(\theta)$ is the Fisher information.
- *Conjugate prior* — chosen so the posterior belongs to the same family as the prior (computational convenience).

**Step 3 — Model the Data-Generating Mechanism via the Likelihood $P(D \mid \theta)$.**
The likelihood is the same function that appears in maximum likelihood estimation (MLE). It answers: "If $\theta$ were the true value, how plausible is the data I actually observed?"

**Step 4 — Update Belief with Bayes' Theorem.**
The data *updates* the prior multiplicatively. The unnormalised posterior is:
$$
P(\theta \mid D) \;\propto\; P(D \mid \theta)\, P(\theta)
$$
The proportionality constant $P(D)$ is computed as:
$$
P(D) \;=\; \int_{\Theta} P(D \mid \theta)\, P(\theta)\, d\theta
$$
which is often analytically intractable and is the central computational challenge in Bayesian methods (MCMC, variational inference, etc., are addressed in later modules).

**Step 5 — Perform Inference from the Posterior.**
- **Point estimate (Bayes estimator):** Posterior mean $\hat{\theta}_{Bayes} = E[\theta \mid D]$, or posterior median, or **MAP** (Maximum A Posteriori) $\hat{\theta}_{MAP} = \arg\max_{\theta} P(\theta \mid D)$.
- **Interval estimate:** A $100(1-\alpha)\%$ **credible interval** is any interval $[L, U]$ such that $P(L \le \theta \le U \mid D) = 1 - \alpha$. The *Highest Posterior Density (HPD)* interval is the shortest such interval.
- **Hypothesis test:** Compare posterior probabilities $P(\theta \in \Theta_0 \mid D)$ vs $P(\theta \in \Theta_0^c \mid D)$, or compute the **Bayes Factor** $BF_{01} = \dfrac{P(D \mid \theta \in \Theta_0)}{P(D \mid \theta \in \Theta_0^c)}$.

### 2.2 Conjugate Priors — The Workhorse of Analytical Bayesian Inference

> [!NOTE]
> A prior $P(\theta)$ is **conjugate** to a likelihood $P(D \mid \theta)$ if the posterior $P(\theta \mid D)$ belongs to the same distributional family as the prior. Conjugacy makes the posterior analytically tractable — no MCMC needed.

The table below summarises the most frequently tested conjugate families in KTU biostatistics examinations.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Likelihood $P(D \mid \theta)$ | Parameter | Conjugate Prior $P(\theta)$ | Posterior $P(\theta \mid D)$ | Posterior Hyperparameters |
|:--|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Binomial$(n, \theta)$ | Success prob. $\theta \in [0,1]$ | Beta$(\alpha, \beta)$ | Beta$(\alpha + x,\ \beta + n - x)$ | $\alpha' = \alpha + x$, $\beta' = \beta + n - x$ |
| 2 | Poisson$(\lambda)$ | Rate $\lambda > 0$ | Gamma$(\alpha, \beta)$ | Gamma$(\alpha + \sum x_i,\ \beta + n)$ | $\alpha' = \alpha + n\bar{x}$, $\beta' = \beta + n$ |
| 3 | Exponential$(\lambda)$ | Rate $\lambda > 0$ | Gamma$(\alpha, \beta)$ | Gamma$(\alpha + n,\ \beta + \sum x_i)$ | $\alpha' = \alpha + n$, $\beta' = \beta + n\bar{x}$ |
| 4 | Normal$(\mu, \sigma^2)$, $\sigma^2$ known | Mean $\mu$ | Normal$(\mu_0, \tau_0^2)$ | Normal$(\mu_n, \tau_n^2)$ | $\dfrac{1}{\tau_n^2} = \dfrac{1}{\tau_0^2} + \dfrac{n}{\sigma^2}$ |
| 5 | Bernoulli$(\theta)$ | Success prob. $\theta$ | Uniform$(0,1)$ $\equiv$ Beta$(1,1)$ | Beta$(1+x,\ 1+n-x)$ | Same as Row 1 with $\alpha=\beta=1$ |

**The Bayes Estimator Under Squared-Error Loss (Posterior Mean) for Beta-Binomial:**

$$
\hat{\theta}_{Bayes} \;=\; E[\theta \mid D] \;=\; \dfrac{\alpha + x}{\alpha + \beta + n}
$$

**The MAP Estimator for Beta-Binomial:**

$$
\hat{\theta}_{MAP} \;=\; \dfrac{\alpha + x - 1}{\alpha + \beta + n - 2} \quad (\text{valid when } \alpha+x>1,\ \beta+n-x>1)
$$

**The Bayes Factor for Two Simple Hypotheses $H_0: \theta = \theta_0$ vs $H_1: \theta = \theta_1$:**

$$
BF_{01} \;=\; \dfrac{P(D \mid \theta_0)}{P(D \mid \theta_1)}
$$

**The Jeffreys' Scale for Evidence Strength (Bayes Factor Interpretation):**

| $\log_{10} BF_{10}$ | Strength of Evidence for $H_1$ |
|:--:|:---|
| $0$ to $0.5$ | Barely worth mentioning |
| $0.5$ to $1$ | Substantial |
| $1$ to $2$ | Strong |
| $> 2$ | Decisive |

**Posterior Odds (Key Decision Rule):**

$$
\underbrace{\dfrac{P(H_1 \mid D)}{P(H_0 \mid D)}}_{\text{Posterior Odds}} \;=\; \underbrace{\dfrac{P(D \mid H_1)}{P(D \mid H_0)}}_{\text{Bayes Factor}} \;\times\; \underbrace{\dfrac{P(H_1)}{P(H_0)}}_{\text{Prior Odds}}
$$

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering & Biomedical Utility

- **Clinical Trials:** Bayesian adaptive designs (e.g., the famous *BATTLE trial* in lung cancer) use posteriors to allocate patients to the most promising treatment arm in real time.
- **Genomics & Disease Mapping:** Bayesian hierarchical models handle the multiple-testing problem in GWAS by shrinking noisy estimates toward a prior.
- **Epidemiology:** Posterior credible intervals for $R_0$ (basic reproduction number) integrate uncertainty from multiple noisy data sources.
- **Diagnostic Testing:** Direct computation of $P(\text{disease} \mid \text{positive test})$ from sensitivity, specificity, and disease prevalence — a problem virtually guaranteed to appear on a KTU exam.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Bayes' Theorem from the Definition of Conditional Probability

By the **definition of conditional probability**, for any two events $A$ and $B$ with $P(B) > 0$:

$$
P(A \mid B) \;=\; \dfrac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}
$$

**Step 1 — Symmetry of joint probability.** The joint probability $P(A \cap B)$ can be written two ways:

$$
P(A \cap B) \;=\; P(A \mid B)\, P(B) \;=\; P(B \mid A)\, P(A)
$$

**Step 2 — Equate and isolate.** Setting these equal and dividing by $P(B)$:

$$
P(A \mid B) \;=\; \dfrac{P(B \mid A)\, P(A)}{P(B)}
$$

**Step 3 — Generalise to a parameter space.** Let $A = \theta$ and $B = D$. Then:

$$
P(\theta \mid D) \;=\; \dfrac{P(D \mid \theta)\, P(\theta)}{P(D)}
$$

**Step 4 — Marginal likelihood via the Law of Total Probability.** $P(D)$ is the *evidence*, obtained by averaging the likelihood over the prior:

$$
P(D) \;=\; \int_{\Theta} P(D \mid \theta)\, P(\theta)\, d\theta \quad \text{(continuous case)}
$$

$$
P(D) \;=\; \sum_{i} P(D \mid \theta_i)\, P(\theta_i) \quad \text{(discrete case)}
$$

This completes the derivation of the **Bayes posterior**.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Diagnostic Test (Discreet Two-Outcome Likelihood)

> A screening test for a disease has **sensitivity** $P(T^+ \mid D^+) = 0.95$ and **specificity** $P(T^- \mid D^-) = 0.90$. The **prevalence** of the disease in the population is $P(D^+) = 0.02$. A randomly selected person tests positive. Find the probability that they actually have the disease.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the priors.**
$$
P(D^+) = 0.02, \qquad P(D^-) = 1 - 0.02 = 0.98
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the likelihoods.**
$$
P(T^+ \mid D^+) = 0.95, \qquad P(T^+ \mid D^-) = 1 - 0.90 = 0.10
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the evidence $P(T^+)$ using the law of total probability.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(T^+) &= P(T^+ \mid D^+)\, P(D^+) \;+\; P(T^+ \mid D^-)\, P(D^-) \\
&= (0.95)(0.02) \;+\; (0.10)(0.98) \\
&= 0.0190 \;+\; 0.0980 \\
&= 0.1170
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Bayes' Theorem.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(D^+ \mid T^+) &= \dfrac{P(T^+ \mid D^+)\, P(D^+)}{P(T^+)} \\
&= \dfrac{(0.95)(0.02)}{0.1170} \\
&= \dfrac{0.0190}{0.1170} \\
&\approx 0.1624
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Despite a positive test with 95% sensitivity, the patient has only about a **16.24%** chance of having the disease — because the disease is rare (prior = 2%) and the false-positive rate is non-trivial. This is the *base-rate fallacy* made quantitative.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Beta-Binomial Conjugate Model (Coin-Flipping)

> A biostatistician believes a new laboratory assay has a true positive rate $\theta$ that is likely *close to* 0.7. She encodes this belief with a Beta prior $\theta \sim \text{Beta}(7, 3)$, since the mean of Beta$(\alpha, \beta)$ is $\alpha/(\alpha + \beta) = 7/10 = 0.7$. She then runs the assay on $n = 20$ samples and observes $x = 15$ positive results. Find the posterior distribution, the Bayes estimator under squared-error loss, and a 95% credible interval.

**Step 1 — Write the prior.**

$$
\theta \sim \text{Beta}(7, 3) \;\Rightarrow\; P(\theta) \propto \theta^{7-1}(1-\theta)^{3-1} = \theta^{6}(1-\theta)^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Write the likelihood.** With $x = 15$ successes in $n = 20$ trials:

$$
P(D \mid \theta) \;=\; \binom{20}{15} \theta^{15} (1-\theta)^{5}
$$

**Step 3 — Form the unnormalised posterior.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\theta \mid D) &\propto P(D \mid \theta)\, P(\theta) \\
&\propto \theta^{15} (1-\theta)^{5} \cdot \theta^{6}(1-\theta)^{2} \\
&\propto \theta^{21} (1-\theta)^{7}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the kernel of a **Beta$(22, 8)$** distribution (recognising that $\alpha' = \alpha + x = 7 + 15 = 22$ and $\beta' = \beta + n - x = 3 + 5 = 8$).

**Step 4 — Write the normalised posterior.**

$$
\theta \mid D \;\sim\; \text{Beta}(22, 8)
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Bayes estimator (posterior mean).**

$$
\hat{\theta}_{Bayes} \;=\; E[\theta \mid D] \;=\; \dfrac{22}{22 + 8} \;=\; \dfrac{22}{30} \;\approx\; 0.7333
$$

For comparison, the MLE would be $\hat{\theta}_{MLE} = 15/20 = 0.75$, and the prior mean was $0.70$. The Bayes estimate is a **shrinkage** of the MLE toward the prior mean.

**Step 6 — Construct a 95% equal-tailed credible interval.**
For a Beta$(22, 8)$ distribution, the 2.5th and 97.5th percentiles are obtained from Beta tables or numerical inversion:

$$
\text{CI}_{95\%} \;=\; \left[\, \theta_{0.025},\ \theta_{0.975}\, \right] \;\approx\; [0.556,\ 0.876]
$$

**Interpretation:** We are 95% certain (under this prior and data) that the assay's true positive rate lies between roughly 55.6% and 87.6%.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Bayesian vs. Frequentist Inference

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is a *high-yield* KTU exam comparison. Examiners often ask for a 7-mark comparison of the two paradigms.

| Aspect | Frequentist Inference | Bayesian Inference |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Nature of $\theta$ | Fixed unknown constant | Random variable with distribution |
| Probability interpreted as | Long-run frequency | Degree of belief (subjective) |
| Role of prior | None (in classical form) | Central — encodes prior knowledge |
| Output of inference | Point estimate + $p$-value + CI | Full posterior distribution |
| Interval interpretation | $C\%$ of intervals contain $\theta$ over repetitions | $P(\theta \in \text{interval} \mid D) = C\%$ |
| Use of historical data | Must be re-collected / pooled | Naturally incorporated via prior |
| Small-sample performance | Asymptotics may fail | Stable with informative prior |
| Computational cost | Usually low | High (MCMC often required) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Bayesian Inference Pipeline — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The diagram below maps the **functional flow** of how raw data and prior belief combine to produce all forms of Bayesian inference output.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Prior Knowledge P of theta] --> C[Unnormalised Posterior]
    B[Observed Data D] --> D[Likelihood P of D given theta]
    D --> C
    C --> E[Normalising Constant P of D]
    E --> F[Normalised Posterior P of theta given D]
    F --> G[Point Estimate: Posterior Mean or MAP]
    F --> H[Interval Estimate: 95 percent Credible Interval]
    F --> I[Hypothesis Test: Posterior Odds or Bayes Factor]
    F --> J[Prediction: Posterior Predictive Distribution]
```

**Reading the architecture:**
- The **Prior** and the **Likelihood** are the *only two inputs*.
- The **unnormalised posterior** is the product of these two — this is the conceptual heart of Bayes.
- The **normalising constant** $P(D)$ is computed (analytically for conjugate models, numerically via MCMC otherwise).
- Once the normalised posterior exists, *all* downstream inference — point, interval, test, prediction — is derived from it.

### 4.2 Decision-Theoretic View of Bayesian Inference

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1[Input Layer]
        A1[Prior Distribution P of theta]
        A2[Sampling Model P of D given theta]
    end
    subgraph S2[Computational Core]
        B1[Bayes Theorem Application]
        B2[Posterior Distribution P of theta given D]
    end
    subgraph S3[Inference Output Layer]
        C1[Bayes Estimator]
        C2[Credible Interval]
        C3[Bayes Factor for Hypothesis Testing]
        C4[Posterior Predictive Check]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> C1
    B2 --> C2
    B2 --> C3
    B2 --> C4
```

### 4.3 Conjugate Updating Schematic (Beta-Binomial Case)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Prior Beta alpha beta] -->|Add x successes| PP[Updated Beta alpha+x, beta+n-x]
    D[n trials, x successes] -->|Likelihood| PP
    PP -->|Compute Mean| OUT1[Posterior Mean estimator]
    PP -->|Compute Quantiles| OUT2[Credible Interval]
    PP -->|Find Mode| OUT3[MAP Estimate]
```

This visual emphasises that the **only operation** required in a conjugate update is the addition of the data-augmented counts to the prior hyperparameters — a feature that makes these models a favourite for KTU problems.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**State Bayes' Theorem and explain the role of each term in the context of statistical inference.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Bayes' Theorem is stated as:

$$
P(\theta \mid D) \;=\; \dfrac{P(D \mid \theta)\, P(\theta)}{P(D)}
$$

The terms are:
1. **$P(\theta)$ — Prior:** Encodes the statistician's belief about $\theta$ before observing data (1 mark).
2. **$P(D \mid \theta)$ — Likelihood:** Probability of the observed data under parameter value $\theta$; the bridge between data and parameter (1 mark).
3. **$P(D)$ — Evidence / Marginal likelihood:** The normalising constant ensuring the posterior integrates to 1 (0.5 marks).
4. **$P(\theta \mid D)$ — Posterior:** The updated belief about $\theta$ after observing data — the basis for all Bayesian inference (0.5 marks).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between a *credible interval* and a *confidence interval*.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Credible Interval (Bayesian) | Confidence Interval (Frequentist) |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Definition | Interval $[L, U]$ s.t. $P(L \le \theta \le U \mid D) = 1 - \alpha$ | Random interval that contains the true fixed $\theta$ in $100(1-\alpha)\%$ of repeated samples |
| Interpretation | *Direct* probability statement about $\theta$ | Only the long-run frequency is meaningful; *not* a probability statement about $\theta$ for a given sample |
| Requires prior | Yes | No |

(1 mark each for definition, interpretation, and the prior-dependence distinction.)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – July 2024]*

**(a)** Derive Bayes' Theorem from the definition of conditional probability. Clearly state the role of the *law of total probability* in computing the normalising constant. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A diagnostic test for a disease has sensitivity 98% and specificity 96%. If 1% of the population has the disease, find the probability that a person testing positive actually has the disease. Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q.A(a):**

Starting from the conditional probability definition $P(A \mid B) = P(A \cap B) / P(B)$ (1 mark), we use the symmetry of the joint probability:

$$
P(A \cap B) = P(A \mid B) P(B) = P(B \mid A) P(A) \quad (2 \text{ marks})
$$

Equating and rearranging:

$$
P(A \mid B) = \dfrac{P(B \mid A)\, P(A)}{P(B)} \quad (1 \text{ mark})
$$

Re-labelling $A \to \theta$ and $B \to D$ gives Bayes' Theorem (1 mark). The normalising constant $P(D)$ in the denominator is computed using the **law of total probability**:

$$
P(D) = \sum_{i} P(D \mid \theta_i) P(\theta_i) \quad \text{(discrete)} \quad \text{or} \quad \int P(D \mid \theta) P(\theta) d\theta \quad \text{(continuous)} \quad (2 \text{ marks})
$$

This is the key quantity that makes the posterior a proper probability distribution integrating to 1.

---

**Solution to Q.A(b):**

Let $D^+$ = disease present, $T^+$ = test positive.
Given: $P(T^+ \mid D^+) = 0.98$, $P(T^- \mid D^-) = 0.96$ ⟹ $P(T^+ \mid D^-) = 0.04$, $P(D^+) = 0.01$, $P(D^-) = 0.99$.

**Compute the evidence** (1 mark):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(T^+) &= P(T^+ \mid D^+) P(D^+) + P(T^+ \mid D^-) P(D^-) \\
&= (0.98)(0.01) + (0.04)(0.99) \\
&= 0.0098 + 0.0396 = 0.0494 \quad (3 \text{ marks})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Apply Bayes' Theorem** (2 marks):

$$
P(D^+ \mid T^+) = \dfrac{(0.98)(0.01)}{0.0494} = \dfrac{0.0098}{0.0494} \approx 0.1984
$$

**Comment** (1 mark): Despite the test's 98% sensitivity, the posterior probability of disease given a positive result is only about 19.8%, because the disease is rare (1% prevalence) and the false-positive rate (4%) is non-negligible relative to the prior. This illustrates the **base-rate fallacy** and underscores why Bayesian updating is essential in clinical decision-making.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – July 2024]*

**(a)** Define a *conjugate prior*. For the Binomial$(n, \theta)$ likelihood, show that the Beta$(\alpha, \beta)$ prior yields a Beta$(\alpha + x, \beta + n - x)$ posterior. Hence derive the Bayes estimator under squared-error loss. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A clinician believes the cure rate $\theta$ of a new drug is around 0.6, modelled as $\theta \sim \text{Beta}(6, 4)$. In a trial of 10 patients, 7 are cured. Compute the posterior distribution, the Bayes estimate of $\theta$, and a 95% credible interval using the Beta$(13, 7)$ quantiles $[0.487, 0.927]$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q.B(a):**

A prior $P(\theta)$ is **conjugate** to a likelihood $P(D \mid \theta)$ if the posterior $P(\theta \mid D)$ belongs to the same parametric family as the prior. Conjugacy makes the posterior analytically tractable (1 mark).

For Binomial$(n, \theta)$ with $x$ successes:

$$
P(D \mid \theta) = \binom{n}{x} \theta^{x} (1-\theta)^{n-x} \quad (1 \text{ mark})
$$

Beta$(\alpha, \beta)$ prior: $P(\theta) \propto \theta^{\alpha-1}(1-\theta)^{\beta-1}$ (1 mark).

Unnormalised posterior:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\theta \mid D) &\propto \theta^{x}(1-\theta)^{n-x} \cdot \theta^{\alpha-1}(1-\theta)^{\beta-1} \\
&= \theta^{\alpha + x - 1}(1-\theta)^{\beta + n - x - 1} \quad (2 \text{ marks})
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the kernel of $\text{Beta}(\alpha + x,\ \beta + n - x)$ (1 mark).

**Bayes estimator** (posterior mean under squared-error loss):

$$
\hat{\theta}_{Bayes} = E[\theta \mid D] = \dfrac{\alpha + x}{\alpha + \beta + n} \quad (1 \text{ mark})
$$

---

**Solution to Q.B(b):**

With prior Beta$(6, 4)$, $n = 10$, $x = 7$:

**Posterior hyperparameters** (1 mark):
$$
\alpha' = 6 + 7 = 13, \qquad \beta' = 4 + (10 - 7) = 7
$$
So $\theta \mid D \sim \text{Beta}(13, 7)$.

**Bayes estimate** (posterior mean) (2 marks):
$$
\hat{\theta}_{Bayes} = \dfrac{13}{13 + 7} = \dfrac{13}{20} = 0.65
$$

**Posterior variance** (1 mark, optional but adds value):
$$
\text{Var}[\theta \mid D] = \dfrac{\alpha' \beta'}{(\alpha' + \beta')^2(\alpha' + \beta' + 1)} = \dfrac{13 \cdot 7}{20^2 \cdot 21} \approx 0.0108
$$

**95% Credible interval** (using given Beta$(13, 7)$ quantiles) (2 marks):
$$
\text{CI}_{95\%} = [0.487,\ 0.927]
$$

**Interpretation** (1 mark): The Bayes estimate (0.65) lies between the prior mean (0.60) and the MLE (0.70), confirming the *shrinkage* effect of the prior. The 95% credible interval is wider than a frequentist 95% CI would typically be at this small sample size, reflecting the appropriate expression of uncertainty.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing prior with likelihood.** Many students write $P(\theta \mid D)$ where the question asks for $P(D \mid \theta)$. The prior is *about* the parameter; the likelihood is *about* the data given the parameter.
> 2. **Forgetting the normalising constant in the Beta-Binomial update.** You must show the step where the unnormalised kernel is *recognised* as a Beta kernel — partial credit is given only if you explicitly identify $\alpha' = \alpha + x$.
> 3. **Mixing up credible and confidence intervals.** A credible interval is a *direct* probability statement about $\theta$ given the data; a confidence interval is a *long-run frequency* statement. Examiners will deduct marks for conflating the two.
> 4. **Computational errors in the evidence calculation.** Forgetting one of the two terms in the law of total probability (e.g., the $P(T^+ \mid D^-) P(D^-)$ term) is the most common arithmetic mistake.
> 5. **Not specifying the loss function for a Bayes estimator.** The posterior mean minimises squared-error loss; the posterior median minimises absolute-error loss; the MAP minimises 0–1 loss. Examiners want you to name the loss.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Bayesian Inference**

- **Bayes' Theorem** $P(\theta \mid D) = P(D \mid \theta) P(\theta) / P(D)$ is the single most important formula in the module.
- **Posterior $\propto$ Likelihood $\times$ Prior** — the proportionality is the workhorse identity; the normalising constant is often omitted in derivations and reintroduced at the end.
- **Four key terms:** Prior, Likelihood, Posterior, Evidence — know their roles and units.
- **Conjugate priors** make posterior computation analytical; the **Beta-Binomial** family is the most tested in biostatistics.
- **Bayes estimator** = posterior mean under squared-error loss: $E[\theta \mid D]$.
- **MAP estimator** = mode of the posterior; for Beta$(\alpha, \beta)$ it is $(\alpha + x - 1)/(\alpha + \beta + n - 2)$.
- **Credible interval** = direct probability interval for $\theta$; *not* the same as a confidence interval.
- **Bayes Factor** = $P(D \mid H_1)/P(D \mid H_0)$; combined with prior odds via Bayes' rule to yield posterior odds.
- **Jeffreys' prior** $\propto \sqrt{I(\theta)}$ is the standard non-informative prior in one-parameter problems.
- **Posterior predictive** $P(D_{new} \mid D) = \int P(D_{new} \mid \theta) P(\theta \mid D) d\theta$ is the proper Bayesian way to predict new observations.
- **Diagnostic testing** is a *guaranteed* exam question — practice the four-step Bayes update with sensitivity, specificity, and prevalence.
- **Bayesian vs. Frequentist** comparison is a high-yield 7-mark question — know the philosophical, mathematical, and practical differences.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ROC Curves
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT861 - BIOSTATISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** ROC Curves

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ROC Curve (Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve):** A graphical plot that illustrates the diagnostic ability of a binary classifier system as its discrimination threshold is varied. The curve is created by plotting the **True Positive Rate (TPR)** — also called **Sensitivity** or **Recall** — on the Y-axis against the **False Positive Rate (FPR)** — equal to **1 − Specificity** — on the X-axis, at various threshold settings.

In the KTU 2024 Biostatistics syllabus, ROC analysis is positioned as a **discriminatory performance metric** used to evaluate the validity of a diagnostic test against a gold standard. It is a non-parametric method, meaning it does **not** assume any specific underlying probability distribution of the test results.

> [!NOTE]
> **Origin of the Name:** The term *Receiver Operating Characteristic* originated in **World War II (1940s)** during radar signal detection research. Engineers needed to distinguish enemy aircraft (signal) from noise. The "Receiver" refers to the radar operator, and the "Operating Characteristic" describes how the receiver's detection ability changed with judgment criteria. The technique was later adopted into psychology, radiology, and eventually medical diagnostics and machine learning.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Bouncer at a Club

Imagine a **bouncer (classifier)** at the entrance of an exclusive club trying to decide who to let in.

- The bouncer's job: identify VIP guests (positives) and turn away gate-crashers (negatives).
- The bouncer has a **strictness slider** (threshold): he can be very strict (only let in people he is 100% sure are VIPs) or very lenient (let almost everyone in).
- The ROC curve is essentially a **report card** showing how well the bouncer performs across ALL possible strictness settings.
- A perfect bouncer would have a curve that goes straight up the Y-axis and then straight right — capturing all VIPs and turning away all gate-crashers.
- A useless bouncer (random guessing) would have a diagonal line at 45°, equivalent to flipping a coin.

## 1.3 Core Performance Metrics

The following four quantities form the foundation of ROC analysis. They are derived from a **Confusion Matrix** (also called a *Cross-Tabulation* of test results versus true disease status):

| Quantity | Symbol | Definition |
|----------|--------|------------|
| True Positives | **TP** | Diseased subjects correctly identified as positive |
| False Negatives | **FN** | Diseased subjects incorrectly identified as negative |
| False Positives | **FP** | Non-diseased subjects incorrectly identified as positive |
| True Negatives | **TN** | Non-diseased subjects correctly identified as negative |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sensitivity** and **Specificity** are the two cardinal metrics of any diagnostic test:
> - **Sensitivity (Sn)** = $\frac{TP}{TP+FN}$ — the ability to correctly identify those **with** the disease.
> - **Specificity (Sp)** = $\frac{TN}{TN+FP}$ — the ability to correctly identify those **without** the disease.

A test that achieves **Sn = 1.0 and Sp = 1.0** simultaneously is the **ideal test** and corresponds to a single point at the top-left corner of the ROC plot: coordinates (FPR=0, TPR=1).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constructing a ROC Curve — Step-by-Step Logic

The construction of a ROC curve follows a strict algorithmic flow:

1. **Obtain a continuous (or ordinal) diagnostic test result** for every subject in a validation study. Examples: blood pressure, cholesterol level, optical density in ELISA, gene expression score.
2. **Verify the true disease status** of every subject using a "gold standard" reference test (biopsy, PCR, MRI, etc.).
3. **Sort all test results in descending order** (largest to smallest). The ROC is built by sliding a threshold $\tau$ from the largest value to the smallest.
4. **For each candidate threshold $\tau$:**
   - Classify every subject with a test result $\geq \tau$ as **Test Positive**.
   - Classify every subject with a test result $< \tau$ as **Test Negative**.
   - Compute TPR and FPR for that specific threshold.
5. **Plot (FPR, TPR) pairs** as points on a 2-D graph. Connect consecutive points with straight line segments.
6. The result is a piecewise-linear, monotonically non-decreasing curve in the unit square $[0,1] \times [0,1]$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why descending order?** In most medical tests, *higher* readings indicate *more* disease. By starting at the highest value, we begin in a state where we classify NO ONE as positive (TPR=0, FPR=0 — bottom-left corner). As we lower the threshold, we progressively flip subjects from "negative" to "positive", so both TPR and FPR only increase.

## 2.2 The AUC — Area Under the ROC Curve

The single most important summary statistic of a ROC curve is the **AUC (Area Under the Curve)**, sometimes denoted $A_z$ in radiology literature.

**Theoretical Bounds:**
- **AUC = 1.0** → Perfect discrimination. The test separates diseased from non-diseased with zero overlap.
- **AUC = 0.5** → No discrimination. Equivalent to a random guess (the diagonal "chance line" from (0,0) to (1,1)).
- **AUC < 0.5** → Worse than chance. The test is systematically inverted (can be corrected by swapping the disease label).

**Statistical Interpretation of AUC (Green & Swets, 1966):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The probability statement of AUC:** The AUC of a diagnostic test equals the probability that, when you randomly pick one diseased subject and one non-diseased subject, the test will assign a **higher score** to the diseased subject than to the non-diseased one. Formally:
> $$\text{AUC} = P\bigl(S(D^+) > S(D^-)\bigr)$$
> where $S(D^+)$ is the test score of a randomly chosen diseased individual and $S(D^-)$ is the test score of a randomly chosen non-diseased individual.

This makes AUC a genuine **effect size** comparable across studies and populations.

## 2.3 Youden's Index — The Optimal Cutoff

When clinicians need a **single operating threshold** to convert the continuous test into a binary call, the **Youden's J Statistic** is used:

$$J = \text{Sensitivity} + \text{Specificity} - 1 = \text{TPR} - \text{FPR}$$

The threshold $\tau^*$ that **maximizes J** is the optimal cut-off. On the ROC plot, this corresponds to the point on the curve that is **geometrically farthest** from the diagonal chance line (perpendicular distance).

> [!TIP]
> **When to use Youden's Index:** It is most appropriate when **false positives and false negatives carry equal weight** (i.e., the cost of missing a case equals the cost of a false alarm). In screening programs where sensitivity is preferred, the threshold is shifted left (lower); in confirmatory diagnostics where specificity is preferred, the threshold is shifted right (higher).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Quantity | Mathematical Expression | Range / Units | Engineering / Medical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| True Positive Rate (Sensitivity) | $\text{TPR} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | $[0, 1]$, dimensionless | Diagnostic yield of a screening test |
| False Positive Rate | $\text{FPR} = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN} = 1 - \text{Sp}$ | $[0, 1]$, dimensionless | False alarm rate |
| True Negative Rate (Specificity) | $\text{TNR} = \dfrac{TN}{TN + FP}$ | $[0, 1]$, dimensionless | Confirmatory test power |
| Positive Predictive Value | $\text{PPV} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Probability disease is present given a positive test |
| Negative Predictive Value | $\text{NPV} = \dfrac{TN}{TN + FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Probability disease is absent given a negative test |
| Youden's J Index | $J = \text{Sn} + \text{Sp} - 1$ | $[-1, 1]$ | Optimal cut-off selection |
| Diagnostic Odds Ratio | $\text{DOR} = \dfrac{TP \cdot TN}{FP \cdot FN}$ | $[0, \infty)$ | Combined test performance |
| AUC (Trapezoidal) | $\text{AUC} = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \dfrac{(TPR_{i+1}+TPR_i)(FPR_{i+1}-FPR_i)}{2}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Global discrimination index |
| Likelihood Ratio (+) | $LR^+ = \dfrac{Sn}{1-Sp}$ | $[1, \infty)$ | Post-test odds multiplier (positive) |
| Likelihood Ratio (−) | $LR^- = \dfrac{1-Sn}{Sp}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Post-test odds multiplier (negative) |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering & Medical Utility

- **Radiology & Imaging:** Comparing the accuracy of CT vs. MRI vs. ultrasound in tumor detection.
- **Clinical Chemistry:** Establishing cut-offs for troponin in myocardial infarction diagnosis.
- **Machine Learning & AI:** Selecting classification models (logistic regression, SVM, neural networks) for medical decision support.
- **Epidemiology & Public Health:** Evaluating rapid antigen tests, ELISA kits for HIV, dengue, COVID-19.
- **Bioinformatics:** Assessing gene-expression-based diagnostic signatures for cancer subtyping.
- **Quality Control:** ROC methodology is also used in radar, psychology psychophysics, and credit-scoring fraud detection — but the KTU syllabus emphasizes biostatistical applications.
