```mermaid
    C --> C2["Independence NOT required"]
    D --> D1["Var X = E(X squared) minus mu squared"]
    E --> E1["E(X) = M prime of X at zero"]
    F --> F1["E(X) = E of E(X given Y)"]
```

### 4.3 Hierarchical Relationship: Moments Built on Expectation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Moment Generating Function M_X of t"] --> B["M prime at 0"]
    A --> C["M double prime at 0"]
    A --> D["M triple prime at 0"]
    B --> E["E(X) = First Raw Moment"]
    C --> F["E(X squared) = Second Raw Moment"]
    D --> G["E(X cubed) = Third Raw Moment"]
    E --> H["Mean mu"]
    F --> I["Variance = E(X squared) minus mu squared"]
    G --> J["Skewness numerator"]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram: From Definition to Application in Biostatistics

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Random Experiment or Clinical Trial"] --> B["Define Random Variable X"]
    B --> C{"Distribution Type?"}
    C -->|Discrete| D["Identify PMF P of x"]
    C -->|Continuous| E["Identify PDF f of x"]
    D --> F["Apply Discrete E Formula"]
    E --> G["Apply Continuous E Formula"]
    F --> H["Compute E of X"]
    G --> H
    H --> I["Compute Higher Moments"]
    I --> J["Derive Variance SD Skewness"]
    J --> K["Clinical Interpretation and Decision Making"]
```

> **Note on Diagram Use:** These block-level architecture diagrams emphasize the *process flow* and *logical dependencies* central to KTU 2024 Scheme problem-solving, where students are expected to identify the correct path from a defined random variable to the final statistical decision.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the expected value of a discrete random variable. State and explain the linearity property of expectation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** For a discrete random variable $X$ with values $x_1, x_2, \ldots$ and probability mass function $P(X = x_i)$, the expected value is:

$$\mathbb{E}[X] = \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} x_i \cdot P(X = x_i)$$

provided $\sum \vert x_i \vert \cdot P(X = x_i) < \infty$.

**Linearity Property (1 mark):** For constants $a, b$ and random variable $X$:
$$\mathbb{E}[aX + b] = a\,\mathbb{E}[X] + b$$
This property is fundamental because it allows us to compute expectations of complex linear combinations without computing the full distribution, and crucially, it does not require any assumption of independence.

#### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State and prove the variance identity $\text{Var}(X) = \mathbb{E}[X^2] - (\mathbb{E}[X])^2$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Let $\mu = \mathbb{E}[X]$. By definition, $\text{Var}(X) = \mathbb{E}[(X-\mu)^2]$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Var}(X) &= \mathbb{E}[(X-\mu)^2] \\
&= \mathbb{E}[X^2 - 2\mu X + \mu^2] \\
&= \mathbb{E}[X^2] - 2\mu\,\mathbb{E}[X] + \mu^2 \\
&= \mathbb{E}[X^2] - 2\mu^2 + \mu^2 \\
&= \mathbb{E}[X^2] - \mu^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence $\text{Var}(X) = \mathbb{E}[X^2] - (\mathbb{E}[X])^2$. This is the **computational formula** for variance used in nearly all KTU numerical problems.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]` (14 Marks)

**(a)** A discrete random variable $X$ has the probability distribution:

| $X$ | $-2$ | $-1$ | $0$ | $1$ | $2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $P(X)$ | $0.1$ | $0.2$ | $0.3$ | $0.3$ | $0.1$ |

Compute (i) $\mathbb{E}[X]$, (ii) $\mathbb{E}[X^2]$, (iii) $\text{Var}(X)$, (iv) $\mathbb{E}[3X - 5]$. (7 marks)

**(b)** Let $X \sim \text{Uniform}(0, 4)$. Compute $\mathbb{E}[X]$, $\mathbb{E}[X^2]$, and $\text{Var}(X)$. Verify the result using the variance identity. (7 marks)

##### Model Solution for Part (a) — (7 marks)

**(i) Computation of $\mathbb{E}[X]$ (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}[X] &= \sum x_i P(x_i) \\
&= (-2)(0.1) + (-1)(0.2) + (0)(0.3) + (1)(0.3) + (2)(0.1) \\
&= -0.2 - 0.2 + 0 + 0.3 + 0.2 \\
&= 0.1
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Computation of $\mathbb{E}[X^2]$ (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}[X^2] &= \sum x_i^2 P(x_i) \\
&= (4)(0.1) + (1)(0.2) + (0)(0.3) + (1)(0.3) + (4)(0.1) \\
&= 0.4 + 0.2 + 0 + 0.3 + 0.4 \\
&= 1.3
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Computation of $\text{Var}(X)$ (1 mark):**

$$\text{Var}(X) = \mathbb{E}[X^2] - (\mathbb{E}[X])^2 = 1.3 - (0.1)^2 = 1.3 - 0.01 = 1.29$$

**(iv) Computation of $\mathbb{E}[3X - 5]$ (2 marks):**

By linearity, $\mathbb{E}[3X - 5] = 3\mathbb{E}[X] - 5 = 3(0.1) - 5 = 0.3 - 5 = -4.7$.

##### Model Solution for Part (b) — (7 marks)

For $X \sim U(0, 4)$, the pdf is $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{4-0} = \dfrac{1}{4}$ for $0 \leq x \leq 4$.

**(i) Computation of $\mathbb{E}[X]$ (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}[X] &= \int_0^4 x \cdot \frac{1}{4} \, dx \\
&= \frac{1}{4} \cdot \left[ \frac{x^2}{2} \right]_0^4 \\
&= \frac{1}{4} \cdot \frac{16}{2} = \frac{1}{4} \cdot 8 = 2
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Computation of $\mathbb{E}[X^2]$ (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}[X^2] &= \int_0^4 x^2 \cdot \frac{1}{4} \, dx \\
&= \frac{1}{4} \cdot \left[ \frac{x^3}{3} \right]_0^4 \\
&= \frac{1}{4} \cdot \frac{64}{3} = \frac{16}{3} \approx 5.333
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Verification of Variance Identity (3 marks):**

$$\text{Var}(X) = \mathbb{E}[X^2] - (\mathbb{E}[X])^2 = \frac{16}{3} - (2)^2 = \frac{16}{3} - 4 = \frac{16 - 12}{3} = \frac{4}{3} \approx 1.333$$

Cross-check via definition:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Var}(X) &= \int_0^4 (x - 2)^2 \cdot \frac{1}{4} \, dx \\
&= \frac{1}{4} \left[ \frac{(x-2)^3}{3} \right]_0^4 \\
&= \frac{1}{4} \left[ \frac{8}{3} - \frac{-8}{3} \right] = \frac{1}{4} \cdot \frac{16}{3} = \frac{4}{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

Both methods give $\dfrac{4}{3}$, confirming the variance identity. $\blacksquare$

---

#### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]` (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** The number of patients arriving at a hospital emergency room per hour follows a Poisson distribution with parameter $\lambda = 3$. Compute (i) $\mathbb{E}[X]$, (ii) $\text{Var}(X)$, (iii) the expected value of $Y = 2X^2 + 5X - 1$. (7 marks)

**(b)** A continuous random variable $X$ has pdf $f(x) = \dfrac{3x^2}{8}$ for $0 \leq x \leq 2$, and 0 otherwise. Verify that this is a valid pdf. Compute $\mathbb{E}[X]$, $\mathbb{E}[X^2]$, and $\text{Var}(X)$. (7 marks)

##### Model Solution for Part (a) — (7 marks)

For $X \sim \text{Poisson}(\lambda = 3)$: $\mathbb{E}[X] = \lambda = 3$ and $\text{Var}(X) = \lambda = 3$. (These are well-known results, but they can be derived from the pmf $P(X=k) = \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^k}{k!}$.)

**(i) $\mathbb{E}[X] = 3$** (1 mark)

**(ii) $\text{Var}(X) = 3$** (1 mark)

**(iii) Computation of $\mathbb{E}[2X^2 + 5X - 1]$ (5 marks):**

By linearity, $\mathbb{E}[2X^2 + 5X - 1] = 2\mathbb{E}[X^2] + 5\mathbb{E}[X] - 1$.

We need $\mathbb{E}[X^2]$. From the variance identity:
$$\mathbb{E}[X^2] = \text{Var}(X) + (\mathbb{E}[X])^2 = 3 + 9 = 12$$

Therefore:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}[2X^2 + 5X - 1] &= 2(12) + 5(3) - 1 \\
&= 24 + 15 - 1 \\
&= 38
\end{aligned}
$$

##### Model Solution for Part (b) — (7 marks)

**Verification of Valid PDF (2 marks):**

- Non-negativity: $f(x) = \frac{3x^2}{8} \geq 0$ for $0 \leq x \leq 2$. ✓
- Total probability:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_0^2 f(x) \, dx &= \int_0^2 \frac{3x^2}{8} \, dx = \frac{3}{8} \left[ \frac{x^3}{3} \right]_0^2 = \frac{3}{8} \cdot \frac{8}{3} = 1 \quad ✓
\end{aligned}
$$

**Computation of $\mathbb{E}[X]$ (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}[X] &= \int_0^2 x \cdot \frac{3x^2}{8} \, dx = \int_0^2 \frac{3x^3}{8} \, dx = \frac{3}{8} \left[ \frac{x^4}{4} \right]_0^2 = \frac{3}{8} \cdot \frac{16}{4} = \frac{3}{8} \cdot 4 = \frac{3}{2} = 1.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Computation of $\mathbb{E}[X^2]$ (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}[X^2] &= \int_0^2 x^2 \cdot \frac{3x^2}{8} \, dx = \int_0^2 \frac{3x^4}{8} \, dx = \frac{3}{8} \left[ \frac{x^5}{5} \right]_0^2 = \frac{3}{8} \cdot \frac{32}{5} = \frac{96}{40} = \frac{12}{5} = 2.4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Computation of $\text{Var}(X)$ (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Var}(X) = \mathbb{E}[X^2] - (\mathbb{E}[X])^2 = 2.4 - (1.5)^2 = 2.4 - 2.25 = 0.15
\end{aligned}
$$

$\blacksquare$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones (from KTU Board Valuation Keys):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to verify $\sum P(x_i) = 1$** before computing expectation. If the probabilities don't sum to 1, the entire answer is wrong. **Always start a discrete problem by summing the pmf column.**
> 2. **Confusing $\mathbb{E}[X^2]$ with $(\mathbb{E}[X])^2$.** These are fundamentally different. The variance identity is the *only* correct relationship: $\text{Var}(X) = \mathbb{E}[X^2] - (\mathbb{E}[X])^2$. Many students incorrectly write $\text{Var}(X) = \mathbb{E}[X^2]$ and lose 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Assuming independence for the sum.** Additivity $\mathbb{E}[X+Y] = \mathbb{E}[X] + \mathbb{E}[Y]$ requires *no* assumption. But multiplicativity $\mathbb{E}[XY] = \mathbb{E}[X]\mathbb{E}[Y]$ requires independence. Mixing these up costs 1–2 marks in conceptual questions.
> 4. **Forgetting the absolute convergence condition.** When asked to "state the conditions for existence of expectation," the formal answer *must* include $\mathbb{E}[\vert X \vert] < \infty$. The Cauchy distribution is the classic counterexample.
> 5. **Sign errors in linearity problems.** When applying $\mathbb{E}[aX+b] = a\mu + b$, watch the sign of $b$. A negative $b$ can become a positive error if arithmetic is rushed.
> 6. **Using $\mu$ as both population and sample mean.** In biostatistics, $\mu$ is the population parameter; $\bar{x}$ is the sample estimator. Use the correct symbol based on the context.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition (Discrete):** $\mathbb{E}[X] = \sum x_i P(x_i)$ — sum of value × probability.
- **Definition (Continuous):** $\mathbb{E}[X] = \int x f(x) \, dx$ — integral of value × density.
- **Existence condition:** $\mathbb{E}[\vert X \vert] < \infty$ is mandatory; otherwise the expectation is undefined (e.g., Cauchy distribution).
- **Linearity:** $\mathbb{E}[aX + b] = a\mathbb{E}[X] + b$ — *always* true, no assumptions needed.
- **Additivity:** $\mathbb{E}[X+Y] = \mathbb{E}[X] + \mathbb{E}[Y]$ — *always* true, even without independence.
- **Multiplicativity:** $\mathbb{E}[XY] = \mathbb{E}[X]\mathbb{E}[Y]$ — only true if $X$ and $Y$ are independent.
- **Variance identity:** $\text{Var}(X) = \mathbb{E}[X^2] - (\mathbb{E}[X])^2$ — the computational shortcut.
- **Constant rule:** $\mathbb{E}[c] = c$ for any constant $c$ (constants have zero variance).
- **MGF bridge:** $\mathbb{E}[X^n] = M_X^{(n)}(0)$ — the $n$-th derivative of the MGF at zero.
- **Indicator trick:** For any event $A$, let $I_A = 1$ if $A$ occurs, else $0$. Then $\mathbb{E}[I_A] = P(A)$. Extremely useful for proving probability identities.
- **Law of Total Expectation:** $\mathbb{E}[X] = \mathbb{E}[\mathbb{E}[X \vert Y]]$ — bridges marginal and conditional distributions.
- **Standard results to memorize:** $\mathbb{E}[\text{Bernoulli}(p)] = p$; $\mathbb{E}[\text{Binomial}(n,p)] = np$; $\mathbb{E}[\text{Poisson}(\lambda)] = \lambda$; $\mathbb{E}[U(a,b)] = \frac{a+b}{2}$; $\mathbb{E}[\text{Exp}(\lambda)] = \frac{1}{\lambda}$.
- **Biostatistics connection:** Expected value underpins hypothesis testing, confidence intervals, regression coefficients, survival analysis, and pharmacokinetic modeling.
- **Always verify the support** of the random variable before integration. The limits of integration must match the support exactly; otherwise the result is mathematically meaningless.
- **Discrete vs Continuous trap:** A binomial random variable is *discrete* (sum of Bernoullis), not continuous, even when $n$ is large. Do not use the integral formula on it.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Variance and Cumulative Distribution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT861 - BIOSTATISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Variance and Cumulative Distribution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Variance — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Variance** of a random variable $X$ is the **second central moment** about the mean. It quantifies the **average squared deviation** of observations from the expected value (mean), and is the most widely used measure of **dispersion** in biostatistics and probability theory.

Mathematically, the variance of a discrete random variable $X$ is defined as:

$$V(X) = \sigma^{2} = E\left[ (X - \mu)^{2} \right] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_{i} - \mu)^{2} \cdot P(x_{i})$$

And for a continuous random variable $X$:

$$V(X) = \sigma^{2} = E\left[ (X - \mu)^{2} \right] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} (x - \mu)^{2} \cdot f(x) \, dx$$

Where:
- $\mu = E(X)$ is the **expected value** (mean)
- $P(x_{i})$ is the probability mass function (discrete case)
- $f(x)$ is the probability density function (continuous case)
- The **unit of variance** is the **square** of the unit of $X$ (e.g., $\text{mg}^{2}$, $\text{cm}^{2}$)

The **positive square root** of variance is called the **Standard Deviation** ($\sigma$), and is expressed in the same units as $X$, making it more interpretable in biomedical contexts.

---

### 1.2 Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF)** of a random variable $X$, denoted $F_{X}(x)$, is the probability that $X$ takes a value **less than or equal to** a given real number $x$. It is the **integrated (or summed) form** of the probability distribution.

The CDF is defined as:

$$F_{X}(x) = P(X \leq x)$$

- For a **discrete** random variable:

$$F_{X}(x) = P(X \leq x) = \sum_{x_{i} \leq x} P(X = x_{i})$$

- For a **continuous** random variable:

$$F_{X}(x) = P(X \leq x) = \int_{-\infty}^{x} f(t) \, dt$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Biostatistics students must master both forms, as clinical trial data often arrives as **discrete counts** (e.g., number of relapses) while physiological measurements (e.g., blood pressure, BMI) are **continuous** variables.

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Variance — The "Spread" Metaphor:**
Imagine two groups of 10 diabetic patients, each with a **mean blood glucose** of $140 \, \text{mg/dL}$. In Group A, every patient hovers around $138$–$142$; in Group B, values scatter wildly from $90$ to $200$. The **mean is identical**, but the **clinical risk is vastly different**. **Variance captures this "scatter"** — a single number that tells you how tightly or loosely data is clustered around the mean.

**CDF — The "Running Total" Metaphor:**
Think of a hospital's daily patient admission register. The CDF is like a **cumulative tally** — at 8 AM, 5 patients have arrived; by 10 AM, 12 cumulative; by noon, 25 cumulative. The CDF is this running cumulative probability. Reading $F(120) = 0.85$ means *"85% of the population has a value at or below 120."*

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid:** **C**DF = **C**umulative (accumulating) and **C**omplementary relationship with survival function $S(x) = 1 - F(x)$.

---

### 1.4 Notation Standard (KTU Convention)

| Symbol | Meaning | KTU Convention |
| --- | --- | --- |
| $\mu$ | Population mean | Greek letter "mu" |
| $\sigma^{2}$ | Population variance | Greek "sigma squared" |
| $\sigma$ | Standard deviation | Greek "sigma" |
| $F(x)$ | Cumulative distribution function | Capital $F$ |
| $f(x)$ | Probability density/mass function | Lowercase $f$ |
| $E(X)$ | Expectation of $X$ | Operator $E$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Variance — Theoretical Breakdown

The variance is built upon the **concept of expectation**. Let us dissect it logically:

- **Step 1: Compute the Mean** — Find $\mu = E(X)$, the central tendency.
- **Step 2: Measure Deviations** — For each value $x_{i}$, compute $(x_{i} - \mu)$, the deviation from the mean.
- **Step 3: Square the Deviations** — Squaring eliminates the sign (positive/negative deviations both contribute positively) and **penalizes large deviations more heavily** (a $10$-unit deviation contributes $100$, not $10$).
- **Step 4: Average the Squared Deviations** — Weighted by probabilities in the random-variable case, or simply averaged in the sample case.

#### Alternative Computational Formula (The KTU Shortcut)

Computing $E[(X-\mu)^{2}]$ directly is tedious. Using the algebraic identity:

$$E[(X - \mu)^{2}] = E(X^{2}) - [E(X)]^{2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most-used formula in KTU board exams** because $E(X^{2})$ is typically easier to derive from a given PMF/PDF than computing deviations first.

**Proof Sketch:**

$$E[(X-\mu)^{2}] = E[X^{2} - 2\mu X + \mu^{2}] = E(X^{2}) - 2\mu E(X) + \mu^{2}$$
$$= E(X^{2}) - 2\mu^{2} + \mu^{2} = E(X^{2}) - \mu^{2} = E(X^{2}) - [E(X)]^{2}$$

---

### 2.2 Properties of Variance (KTU Frequently Tested)

For random variables $X$ and $Y$ and constants $a, b \in \mathbb{R}$:

- **P1:** $V(a) = 0$ (constant has zero variance)
- **P2:** $V(aX) = a^{2} V(X)$ (scalar multiplication squares the constant)
- **P3:** $V(aX + b) = a^{2} V(X)$ (translation does not affect variance)
- **P4:** $V(X + Y) = V(X) + V(Y) + 2 \text{Cov}(X, Y)$
- **P5:** If $X$ and $Y$ are **independent**, $\text{Cov}(X, Y) = 0$, hence $V(X + Y) = V(X) + V(Y)$
- **P6:** $V(X - Y) = V(X) + V(Y) - 2 \text{Cov}(X, Y)$
- **P7:** Standard deviation $\sigma = \sqrt{V(X)}$ is **never negative**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write $V(X + Y) = V(X) + V(Y)$ **without** checking independence. In KTU exams, you must state "since $X$ and $Y$ are independent" before applying P5.

---

### 2.3 Cumulative Distribution Function — Theoretical Breakdown

The CDF is derived by **integrating** (continuous case) or **summing** (discrete case) the probability distribution. Conceptually:

- **Step 1:** Start with the PDF $f(x)$ or PMF $P(x)$.
- **Step 2:** Accumulate probability from $-\infty$ up to the point $x$.
- **Step 3:** The result $F(x)$ is a **non-decreasing** function that ranges from $0$ to $1$.

#### Fundamental Properties of $F(x)$ (Mandatory for KTU)

- **C1:** $0 \leq F(x) \leq 1$ for all $x \in \mathbb{R}$
- **C2:** $F(-\infty) = \lim_{x \to -\infty} F(x) = 0$
- **C3:** $F(+\infty) = \lim_{x \to +\infty} F(x) = 1$
- **C4:** $F$ is **monotonically non-decreasing** (i.e., if $x_{1} < x_{2}$, then $F(x_{1}) \leq F(x_{2})$)
- **C5:** $F$ is **right-continuous** (i.e., $\lim_{x \to a^{+}} F(x) = F(a)$)
- **C6:** $P(a < X \leq b) = F(b) - F(a)$ (probability of an interval)
- **C7:** $P(X > a) = 1 - F(a)$ (survival/complement rule)

#### Recovering PDF/PMF from CDF

- **Continuous case:** $f(x) = \dfrac{d}{dx} F(x) = F'(x)$
- **Discrete case:** $P(X = x_{i}) = F(x_{i}) - F(x_{i}^{-})$ (jump at $x_{i}$)

#### Finding Variance Using the CDF

For a **continuous** random variable, an elegant identity holds:

$$E(X) = \int_{0}^{\infty} [1 - F(x)] \, dx - \int_{-\infty}^{0} F(x) \, dx$$

And a similar survival-function approach can be used to compute $E(X^{2})$ and hence variance. For KTU, however, the most common route is to **first find $f(x) = F'(x)$** and then apply the standard variance formula.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains the **high-yield formulas** you must memorize for the KTU ESE (End Semester Examination). Pay close attention to the use of `\vert` and `\mid` for absolute value and conditioning symbols, to comply with markdown table syntax requirements.

| # | Concept | Formula | Domain / Condition |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | Variance (definition) | $V(X) = E[(X - \mu)^{2}]$ | All random variables |
| 2 | Variance (computational) | $V(X) = E(X^{2}) - [E(X)]^{2}$ | Easier for KTU problems |
| 3 | Standard deviation | $\sigma = \sqrt{V(X)}$ | $\sigma \geq 0$ always |
| 4 | $V(aX + b)$ | $a^{2} \cdot V(X)$ | Constants $a, b$ |
| 5 | $V(X+Y)$ independent | $V(X) + V(Y)$ | Requires independence |
| 6 | CDF definition | $F(x) = P(X \leq x)$ | Universal |
| 7 | CDF from PDF | $F(x) = \int_{-\infty}^{x} f(t) \, dt$ | Continuous case |
| 8 | CDF from PMF | $F(x) = \sum_{x_{i} \leq x} P(x_{i})$ | Discrete case |
| 9 | PDF from CDF | $f(x) = F'(x)$ | Continuous case |
| 10 | Interval probability | $P(a < X \leq b) = F(b) - F(a)$ | Universal |
| 11 | Survival function | $S(x) = 1 - F(x) = P(X > x)$ | Universal |
| 12 | Chebyshev's inequality | $P(\vert X - \mu \vert \geq k\sigma) \leq \frac{1}{k^{2}}$ | All distributions |

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering and Biomedical Utility

- **Clinical Trials:** Variance quantifies the **heterogeneity of patient response** to a drug. A low-variance drug yields consistent outcomes; high variance signals unpredictable responses.
- **Quality Control in Pharma:** Standard deviation $\sigma$ defines **tolerance limits** for tablet weight, dissolution rate, and active ingredient concentration.
- **Epidemiology:** The CDF models **incidence curves** — $F(t)$ gives the probability that a disease manifests by age $t$.
- **Survival Analysis:** The complement of CDF, $S(t) = 1 - F(t)$, is the **survival function**, foundational in oncology and cardiology.
- **Biomedical Signal Processing:** Variance of EEG/ECG signals is a measure of signal power and noise content.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Equivalence of the Two Variance Formulas

We rigorously prove that $E[(X - \mu)^{2}] = E(X^{2}) - [E(X)]^{2}$.

Starting from the definition:

$$V(X) = E[(X - \mu)^{2}]$$

Expanding the squared term using the algebraic identity $(a - b)^{2} = a^{2} - 2ab + b^{2}$:

$$\Rightarrow V(X) = E[X^{2} - 2\mu X + \mu^{2}]$$

Applying the **linearity of expectation** $E[aX + bY + c] = aE(X) + bE(Y) + c$:

$$\Rightarrow V(X) = E(X^{2}) - 2\mu \, E(X) + E(\mu^{2})$$

Since $\mu$ is a constant, $E(\mu) = \mu$ and $E(\mu^{2}) = \mu^{2}$:

$$\Rightarrow V(X) = E(X^{2}) - 2\mu \cdot \mu + \mu^{2}$$

$$\Rightarrow V(X) = E(X^{2}) - 2\mu^{2} + \mu^{2}$$

$$\Rightarrow V(X) = E(X^{2}) - \mu^{2}$$

Substituting back $\mu = E(X)$:

$$\boxed{V(X) = E(X^{2}) - [E(X)]^{2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **computational formula** you will use in **90% of KTU problems** involving variance. Always compute $E(X)$ first, then $E(X^{2})$, then plug into this identity.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Relationship Between CDF and PDF (Continuous Case)

Given $F(x) = \int_{-\infty}^{x} f(t) \, dt$, we apply the **Fundamental Theorem of Calculus**:

$$F'(x) = \frac{d}{dx} \int_{-\infty}^{x} f(t) \, dt = f(x)$$

Hence:

$$\boxed{f(x) = F'(x)}$$

This is why the **derivative of the CDF gives the PDF**, and conversely the **integral of the PDF gives the CDF**.

**Verification Step:** If $f(x) \geq 0$ everywhere and $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x) \, dx = 1$, then $F(+\infty) = 1$ and $F(-\infty) = 0$, satisfying the CDF properties C2 and C3.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 1 — Discrete Variance (Full KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A discrete random variable $X$ has the following PMF:

| $x$ | $0$ | $1$ | $2$ | $3$ |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| $P(x)$ | $0.1$ | $0.2$ | $0.4$ | $0.3$ |

Find $E(X)$, $E(X^{2})$, $V(X)$, and $\sigma$.

**Step 1: Compute $E(X)$**

$$E(X) = \sum x \cdot P(x) = (0)(0.1) + (1)(0.2) + (2)(0.4) + (3)(0.3)$$

$$E(X) = 0 + 0.2 + 0.8 + 0.9 = 1.9$$

**Step 2: Compute $E(X^{2})$**

$$E(X^{2}) = \sum x^{2} \cdot P(x) = (0)^{2}(0.1) + (1)^{2}(0.2) + (2)^{2}(0.4) + (3)^{2}(0.3)$$

$$E(X^{2}) = 0 + 0.2 + 1.6 + 2.7 = 4.5$$

**Step 3: Compute $V(X)$ using the computational formula**

$$V(X) = E(X^{2}) - [E(X)]^{2} = 4.5 - (1.9)^{2} = 4.5 - 3.61 = 0.89$$

**Step 4: Compute $\sigma$**

$$\sigma = \sqrt{V(X)} = \sqrt{0.89} \approx 0.9434$$

**Final Answer:** $E(X) = 1.9$, $V(X) = 0.89$, $\sigma \approx 0.9434$.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 2 — Continuous Variance from PDF (Full KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A continuous random variable $X$ has PDF:

$$f(x) = \begin{cases} 2x, & 0 \leq x \leq 1 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

Find $E(X)$, $E(X^{2})$, $V(X)$, and the **CDF** $F(x)$.

**Step 1: Compute $E(X)$**

$$E(X) = \int_{0}^{1} x \cdot f(x) \, dx = \int_{0}^{1} x \cdot 2x \, dx = \int_{0}^{1} 2x^{2} \, dx$$

$$E(X) = 2 \left[ \frac{x^{3}}{3} \right]_{0}^{1} = 2 \cdot \frac{1}{3} = \frac{2}{3}$$

**Step 2: Compute $E(X^{2})$**

$$E(X^{2}) = \int_{0}^{1} x^{2} \cdot 2x \, dx = \int_{0}^{1} 2x^{3} \, dx$$

$$E(X^{2}) = 2 \left[ \frac{x^{4}}{4} \right]_{0}^{1} = 2 \cdot \frac{1}{4} = \frac{1}{2}$$

**Step 3: Compute $V(X)$**

$$V(X) = E(X^{2}) - [E(X)]^{2} = \frac{1}{2} - \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^{2} = \frac{1}{2} - \frac{4}{9}$$

$$V(X) = \frac{9 - 8}{18} = \frac{1}{18} \approx 0.0556$$

**Step 4: Derive the CDF $F(x)$**

For $0 \leq x \leq 1$:

$$F(x) = \int_{0}^{x} 2t \, dt = \left[ t^{2} \right]_{0}^{x} = x^{2}$$

So the full CDF is:

$$F(x) = \begin{cases} 0, & x < 0 \\ x^{2}, & 0 \leq x \leq 1 \\ 1, & x > 1 \end{cases}$$

**Final Answer:** $E(X) = \frac{2}{3}$, $V(X) = \frac{1}{18}$, $F(x) = x^{2}$ for $0 \leq x \leq 1$.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Trick:** In Example 2, you could also recover the PDF by differentiating: $F'(x) = 2x = f(x)$. This confirms the dual relationship between $f$ and $F$.

---

### 3.5 Worked Example 3 — Finding Variance Given Only the CDF

**Problem:** A random variable $X$ has the CDF:

$$F(x) = \begin{cases} 0, & x < 0 \\ 1 - e^{-2x}, & x \geq 0 \end{cases}$$

This is an **exponential distribution** with rate $\lambda = 2$. Find $E(X)$ and $V(X)$.

**Step 1: Find the PDF**

$$f(x) = F'(x) = \frac{d}{dx}(1 - e^{-2x}) = 2e^{-2x}, \quad x \geq 0$$

**Step 2: Compute $E(X)$**

$$E(X) = \int_{0}^{\infty} x \cdot 2e^{-2x} \, dx = 2 \int_{0}^{\infty} x e^{-2x} \, dx$$

Using integration by parts: $\int x e^{-ax} dx = -\frac{x}{a}e^{-ax} - \frac{1}{a^{2}}e^{-ax}$

$$E(X) = 2 \left[ -\frac{x}{2}e^{-2x} - \frac{1}{4}e^{-2x} \right]_{0}^{\infty} = 2 \left[ 0 - \left( 0 - \frac{1}{4} \right) \right] = 2 \cdot \frac{1}{4} = \frac{1}{2}$$

**Step 3: Compute $E(X^{2})$**

$$E(X^{2}) = \int_{0}^{\infty} x^{2} \cdot 2e^{-2x} \, dx = 2 \int_{0}^{\infty} x^{2} e^{-2x} \, dx$$

Using $\int x^{2} e^{-ax} dx = -\frac{x^{2}}{a}e^{-ax} - \frac{2x}{a^{2}}e^{-ax} - \frac{2}{a^{3}}e^{-ax}$:

$$E(X^{2}) = 2 \left[ -\frac{x^{2}}{2}e^{-2x} - \frac{2x}{4}e^{-2x} - \frac{2}{8}e^{-2x} \right]_{0}^{\infty} = 2 \left[ 0 - \left( 0 - 0 - \frac{1}{4} \right) \right] = \frac{1}{2}$$

**Step 4: Compute $V(X)$**

$$V(X) = E(X^{2}) - [E(X)]^{2} = \frac{1}{2} - \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2} = \frac{1}{2} - \frac{1}{4} = \frac{1}{4}$$

**Final Answer:** $E(X) = 0.5$, $V(X) = 0.25$.

> [!NOTE]
> **General Result for Exponential($\lambda$):** $E(X) = \frac{1}{\lambda}$ and $V(X) = \frac{1}{\lambda^{2}}$. For $\lambda = 2$, this confirms $E(X) = 0.5$ and $V(X) = 0.25$. Memorize this for KTU.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture: Variance and CDF Computation Pipeline

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **logical flow** for solving any KTU problem involving variance and the cumulative distribution function. It maps the entire analytical pipeline from problem statement to final numerical answer.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Start: Given Random Variable X] --> nodeB{Discrete or Continuous?}
    nodeB -->|Discrete| nodeC[Step 1: Identify PMF P of x]
    nodeB -->|Continuous| nodeD[Step 1: Identify PDF f of x]
    nodeC --> nodeE[Step 2a: Compute E of X as Sum x times P of x]
    nodeD --> nodeF[Step 2a: Compute E of X as Integral of x times f of x dx]
    nodeC --> nodeG[Step 2b: Compute E of X squared as Sum x squared P of x]
    nodeD --> nodeH[Step 2b: Compute E of X squared as Integral of x squared f of x dx]
    nodeE --> nodeI[Step 3: Apply V of X equals E of X squared minus E of X squared]
    nodeF --> nodeI
    nodeG --> nodeI
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Step 4: Compute sigma as square root of V of X]
    nodeE --> nodeK[Step 5a: For CDF discrete, F of x equals Sum P of x_i for x_i less than or equal x]
    nodeF --> nodeL[Step 5b: For CDF continuous, F of x equals Integral of f of t dt from minus infinity to x]
    nodeK --> nodeM[Step 6: Verify F of minus infinity equals 0 and F of plus infinity equals 1]
    nodeL --> nodeM
    nodeJ --> nodeN[Final Answer: Report E of X, V of X, sigma, and F of x]
    nodeM --> nodeN
```

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Relationship Between PMF/PDF, CDF, and Variance

This block diagram shows the **interconnected relationship** between the three core objects of a probability distribution.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pdfBox["PDF or PMF: f of x or P of x"] -->|Integrate from minus infinity to x| cdfBox["CDF: F of x equals P of X less than or equal x"]
    pdfBox -->|Weighted Average| meanBox["Mean: mu equals E of X"]
    pdfBox -->|Squared Weighted Average| meansqBox["Second Moment: E of X squared"]
    meanBox --> varBox["Variance: V of X equals E of X squared minus mu squared"]
    meansqBox --> varBox
    varBox --> stdBox["Standard Deviation: sigma equals square root V of X"]
    cdfBox -->|Differentiate| pdfBox
    cdfBox -->|F of b minus F of a| intervalBox["Interval Probability: P of a less than X less than or equal b"]
    cdfBox -->|1 minus F of x| survivalBox["Survival Function: S of x equals P of X greater than x"]
```

---

### 4.3 Conceptual Block Diagram: Property Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    propStart[Properties to Verify] --> propA[F of minus infinity equals 0]
    propStart --> propB[F of plus infinity equals 1]
    propStart --> propC[F is Non Decreasing]
    propStart --> propD[F is Right Continuous]
    propStart --> propE[V of X is always Non Negative]
    propStart --> propF[V of constant equals 0]
    propStart --> propG[V of aX plus b equals a squared V of X]
    propA --> verifyEnd[All Conditions Satisfied: Valid CDF and Variance]
    propB --> verifyEnd
    propC --> verifyEnd
    propD --> verifyEnd
    propE --> verifyEnd
    propF --> verifyEnd
    propG --> verifyEnd
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each block represents a computational or conceptual step. The arrows indicate the **direction of data flow** in the standard KTU problem-solving methodology. Always move from "Given Data" → "Mean" → "Variance" → "Standard Deviation", and from "PDF" → "CDF" (or vice versa).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

> Define the **variance** of a random variable. State and prove the relationship $V(X) = E(X^{2}) - [E(X)]^{2}$.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Variance of a random variable $X$ is the **expected value of the squared deviation** of $X$ from its mean $\mu$:

$$V(X) = E[(X - \mu)^{2}]$$

**Statement (0.5 Mark):** $V(X) = E(X^{2}) - [E(X)]^{2}$.

**Proof (1.5 Marks):**

$$V(X) = E[(X - \mu)^{2}] = E[X^{2} - 2\mu X + \mu^{2}]$$

Applying linearity of expectation:

$$V(X) = E(X^{2}) - 2\mu E(X) + E(\mu^{2})$$

Since $\mu$ is a constant, $E(\mu) = \mu$ and $E(\mu^{2}) = \mu^{2}$:

$$V(X) = E(X^{2}) - 2\mu^{2} + \mu^{2} = E(X^{2}) - \mu^{2} = E(X^{2}) - [E(X)]^{2}$$

Hence proved.

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

> What is a **Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF)**? List any **four properties** of the CDF.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** The CDF of a random variable $X$ is defined as:

$$F(x) = P(X \leq x), \quad \text{for all } x \in \mathbb{R}$$

**Four Properties (0.5 Mark Each):**

1. **Range:** $0 \leq F(x) \leq 1$ for all $x$.
2. **Boundary at $-\infty$:** $F(-\infty) = \lim_{x \to -\infty} F(x) = 0$.
3. **Boundary at $+\infty$:** $F(+\infty) = \lim_{x \to +\infty} F(x) = 1$.
4. **Monotonicity:** $F$ is a non-decreasing function of $x$. If $x_{1} < x_{2}$, then $F(x_{1}) \leq F(x_{2})$.

> [!TIP]
> For full 3 marks, the examiner expects the **definition** and **four clearly stated properties**.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

> **(a)** A discrete random variable $X$ has the probability mass function:
>
> $$P(X = x) = \begin{cases} \frac{k}{x}, & x = 1, 2, 3 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$
>
> Find the value of $k$, $E(X)$, $E(X^{2})$, $V(X)$, and the standard deviation $\sigma$. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Define the CDF. Given the PDF of a continuous random variable $X$ as:
>
> $$f(x) = \begin{cases} \frac{x}{8}, & 0 \leq x \leq 4 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$
>
> Find the CDF $F(x)$, $E(X)$, and $V(X)$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

##### **Part (a) — Discrete Case (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: Find $k$ using normalization** (1 Mark)

$$\sum P(X = x) = 1 \Rightarrow \frac{k}{1} + \frac{k}{2} + \frac{k}{3} = 1$$

$$k \left( 1 + \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{3} \right) = 1 \Rightarrow k \cdot \frac{11}{6} = 1 \Rightarrow k = \frac{6}{11}$$

**Step 2: Construct the PMF table** (1 Mark)

| $x$ | $1$ | $2$ | $3$ |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| $P(x)$ | $\frac{6}{11}$ | $\frac{3}{11}$ | $\frac{2}{11}$ |

**Step 3: Compute $E(X)$** (1 Mark)

$$E(X) = (1)\left(\frac{6}{11}\right) + (2)\left(\frac{3}{11}\right) + (3)\left(\frac{2}{11}\right) = \frac{6 + 6 + 6}{11} = \frac{18}{11}$$

**Step 4: Compute $E(X^{2})$** (1 Mark)

$$E(X^{2}) = (1)^{2}\left(\frac{6}{11}\right) + (2)^{2}\left(\frac{3}{11}\right) + (3)^{2}\left(\frac{2}{11}\right) = \frac{6 + 12 + 18}{11} = \frac{36}{11}$$

**Step 5: Compute $V(X)$** (1 Mark)

$$V(X) = E(X^{2}) - [E(X)]^{2} = \frac{36}{11} - \left(\frac{18}{11}\right)^{2} = \frac{36}{11} - \frac{324}{121}$$

$$V(X) = \frac{396 - 324}{121} = \frac{72}{121} \approx 0.595$$

**Step 6: Compute $\sigma$** (1 Mark)

$$\sigma = \sqrt{V(X)} = \sqrt{\frac{72}{121}} = \frac{\sqrt{72}}{11} = \frac{6\sqrt{2}}{11} \approx 0.7714$$

**Final Answer:** $k = \frac{6}{11}$, $E(X) = \frac{18}{11}$, $V(X) = \frac{72}{121}$, $\sigma = \frac{6\sqrt{2}}{11}$.

---

##### **Part (b) — Continuous Case (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: Definition of CDF** (1 Mark)

$$F(x) = P(X \leq x) = \int_{-\infty}^{x} f(t) \, dt$$

**Step 2: Verify normalization** (0.5 Mark)

$$\int_{0}^{4} \frac{x}{8} \, dx = \left[ \frac{x^{2}}{16} \right]_{0}^{4} = \frac{16}{16} = 1 \checkmark$$

**Step 3: Derive the CDF for $0 \leq x \leq 4$** (1.5 Marks)

$$F(x) = \int_{0}^{x} \frac{t}{8} \, dt = \left[ \frac{t^{2}}{16} \right]_{0}^{x} = \frac{x^{2}}{16}$$

So the full CDF is:

$$F(x) = \begin{cases} 0, & x < 0 \\ \frac{x^{2}}{16}, & 0 \leq x \leq 4 \\ 1, & x > 4 \end{cases}$$

**Step 4: Compute $E(X)$** (1.5 Marks)

$$E(X) = \int_{0}^{4} x \cdot \frac{x}{8} \, dx = \int_{0}^{4} \frac{x^{2}}{8} \, dx = \frac{1}{8} \left[ \frac{x^{3}}{3} \right]_{0}^{4} = \frac{1}{8} \cdot \frac{64}{3} = \frac{8}{3}$$

**Step 5: Compute $E(X^{2})$** (1.5 Marks)

$$E(X^{2}) = \int_{0}^{4} x^{2} \cdot \frac{x}{8} \, dx = \int_{0}^{4} \frac{x^{3}}{8} \, dx = \frac{1}{8} \left[ \frac{x^{4}}{4} \right]_{0}^{4} = \frac{1}{8} \cdot \frac{256}{4} = \frac{256}{32} = 8$$

**Step 6: Compute $V(X)$** (1 Mark)

$$V(X) = E(X^{2}) - [E(X)]^{2} = 8 - \left(\frac{8}{3}\right)^{2} = 8 - \frac{64}{9} = \frac{72 - 64}{9} = \frac{8}{9} \approx 0.8889$$

**Final Answer:** $F(x) = \frac{x^{2}}{16}$ for $0 \leq x \leq 4$, $E(X) = \frac{8}{3}$, $V(X) = \frac{8}{9}$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

> **(a)** State and prove the **properties of variance** $V(aX + b) = a^{2}V(X)$ and $V(X + Y) = V(X) + V(Y)$ for independent random variables. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** The CDF of a random variable $X$ is given by:
>
> $$F(x) = \begin{cases} 0, & x < 0 \\ \frac{x^{3}}{27}, & 0 \leq x \leq 3 \\ 1, & x > 3 \end{cases}$$
>
> Find: (i) the PDF $f(x)$, (ii) $P(1 < X \leq 2)$, (iii) $E(X)$, and (iv) $V(X)$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

##### **Part (a) — Proof of Variance Properties (7 Marks)**

**Property 1: $V(aX + b) = a^{2} V(X)$** (3.5 Marks)

By definition:

$$V(aX + b) = E\left[\left((aX + b) - E(aX + b)\right)^{2}\right]$$

Since $E(aX + b) = aE(X) + b$:

$$V(aX + b) = E\left[\left(aX + b - aE(X) - b\right)^{2}\right] = E\left[\left(a(X - E(X))\right)^{2}\right]$$

$$= E\left[a^{2}(X - E(X))^{2}\right] = a^{2} E[(X - E(X))^{2}] = a^{2} V(X)$$

Hence proved.

**Property 2: $V(X + Y) = V(X) + V(Y)$ when $X$ and $Y$ are independent** (3.5 Marks)

By definition:

$$V(X + Y) = E\left[\left((X + Y) - E(X + Y)\right)^{2}\right]$$

$$= E\left[\left((X - E(X)) + (Y - E(Y))\right)^{2}\right]$$

$$= E[(X - E(X))^{2}] + E[(Y - E(Y))^{2}] + 2E[(X - E(X))(Y - E(Y))]$$

$$= V(X) + V(Y) + 2 \cdot \text{Cov}(X, Y)$$

Since $X$ and $Y$ are **independent**, $\text{Cov}(X, Y) = 0$:

$$\boxed{V(X + Y) = V(X) + V(Y)}$$

Hence proved.

---

##### **Part (b) — CDF-Based Problem (7 Marks)**

**Step (i): Find the PDF $f(x)$** (2 Marks)

Differentiating $F(x)$ for $0 \leq x \leq 3$:

$$f(x) = F'(x) = \frac{d}{dx}\left(\frac{x^{3}}{27}\right) = \frac{3x^{2}}{27} = \frac{x^{2}}{9}$$

So the full PDF is:

$$f(x) = \begin{cases} \frac{x^{2}}{9}, & 0 \leq x \leq 3 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step (ii): Find $P(1 < X \leq 2)$** (1.5 Marks)

$$P(1 < X \leq 2) = F(2) - F(1) = \frac{(2)^{3}}{27} - \frac{(1)^{3}}{27} = \frac{8 - 1}{27} = \frac{7}{27} \approx 0.2593$$

**Step (iii): Find $E(X)$** (1.5 Marks)

$$E(X) = \int_{0}^{3} x \cdot \frac{x^{2}}{9} \, dx = \int_{0}^{3} \frac{x^{3}}{9} \, dx = \frac{1}{9} \left[ \frac{x^{4}}{4} \right]_{0}^{3} = \frac{1}{9} \cdot \frac{81}{4} = \frac{9}{4} = 2.25$$

**Step (iv): Find $V(X)$** (2 Marks)

$$E(X^{2}) = \int_{0}^{3} x^{2} \cdot \frac{x^{2}}{9} \, dx = \int_{0}^{3} \frac{x^{4}}{9} \, dx = \frac{1}{9} \left[ \frac{x^{5}}{5} \right]_{0}^{3} = \frac{1}{9} \cdot \frac{243}{5} = \frac{27}{5} = 5.4$$

$$V(X) = E(X^{2}) - [E(X)]^{2} = \frac{27}{5} - \left(\frac{9}{4}\right)^{2} = \frac{27}{5} - \frac{81}{16}$$

$$V(X) = \frac{27 \cdot 16 - 81 \cdot 5}{80} = \frac{432 - 405}{80} = \frac{27}{80} = 0.3375$$

**Final Answer:** $f(x) = \frac{x^{2}}{9}$, $P(1 < X \leq 2) = \frac{7}{27}$, $E(X) = 2.25$, $V(X) = 0.3375$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
>
> Students commonly lose marks on the following issues:
>
> - **Pitfall 1:** Forgetting to verify that the PMF/PDF is **properly normalized** (sums to 1 in discrete case, integrates to 1 in continuous case) before computing $E(X)$. If the constant $k$ is wrong, **all subsequent answers lose marks**.
> - **Pitfall 2:** Using the **definition formula** $V(X) = E[(X-\mu)^{2}]$ directly instead of the computational formula. This is much more tedious and **invites arithmetic errors**. Always prefer $V(X) = E(X^{2}) - [E(X)]^{2}$.
> - **Pitfall 3:** Forgetting to **state "since $X$ and $Y$ are independent"** when applying $V(X + Y) = V(X) + V(Y)$. The examiner deducts 1–2 marks if independence is not explicitly mentioned.
> - **Pitfall 4:** When the problem gives the **CDF instead of PDF**, students often directly compute $E(X) = \int x \cdot F(x) \, dx$, which is **incorrect**. You must first differentiate to obtain the PDF.
> - **Pitfall 5:** Failing to give the **complete piecewise CDF** including the regions $x < a$ and $x > b$ in continuous problems. Examiners deduct marks if the piecewise structure is incomplete.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Variance** measures the **average squared dispersion** of data around the mean and is the **second central moment**.
- The **computational formula** $V(X) = E(X^{2}) - [E(X)]^{2}$ is the **default choice** in KTU problems.
- The **Standard Deviation** $\sigma = \sqrt{V(X)}$ is expressed in the **same units** as $X$ and is preferred for **interpretation**.
- $V(aX + b) = a^{2} V(X)$ — **translation by $b$ does not change variance**, but **scalar multiplication squares the constant**.
- For **independent** random variables, $V(X + Y) = V(X) + V(Y)$ and $V(X - Y) = V(X) + V(Y)$.
- The **CDF** $F(x) = P(X \leq x)$ is the **integrated/summed form** of the PDF/PMF.
- **Five key CDF properties** for KTU: range $[0, 1]$, $F(-\infty) = 0$, $F(+\infty) = 1$, **non-decreasing**, **right-continuous**.
- $P(a < X \leq b) = F(b) - F(a)$ — this single identity solves **most interval probability questions**.
- The **derivative of the CDF** gives the PDF (continuous case), and the **difference in CDF** at a point gives the PMF value (discrete case).
- The **Exponential$(\lambda)$** distribution has $E(X) = \frac{1}{\lambda}$ and $V(X) = \frac{1}{\lambda^{2}}$ — memorize for quick KTU answers.
- **Chebyshev's inequality** $P(\vert X - \mu \vert \geq k\sigma) \leq \frac{1}{k^{2}}$ is a **distribution-free** bound that follows directly from variance concepts and is a common KTU question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT861 - BIOSTATISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Binomial Distribution):**
> A random variable $X$ is said to follow a **Binomial Distribution** with parameters $n$ (number of independent Bernoulli trials) and $p$ (probability of success in each trial) if it represents the *number of successes* observed in $n$ independent Bernoulli trials. The probability mass function (PMF) is given by:
> $$P(X = r) = \binom{n}{r} p^r q^{n-r}, \quad r = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, n$$
> where $q = 1 - p$ is the probability of failure, and $0 \le p \le 1$. Symbolically, we write $X \sim B(n, p)$.

A **Bernoulli trial** is a random experiment with exactly two mutually exclusive and exhaustive outcomes — conventionally called **Success** (with probability $p$) and **Failure** (with probability $q = 1 - p$). Tossing a fair coin, testing a patient for a disease, and inspecting a manufactured item for defects are textbook Bernoulli trials.

The prefix *Bi* in Binomial refers to the two possible outcomes, and the *nomial* portion refers to the multinomial counting of how many such trials result in successes. The mathematical constant **$n$** denotes the total number of trials, and the parameter **$r$** denotes the specific count of successes whose probability we wish to compute.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a doctor screening **$n = 10$** patients for a particular disease. Each patient independently tests positive with probability $p = 0.3$. Each patient test is a Bernoulli trial — they are either **positive (success)** or **negative (failure)**.

Now the question is: "What is the probability that exactly **$r = 4$** patients out of the 10 test positive?"

Intuitively, we need to count:
- All possible ways of choosing which 4 patients test positive out of 10 → $\binom{10}{4}$ combinations.
- Multiply by the probability of any one specific arrangement: $p^4 \cdot q^{6}$ (4 positives, 6 negatives).

This multiplication-and-counting logic is the *soul* of the Binomial Distribution. **Count the ways, multiply the probabilities.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world applications in Biostatistics (KTU 2024 context):**
> - Probability that a certain number of patients respond to a drug in a clinical trial.
> - Estimating the number of defective blood samples in a batch of $n$ units.
> - Sex distribution in offspring (male/female).
> - Survival analysis of patients undergoing a specific treatment.

## 1.3 Symbolic Notation Standard

| Symbol | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| $X$ | Random variable (number of successes) |
| $n$ | Total number of independent trials (**fixed integer**) |
| $p$ | Probability of success in a single trial |
| $q$ | Probability of failure, $q = 1 - p$ |
| $r$ | Specific number of successes ($0 \le r \le n$) |
| $\binom{n}{r}$ | Binomial coefficient: $\dfrac{n!}{r!(n-r)!}$ |

The key point: the random variable $X$ is *discrete* and *bounded* (it can never exceed $n$).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Assumptions of a Binomial Experiment (KTU Hallmark)

For a random experiment to be modelled by a Binomial Distribution, the following **four conditions** must hold simultaneously. Examiners frequently test these:

1. **Finite number of trials ($n$):** The experiment is repeated a fixed, predetermined number of times.
2. **Two mutually exclusive outcomes:** Each trial results in only Success or Failure (binary outcome).
3. **Constant probability ($p$):** The probability of success $p$ remains the same for every trial. This is the **independence + identical distribution** condition.
4. **Independence of trials:** The outcome of one trial must not influence the outcome of any other trial.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If any of these four assumptions is violated, the Binomial model becomes mathematically invalid. For example, sampling *without replacement* from a small finite population requires the **Hypergeometric Distribution** instead.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Remarks |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **PMF** | $P(X=r) = \binom{n}{r} p^r q^{n-r}$ | Valid for $r = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, n$ |
| 2 | **Cumulative Probability** | $P(X \le r) = \displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{r} \binom{n}{k} p^k q^{n-k}$ | Sum of PMF from 0 to $r$ |
| 3 | **Mean** $\mu$ | $E(X) = np$ | First central raw moment |
| 4 | **Variance** $\sigma^2$ | $Var(X) = npq$ | Second central moment |
| 5 | **Standard Deviation** $\sigma$ | $\sigma = \sqrt{npq}$ | Square root of variance |
| 6 | **Skewness** $\gamma_1$ | $\dfrac{q-p}{\sqrt{npq}}$ | Asymmetry measure |
| 7 | **Kurtosis** $\gamma_2$ | $\dfrac{1 - 6pq}{npq}$ | Tailedness measure |
| 8 | **Moment Generating Function (MGF)** | $M_X(t) = (pe^t + q)^n$ | $E(e^{tX})$ |
| 9 | **Cumulant Generating Function (CGF)** | $K_X(t) = n \log(pe^t + q)$ | $\log M_X(t)$ |
| 10 | **Recurrence Relation** | $\dfrac{P(r+1)}{P(r)} = \dfrac{(n-r)}{(r+1)} \cdot \dfrac{p}{q}$ | Used for fast computation |
| 11 | **Characteristic Function** | $\phi_X(t) = (pe^{it} + q)^n$ | Fourier analogue of MGF |
| 12 | **Additive Property** | If $X_i \sim B(n_i, p)$ independent, then $\sum X_i \sim B(\sum n_i, p)$ | Sum of independent Binomials |
| 13 | **Poisson Approximation** | $P(X=r) \approx \dfrac{e^{-\lambda}\lambda^r}{r!}$ | when $n \to \infty$, $p \to 0$, $np = \lambda$ |
| 14 | **Normal Approximation** | $Z = \dfrac{X - np}{\sqrt{npq}}$ | when $n$ is large ($np, nq \ge 5$) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Substitute for $|x|$ in tables:** When the expression demands absolute value, write $\vert x \vert$ or $\operatorname{abs}(x)$ rather than the vertical pipe, to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.3 Theoretical Insights ("Why" and "How")

- **Why $\binom{n}{r}$?** Because the $r$ successes can appear in any $r$ positions among $n$ trials. The combinations count the distinguishable arrangements, while $p^r q^{n-r}$ accounts for one specific arrangement.
- **Why Mean $= np$?** The expectation operator is linear, and each individual trial has expected value $p$ (because $E(Bernoulli) = 1 \cdot p + 0 \cdot q = p$). By linearity, $E(X) = E(X_1 + X_2 + \cdots + X_n) = np$.
- **Why Variance $= npq$?** Each Bernoulli trial has variance $pq$ (a well-known identity), and because the trials are independent, variances add.
- **Why Skewness and Kurtosis matter:** They characterise the *shape* of the distribution. When $p = 0.5$, the Binomial is symmetric. When $p \neq 0.5$, it is skewed.

## 2.4 Utility in Engineering and Biostatistics

The Binomial Distribution is the workhorse of *quality control*, *reliability engineering*, *clinical trials*, and *epidemiology*. In KTU 2024 Biostatistics, it underpins drug efficacy studies, sample size calculations, and hypothesis testing (e.g., sign test, proportion tests). It is also the foundation from which the Poisson and Normal distributions are derived as limiting cases — a frequent examination question.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Mean $E(X) = np$

Let $X_i$ be the Bernoulli random variable for the $i$-th trial:

$$X_i = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if success} \\ 0 & \text{if failure} \end{cases}$$

Then the total number of successes in $n$ trials is:

$$X = X_1 + X_2 + \cdots + X_n$$

Compute the expectation of one Bernoulli trial:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E(X_i) &= 1 \cdot P(X_i = 1) + 0 \cdot P(X_i = 0) \\
       &= 1 \cdot p + 0 \cdot q \\
       &= p
\end{aligned}
$$

By the **linearity of expectation**, regardless of independence:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E(X) &= E(X_1 + X_2 + \cdots + X_n) \\
     &= E(X_1) + E(X_2) + \cdots + E(X_n) \\
     &= \underbrace{p + p + \cdots + p}_{n \text{ times}} \\
     &= np
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic Note:** Linearity of expectation is one of the few probability tools that does *not* require independence. Even if the trials were dependent, the mean would still be $np$ — though the variance would no longer be $npq$.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Variance $Var(X) = npq$

Recall that $Var(X) = E(X^2) - [E(X)]^2$. We compute $E(X^2)$ by first computing $E(X_i^2)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E(X_i^2) &= 1^2 \cdot p + 0^2 \cdot q = p
\end{aligned}
$$

The variance of one Bernoulli trial is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Var(X_i) &= E(X_i^2) - [E(X_i)]^2 \\
         &= p - p^2 \\
         &= p(1 - p) \\
         &= pq
\end{aligned}
$$

Because the trials are **independent**, the variances add:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Var(X) &= Var(X_1 + X_2 + \cdots + X_n) \\
       &= Var(X_1) + Var(X_2) + \cdots + Var(X_n) \\
       &= pq + pq + \cdots + pq \quad (n \text{ times}) \\
       &= npq
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic Note:** This step *critically depends* on independence. If trials are correlated, the variance formula changes.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Moment Generating Function

By definition:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_X(t) &= E(e^{tX}) = E(e^{t(X_1 + X_2 + \cdots + X_n)}) \\
       &= E\left( \prod_{i=1}^{n} e^{tX_i} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

By independence, the expectation of the product equals the product of expectations:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_X(t) &= \prod_{i=1}^{n} E(e^{tX_i}) \\
       &= \prod_{i=1}^{n} \left( e^{t \cdot 1} \cdot p + e^{t \cdot 0} \cdot q \right) \\
       &= \prod_{i=1}^{n} (pe^t + q)
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the product has $n$ identical factors:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_X(t) &= (pe^t + q)^n
\end{aligned}
$$

The cumulant generating function is the natural logarithm of the MGF:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_X(t) &= \log M_X(t) = n \log(pe^t + q)
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Derivation of the Recurrence Relation

Starting with the PMF:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(r) &= \binom{n}{r} p^r q^{n-r} \\
P(r+1) &= \binom{n}{r+1} p^{r+1} q^{n-r-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

Taking the ratio:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{P(r+1)}{P(r)} &= \frac{\binom{n}{r+1}}{\binom{n}{r}} \cdot \frac{p^{r+1}}{p^r} \cdot \frac{q^{n-r-1}}{q^{n-r}} \\
                    &= \frac{n!}{(r+1)!(n-r-1)!} \cdot \frac{r!(n-r)!}{n!} \cdot p \cdot q^{-1} \\
                    &= \frac{r!(n-r)!}{(r+1)!(n-r-1)!} \cdot \frac{p}{q} \\
                    &= \frac{(n-r)}{(r+1)} \cdot \frac{p}{q}
\end{aligned}
$$

This recurrence relation is computationally efficient: once $P(0) = q^n$ is known, all other probabilities can be computed by successive multiplications, avoiding the costly factorials and binomial coefficients.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** In a clinical trial, the probability that a patient recovers after treatment is $p = 0.7$. If 6 patients are treated independently, find the probability that:
- (i) Exactly 4 patients recover.
- (ii) At least 5 patients recover.
- (iii) Compute the mean and variance.

**Solution (i):** $n = 6$, $r = 4$, $p = 0.7$, $q = 0.3$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(X=4) &= \binom{6}{4} (0.7)^4 (0.3)^{2} \\
       &= 15 \times 0.2401 \times 0.09 \\
       &= 15 \times 0.021609 \\
       &= 0.324135
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution (ii):** At least 5 means $P(X \ge 5) = P(X=5) + P(X=6)$.

Compute $P(X=5)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(X=5) &= \binom{6}{5} (0.7)^5 (0.3)^{1} \\
       &= 6 \times 0.16807 \times 0.3 \\
       &= 6 \times 0.050421 \\
       &= 0.302526
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute $P(X=6)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(X=6) &= \binom{6}{6} (0.7)^6 (0.3)^{0} \\
       &= 1 \times 0.117649 \times 1 \\
       &= 0.117649
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(X \ge 5) &= 0.302526 + 0.117649 = 0.420175
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution (iii):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mean } \mu &= np = 6 \times 0.7 = 4.2 \\
\text{Variance } \sigma^2 &= npq = 6 \times 0.7 \times 0.3 = 1.26 \\
\text{Standard Deviation } \sigma &= \sqrt{1.26} \approx 1.1225
\end{aligned}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bernoulli-to-Binomial Hierarchy (Conceptual Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Random Experiment] --> B{Exactly Two Outcomes?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Success with Probability p]
    B -- Yes --> D[Failure with Probability q = 1-p]
    C --> E[Single Bernoulli Trial: B 1, p]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Repeat n times independently]
    F --> G[X = Number of Successes]
    G --> H[X follows Binomial Distribution B n, p]
    H --> I["PMF: P X=r = nCr p to the r q to the n-r"]
    H --> J[Mean: np]
    H --> K[Variance: npq]
    H --> L[Skewness: q-p divided by sqrt npq]
    H --> M[MGF: pe to the t plus q to the n]
```

## 4.2 Binomial Distribution Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Input: n] --> C1[Core Processor: Binomial PMF Engine]
    P2[Input: p] --> C1
    P3[Input: r] --> C1
    C1 --> O1[Output: P X=r]
    C1 --> O2[Output: Mean np]
    C1 --> O3[Output: Variance npq]
    C1 --> O4[Output: MGF]
    C1 --> O5[Output: Skewness, Kurtosis]
    O1 --> V1[Validation: Sum from r=0 to n equals 1]
    O2 --> V2[Validation: Mean lies between 0 and n]
    O3 --> V3[Validation: Variance non-negative]
```

## 4.3 Limiting-Case Flow (Binomial Approximations)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    BD[Binomial Distribution B n, p] --> C1{n large, p small, np equals lambda?}
    C1 -- Yes --> PO[Poisson Approximation]
    BD --> C2{n large, p not too close to 0 or 1?}
    C2 -- Yes --> NR[Normal Approximation with Continuity Correction]
    BD --> C3{p = 0.5 and n small?}
    C3 -- Yes --> SYM[Symmetric Discrete Distribution]
    C3 -- No --> SK[Skewed Distribution]
    SK --> SK1[Right-skewed if p less than 0.5]
    SK --> SK2[Left-skewed if p greater than 0.5]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Choice Rationale:** The block diagrams above map the logical-functional flow of the Binomial model, its parameters, and its derived outputs. They serve as a structural schematic substitute for the static bar-chart shape of a typical Binomial PMF (which would be a plot rather than a schematic).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the conditions under which a random variable follows a Binomial Distribution.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A random variable $X$ follows a Binomial Distribution $B(n, p)$ if the underlying experiment satisfies the following four conditions:
1. The experiment consists of a **fixed number of trials** $n$.
2. Each trial has exactly **two mutually exclusive and exhaustive outcomes**: success (with probability $p$) and failure (with probability $q = 1 - p$).
3. The probability of success $p$ is **constant from trial to trial**.
4. The trials are **statistically independent** of one another.

The PMF is $P(X=r) = \binom{n}{r} p^r q^{n-r}$, for $r = 0, 1, \ldots, n$. **(1 mark for stating PMF, 1 mark for listing first two conditions, 1 mark for remaining two.)**

---

### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the mean and variance of a Binomial Distribution $B(n, p)$.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
For $X \sim B(n, p)$:
- **Mean:** $E(X) = np$
- **Variance:** $Var(X) = npq = np(1-p)$

**Derivation outline (1 mark each):** The mean is derived using linearity of expectation over $n$ independent Bernoulli trials, each having mean $p$. The variance follows from $Var(X) = E(X^2) - [E(X)]^2$ combined with independence, giving $npq$. The standard deviation is the square root: $\sigma = \sqrt{npq}$. **(1 mark for definitions, 1 mark for derivation logic, 1 mark for the standard deviation.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice Provided)

### Question B-A. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**(a)** Derive the mean and variance of a Binomial Distribution with parameters $n$ and $p$. Show all steps clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the probability that a newborn baby is male is $0.512$, find the probability that in a family of 6 children: (i) exactly 4 are male, (ii) at most 2 are female, (iii) at least 1 is female. Given $n = 6$, $p = 0.512$, $q = 0.488$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation of Mean and Variance (7 marks):**

Define $n$ independent Bernoulli trials $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$ where $X_i = 1$ if success else $0$. Then $X = \sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i$.

For a single trial:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E(X_i) &= (1)(p) + (0)(q) = p \\
E(X_i^2) &= (1^2)(p) + (0^2)(q) = p \\
Var(X_i) &= E(X_i^2) - [E(X_i)]^2 = p - p^2 = pq
\end{aligned}
$$

By linearity of expectation (2 marks):

$$
\begin{aligned}
E(X) &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} E(X_i) = np
\end{aligned}
$$

By independence of trials, variances add (2 marks):

$$
\begin{aligned}
Var(X) &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} Var(X_i) = npq
\end{aligned}
$$

**Concluding remarks (1 mark):** Hence mean $= np$ and variance $= npq$, with standard deviation $\sigma = \sqrt{npq}$.

**Part (b) — Probability Computations (7 marks):**

Given $n = 6$, $p = 0.512$ (male), $q = 0.488$ (female). The number of male children $X \sim B(6, 0.512)$.

**(i) Exactly 4 are male (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(X=4) &= \binom{6}{4} (0.512)^4 (0.488)^2 \\
       &= 15 \times 0.068719 \times 0.238144 \\
       &\approx 15 \times 0.016365 \\
       &\approx 0.2455
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) At most 2 are female (i.e. at least 4 are male) (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{female} \le 2) &= P(X \ge 4) \\
                       &= P(X=4) + P(X=5) + P(X=6)
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute each term:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(X=4) &\approx 0.2455 \\
P(X=5) &= \binom{6}{5}(0.512)^5(0.488)^1 = 6 \times 0.03518 \times 0.488 \approx 0.1030 \\
P(X=6) &= (0.512)^6 \approx 0.01801
\end{aligned}
$$

Summing:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(X \ge 4) &\approx 0.2455 + 0.1030 + 0.0180 \approx 0.3665
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) At least 1 is female (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{at least 1 female}) &= 1 - P(\text{no female}) = 1 - P(X=6) \\
                            &\approx 1 - 0.0180 = 0.9820
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Question B-B. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**(a)** State and prove the additive property of independent Binomial random variables. If $X \sim B(4, 0.3)$ and $Y \sim B(6, 0.3)$ are independent, find the distribution of $X + Y$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A machine produces components with a defect rate of 5%. A sample of 10 components is inspected. Find the mean, variance, and the probability that exactly 2 are defective. Use the Binomial Distribution. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Additive Property (7 marks):**

**Statement (1 mark):** If $X \sim B(n_1, p)$ and $Y \sim B(n_2, p)$ are independent, then $X + Y \sim B(n_1 + n_2, p)$.

**Proof using MGFs (4 marks):**

The MGF of a Binomial $B(n, p)$ is $M(t) = (pe^t + q)^n$. Hence:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{X+Y}(t) &= M_X(t) \cdot M_Y(t) \quad (\text{by independence}) \\
           &= (pe^t + q)^{n_1} \cdot (pe^t + q)^{n_2} \\
           &= (pe^t + q)^{n_1 + n_2}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the MGF of $B(n_1 + n_2, p)$. By the **uniqueness theorem of MGFs**, the distribution of $X + Y$ is $B(n_1 + n_2, p)$. **(2 marks for concluding with the unique identification.)**

**Application (2 marks):** With $n_1 = 4$, $n_2 = 6$, $p = 0.3$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
X + Y &\sim B(4 + 6, 0.3) = B(10, 0.3)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b) — Mean, Variance, and $P(X=2)$ (7 marks):**

Here $n = 10$, $p = 0.05$ (defect rate), $q = 0.95$.

**Mean (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu = np = 10 \times 0.05 = 0.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Variance (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma^2 = npq = 10 \times 0.05 \times 0.95 = 0.475
\end{aligned}
$$

**Probability of exactly 2 defectives (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(X=2) &= \binom{10}{2} (0.05)^2 (0.95)^8 \\
       &= 45 \times 0.0025 \times 0.6634 \\
       &\approx 45 \times 0.0016585 \\
       &\approx 0.0746
\end{aligned}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the complement $q$:** Many students write $P(X=r) = \binom{n}{r} p^r$ without the $q^{n-r}$ term — instant loss of 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Using $n!$ naively:** For large $n$, direct factorial computation is impractical. Use the recurrence relation $\dfrac{P(r+1)}{P(r)} = \dfrac{(n-r)}{(r+1)} \cdot \dfrac{p}{q}$ to avoid numerical overflow.
> 3. **Confusing $p$ and $q$:** Carefully identify which outcome is the "success." In "probability of at least 1 female," it is more efficient to compute $1 - P(\text{no female})$.
> 4. **Independence assumption:** If the problem says "with replacement" or "independently," Binomial is correct. If it says "without replacement" from a finite population, the answer requires the **Hypergeometric Distribution** — failing to recognise this is a heavy mark-deduction.
> 5. **Additive property misuse:** The additive property requires the *same* $p$ for both Binomial random variables. Adding $B(5, 0.3)$ and $B(5, 0.4)$ is invalid.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** $X \sim B(n, p)$ counts successes in $n$ independent Bernoulli trials. PMF: $P(X=r) = \binom{n}{r} p^r q^{n-r}$, where $q = 1-p$.
- **Four Assumptions:** Fixed $n$, binary outcomes, constant $p$, independent trials. Memorise these — they are a top KTU short-answer item.
- **Mean:** $\mu = np$. **Variance:** $\sigma^2 = npq$. **SD:** $\sigma = \sqrt{npq}$.
- **MGF:** $M_X(t) = (pe^t + q)^n$. **CGF:** $K_X(t) = n \log(pe^t + q)$.
- **Recurrence Relation:** $\dfrac{P(r+1)}{P(r)} = \dfrac{(n-r)}{(r+1)} \cdot \dfrac{p}{q}$ — speeds up computation.
- **Additive Property:** Independent $B(n_1, p)$ and $B(n_2, p)$ sum to $B(n_1 + n_2, p)$. Requires *same* $p$ and independence.
- **Skewness:** $\gamma_1 = \dfrac{q-p}{\sqrt{npq}}$. **Kurtosis:** $\gamma_2 = \dfrac{1 - 6pq}{npq}$.
- **Symmetry:** The Binomial is symmetric only when $p = q = 0.5$.
- **Poisson Approximation:** Used when $n \to \infty$, $p \to 0$, with $np = \lambda$ held constant.
- **Normal Approximation:** Used when $n$ is large and $p$ is not near 0 or 1, with continuity correction.
- **Hypergeometric replacement:** If trials are *without replacement* from a finite population, switch to Hypergeometric Distribution.
- **Common biostatistics use-cases:** Drug response, defect inspection, sex ratio, vaccine efficacy.
- **Examination tactic:** Always verify the four assumptions before declaring a Binomial model.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Poisson Distribution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT861 - BIOSTATISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Poisson Distribution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Poisson Distribution** is a discrete probability distribution that models the number of events occurring in a fixed interval of time or space, when these events happen with a known constant mean rate $\lambda$ and independently of the time since the last event. It is a limiting case of the **Binomial Distribution** when $n \to \infty$ and $p \to 0$ such that the product $np = \lambda$ remains finite.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> The Poisson Distribution is classified as a fundamental *univariate discrete probability model* in Biostatistics. It is the primary tool for modeling **rare events** in medical, biological, and epidemiological data (e.g., number of disease cases per month, mutation counts per gene, hospital arrivals per hour).

The Probability Mass Function (PMF) of a Poisson random variable $X$ is given by:

$$P(X = k) = \frac{e^{-\lambda} \, \lambda^{k}}{k!}, \quad k = 0, 1, 2, 3, \ldots$$

where:
- $\lambda > 0$ is the **rate parameter** (mean number of occurrences per interval)
- $e$ is **Euler's number**, $e \approx 2.71828$
- $k$ is the non-negative integer count of events

The cumulative distribution function (CDF) is:

$$F(k; \lambda) = \sum_{i=0}^{k} \frac{e^{-\lambda} \, \lambda^{i}}{i!}$$

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition:**
> Imagine you are sitting at the window of a busy hospital emergency ward and counting ambulances arriving **per hour**. On average, you observe **3 ambulances per hour** ($\lambda = 3$). The Poisson Distribution answers: *"What is the probability that exactly 5 ambulances will arrive in the next hour?"* or *"What is the probability that no ambulance arrives in the next 30 minutes?"*

The two golden rules for a Poisson process to be valid are:
1. **Independence** — The arrival of one ambulance does not influence the arrival of another.
2. **Constant Average Rate** — Events occur at a fixed mean rate $\lambda$ per unit interval, regardless of time.

**Where does the Poisson Distribution appear in Biostatistics?**
- **Epidemiology:** Modeling the number of new leukemia cases per year in a city.
- **Microbiology:** Counting the number of bacterial colonies on a Petri dish.
- **Genetics:** Number of mutations in a DNA strand.
- **Pharmacology:** Radioactive decay of tracer molecules in a patient's bloodstream.
- **Healthcare Operations:** Number of patient arrivals at an OPD per hour.

## 1.3 Key Characteristics at a Glance

| Characteristic | Description |
|---|---|
| **Type** | Discrete Probability Distribution |
| **Parameter** | Single parameter $\lambda > 0$ (rate) |
| **Support** | $k \in \{0, 1, 2, \ldots\}$ |
| **Mean** | $\lambda$ |
| **Variance** | $\lambda$ |
| **Skewness** | $\lambda^{-1/2}$ (right-skewed for small $\lambda$) |
| **Kurtosis** | $\lambda^{-1}$ (leptokurtic) |
| **Generating Process** | Limit of Binomial as $n \to \infty$, $p \to 0$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Assumptions of a Poisson Process

For a random variable $X$ to follow a Poisson Distribution with parameter $\lambda$, the underlying process must satisfy **four axioms** (these are exam-favorite 3-mark questions):

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Four Poisson Process Axioms (Postulate Set):**
> 1. **Stationarity (Homogeneity):** The probability of exactly one event in a small sub-interval of length $h$ is approximately $\lambda h$, independent of the position of the sub-interval.
> 2. **Independence (Non-multiplicity):** The probability of two or more events occurring in a vanishingly small interval $h$ is $o(h)$ — a quantity that approaches zero *faster* than $h$.
> 3. **Orderliness:** Events cannot occur simultaneously.
> 4. **Non-overlapping Intervals are Independent:** Event counts in disjoint intervals are statistically independent.

## 2.2 Why "Mean = Variance = λ"?

The single most striking and **high-yield KTU property** of the Poisson Distribution is the **equidispersion** property: the mean equals the variance, both numerically equal to $\lambda$. This is unlike the Normal Distribution (where mean and variance are independent parameters) and unlike the Binomial (where variance $= np(1-p)$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering/Medical Utility:**
> In biostatistics, when sample variance is found to be **substantially greater** than the sample mean in count data, this is a strong statistical signature of **overdispersion**, indicating the Poisson model is inadequate and a **Negative Binomial** or **Quasi-Poisson** model is required. This diagnostic check is a routine KTU viva question.

## 2.3 The Poisson Approximation to the Binomial

When $n$ is large and $p$ is small (typically $n \ge 20$ and $p \le 0.05$, with $np = \lambda$ moderate), the Binomial $B(n,p)$ is well-approximated by a Poisson with parameter $\lambda = np$:

$$B(n, p) \approx P(\lambda = np)$$

**Practical Rule of Thumb (KTU-Standard):** Use Poisson approximation when $n \ge 50$ AND $p \le 0.1$, with $np < 10$ for accuracy.

## 2.4 Additivity (Reproducibility) Property

If $X_1 \sim P(\lambda_1)$ and $X_2 \sim P(\lambda_2)$ are **independent** Poisson random variables, then their sum is also Poisson:

$$X_1 + X_2 \sim P(\lambda_1 + \lambda_2)$$

This generalizes to $n$ independent Poisson variables: $\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i \sim P\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i\right)$.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Property | Mathematical Expression | Engineering / Biostatistics Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Probability Mass Function (PMF)** | $P(X=k) = \dfrac{e^{-\lambda}\lambda^k}{k!}$ | Probability of exactly $k$ rare events |
| **Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF)** | $F(k;\lambda) = e^{-\lambda} \sum\limits_{i=0}^{k} \dfrac{\lambda^i}{i!}$ | Probability of at most $k$ events |
| **Mean (Expectation)** | $E(X) = \lambda$ | Average event count per interval |
| **Variance** | $\text{Var}(X) = \lambda$ | Dispersion check for overdispersion |
| **Standard Deviation** | $\sigma = \sqrt{\lambda}$ | Used in confidence interval construction |
| **Moment Generating Function (MGF)** | $M_X(t) = e^{\lambda(e^t - 1)}$ | Generating higher moments |
| **Cumulant Generating Function (CGF)** | $K_X(t) = \lambda(e^t - 1)$ | All cumulants equal $\lambda$ |
| **Skewness** | $\gamma_1 = \lambda^{-1/2}$ | Right-skewness vanishes as $\lambda$ grows |
| **Poisson from Binomial Limit** | $B(n,p) \to P(\lambda)$ as $n \to \infty$, $p \to 0$, $np = \lambda$ | Approximation of rare-event Binomial |
| **Additivity of Independent Poissons** | $X_1 + X_2 \sim P(\lambda_1 + \lambda_2)$ | Combining independent count streams |
| **Poisson Process Rate (Time $t$)** | $P(X(t)=k) = \dfrac{e^{-\lambda t}(\lambda t)^k}{k!}$ | Time-scaling for varying intervals |
| **Recurrence Relation** | $\dfrac{P(X=k+1)}{P(X=k)} = \dfrac{\lambda}{k+1}$ | Fast recursive computation of PMF |
| **Maximum Likelihood Estimate (MLE)** | $\hat{\lambda} = \bar{X} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i$ | Estimating $\lambda$ from sample data |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Tip:** Whenever you see the symbol $\mid$ or $\vert$ in a KTU answer script, it denotes absolute value (e.g., $\vert k - \lambda \vert$). Avoid using the bare pipe $\vert$ in tables, as it breaks the markdown table structure. Use \vert or \mid in LaTeX form instead.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering & Biomedical Utility

- **Quality Control:** Modeling the number of defects per square meter in manufactured bio-chip surfaces.
- **Queueing Theory:** Number of customers arriving at a blood bank counter per minute.
- **Reliability Engineering:** Failures of medical devices in a hospital ward per month.
- **Spatial Statistics:** Number of trees of a given species in randomly placed quadrats in forestry.
- **Clinical Trials:** Adverse event counts per patient per treatment arm.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mean of the Poisson Distribution $E(X) = \lambda$

**Starting Point:** The definition of expectation for a discrete random variable is

$$E(X) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} k \cdot P(X = k)$$

**Step 1 — Substitute the Poisson PMF:**

$$E(X) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} k \cdot \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^{k}}{k!}$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the $k = 0$ term:** When $k = 0$, the factor $k$ itself is zero, so the entire $k=0$ term is $0$. We may start the sum from $k = 1$:

$$E(X) = \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} k \cdot \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^{k}}{k!}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the factorial:** Since $k! = k \cdot (k-1)!$, the factor $k$ cancels:

$$E(X) = \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^{k}}{(k-1)!}$$

**Step 4 — Factor out a single $\lambda$ to expose a known series:**

$$E(X) = \lambda \cdot e^{-\lambda} \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \frac{\lambda^{k-1}}{(k-1)!}$$

**Step 5 — Re-index the summation.** Let $j = k - 1$, so when $k = 1$, $j = 0$; when $k \to \infty$, $j \to \infty$:

$$E(X) = \lambda \cdot e^{-\lambda} \sum_{j=0}^{\infty} \frac{\lambda^{j}}{j!}$$

**Step 6 — Recognize the Taylor series for $e^{\lambda}$:**

$$\sum_{j=0}^{\infty} \frac{\lambda^{j}}{j!} = e^{\lambda}$$

**Step 7 — Final cancellation:**

$$E(X) = \lambda \cdot e^{-\lambda} \cdot e^{\lambda} = \lambda \cdot e^{0} = \lambda$$

$$\boxed{E(X) = \lambda}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** The parameter $\lambda$ literally *is* the average number of events — it is both the descriptive statistic and the population parameter.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Variance of the Poisson Distribution $\text{Var}(X) = \lambda$

**Starting Point:** $\text{Var}(X) = E(X^2) - [E(X)]^2$. We already have $E(X) = \lambda$. We need $E(X^2)$.

**Step 1 — Write $E(X^2)$:**

$$E(X^2) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} k^2 \cdot \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^{k}}{k!}$$

**Step 2 — Use the identity** $k^2 = k(k-1) + k$:

$$E(X^2) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} k(k-1) \cdot \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^{k}}{k!} + \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} k \cdot \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^{k}}{k!}$$

**Step 3 — Identify the second sum as $E(X) = \lambda$:**

$$E(X^2) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} k(k-1) \cdot \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^{k}}{k!} + \lambda$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the first sum.** For $k=0$ and $k=1$, the factor $k(k-1) = 0$, so we start at $k=2$. Also $k! = k(k-1)(k-2)!$:

$$E(X^2) = \sum_{k=2}^{\infty} \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^{k}}{(k-2)!} + \lambda$$

**Step 5 — Factor out $\lambda^2$:**

$$E(X^2) = \lambda^{2} \cdot e^{-\lambda} \sum_{k=2}^{\infty} \frac{\lambda^{k-2}}{(k-2)!} + \lambda$$

**Step 6 — Re-index** with $j = k - 2$:

$$E(X^2) = \lambda^{2} \cdot e^{-\lambda} \sum_{j=0}^{\infty} \frac{\lambda^{j}}{j!} + \lambda$$

**Step 7 — Recognize $e^{\lambda}$ series:**

$$E(X^2) = \lambda^{2} \cdot e^{-\lambda} \cdot e^{\lambda} + \lambda = \lambda^{2} + \lambda$$

**Step 8 — Apply the variance formula:**

$$\text{Var}(X) = E(X^2) - [E(X)]^2 = (\lambda^2 + \lambda) - \lambda^2$$

$$\boxed{\text{Var}(X) = \lambda}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters:** Mean equals variance in Poisson. If you compute a sample mean and a sample variance from biostatistical count data and they are far apart, the Poisson assumption is *violated*.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Poisson as a Limit of the Binomial

**Starting Point:** Binomial PMF is

$$P(X=k) = \binom{n}{k} p^{k} (1-p)^{n-k}$$

**Step 1 — Write the binomial coefficient explicitly:**

$$P(X=k) = \frac{n!}{k!(n-k)!} p^{k} (1-p)^{n-k}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $p = \lambda/n$:**

$$P(X=k) = \frac{n!}{k!(n-k)!} \left(\frac{\lambda}{n}\right)^{k} \left(1 - \frac{\lambda}{n}\right)^{n-k}$$

**Step 3 — Expand the right-hand side:**

$$P(X=k) = \frac{n!}{k!(n-k)!} \cdot \frac{\lambda^{k}}{n^{k}} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{\lambda}{n}\right)^{n} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{\lambda}{n}\right)^{-k}$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange:**

$$P(X=k) = \frac{\lambda^{k}}{k!} \cdot \frac{n!}{(n-k)! \cdot n^{k}} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{\lambda}{n}\right)^{n} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{\lambda}{n}\right)^{-k}$$

**Step 5 — Analyze each factor as $n \to \infty$:**

- **Factor A:** $\dfrac{n!}{(n-k)! \cdot n^{k}} = \dfrac{n(n-1)(n-2)\cdots(n-k+1)}{n^{k}} \to 1$
- **Factor B:** $\left(1 - \dfrac{\lambda}{n}\right)^{n} \to e^{-\lambda}$ (classic limit definition of $e$)
- **Factor C:** $\left(1 - \dfrac{\lambda}{n}\right)^{-k} \to 1$

**Step 6 — Combine the limits:**

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} P(X=k) = \frac{\lambda^{k}}{k!} \cdot 1 \cdot e^{-\lambda} \cdot 1$$

$$\boxed{\lim_{n \to \infty} P(X=k) = \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^{k}}{k!}}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Moment Generating Function $M_X(t) = e^{\lambda(e^t - 1)}$

**Starting Point:** $M_X(t) = E(e^{tX}) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} e^{tk} \cdot \dfrac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^{k}}{k!}$

**Step 1 — Combine the exponentials and powers:**

$$M_X(t) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{e^{-\lambda} (\lambda e^{t})^{k}}{k!}$$

**Step 2 — Factor out $e^{-\lambda}$:**

$$M_X(t) = e^{-\lambda} \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{(\lambda e^{t})^{k}}{k!}$$

**Step 3 — Recognize the Taylor series** $e^{u} = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \dfrac{u^{k}}{k!}$ with $u = \lambda e^{t}$:

$$M_X(t) = e^{-\lambda} \cdot e^{\lambda e^{t}} = e^{\lambda e^{t} - \lambda}$$

**Step 4 — Final compact form:**

$$\boxed{M_X(t) = e^{\lambda(e^{t} - 1)}}$$

**Step 5 — Verification of $E(X)$ from MGF** (using $E(X) = M'_X(0)$):

$$\frac{d}{dt} M_X(t) = e^{\lambda(e^{t} - 1)} \cdot \lambda e^{t} \Big|_{t=0} = 1 \cdot \lambda \cdot 1 = \lambda \quad \checkmark$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Illustration

**Problem:** The number of patient arrivals at a hospital OPD follows $P(\lambda = 4)$. Find the probability of exactly 6 arrivals, the cumulative probability of at most 2, and the MLE from a sample.

**Solution Path:**

**Part (i) — Probability of exactly 6 arrivals:**

$$P(X=6) = \frac{e^{-4} \cdot 4^{6}}{6!} = \frac{(0.01832)(4096)}{720} = \frac{75.03}{720} \approx 0.1042$$

**Part (ii) — Probability of at most 2 arrivals:**

$$P(X \le 2) = \sum_{k=0}^{2} \frac{e^{-4} \cdot 4^{k}}{k!} = e^{-4}\left(1 + 4 + 8\right) = 0.01832 \times 13 \approx 0.2381$$

**Part (iii) — Mean, Variance, Standard Deviation:**

$$E(X) = 4, \quad \text{Var}(X) = 4, \quad \sigma = \sqrt{4} = 2$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
